2. Seal the sterilized syringe in a box or tin to ensure it is not accessible to others.
3. **Dispose of the...
Procedure for Safe Disposal of Used Syringes:

1. Sterilize the syringe before disposal to prevent the spread of infections.
2. Seal the sterilized syringe in a box or tin to ensure it is not accessible to others.
3. Dispose of the sealed container at the garbage dump following local waste management guidelines.

Important Notes:
• Used syringes can be dangerous if handled improperly, especially for individuals who may reuse them.
• Always prioritize safe disposal methods over sending syringes directly to a garbage dump without proper containment.
syringe disposal, sterilization, waste management, infection control
Safe Waste Disposal of Placenta
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Handle the placenta with clean hands and gloves to prevent contamination.
2. Place the placenta in a sealed, disposable container or bag.
3. Dispose of the placenta safely, following local regulations for medical waste...
Procedure:

1. Handle the placenta with clean hands and gloves to prevent contamination.
2. Place the placenta in a sealed, disposable container or bag.
3. Dispose of the placenta safely, following local regulations for medical waste disposal.
4. Avoid contact with the placenta after disposal to maintain hygiene.

Notes:
• Safe disposal of the placenta is crucial to prevent infections and ensure proper sanitation.
• This procedure should be followed in all settings, whether at home or in a healthcare facility.
placenta disposal, sanitation, infection control, midwifery
Types of Waste in the Field
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Definition and categories of waste:

Waste includes all refuse generated from human or animal activities. The following types are identified:

1. Human Wastes: Feces and urine.
2. Liquid Wastes: Bathing water, kitchen liquids (e.g.,...
Definition and categories of waste:

Waste includes all refuse generated from human or animal activities. The following types are identified:

1. Human Wastes: Feces and urine.
2. Liquid Wastes: Bathing water, kitchen liquids (e.g., dishwater).
3. Garbage: Organic waste such as food peelings, slicings, and other semi-solid or solid material from food service operations.
4. Rubbish: Non-organic refuse including boxes, cans, paper, and plastics.

Importance of classification:
• Proper categorization ensures appropriate disposal methods are used for each type of waste.
• Incorrect handling can lead to environmental contamination or disease outbreaks.
types of waste, sanitation, classification, field waste
Use Plastic for Collecting and Disposing of Human Waste
Standard Source: Sanitation Basics
Procedure:

1. Have all occupants urinate in the bucket and defecate into a piece of plastic.
2. Urine can be safely dumped outside as it contains few harmful organisms.
3. Use two thicknesses of thin plastic, such as that used to...
Procedure:

1. Have all occupants urinate in the bucket and defecate into a piece of plastic.
2. Urine can be safely dumped outside as it contains few harmful organisms.
3. Use two thicknesses of thin plastic, such as that used to cover freshly drycleaned clothes, to hold bowel movements of several persons.
4. Gather the plastic around the excrement to form a bag-like container.
5. Tie the plastic closed near its upper edges with a string or narrow strip of cloth.
6. Do not tie it so tightly as to be gas-tight.
7. Each day's collection should be gently tossed outside.
8. As the excrement rots, the gas will leak out through the tied end of the plastic covering.
9. Flies will be attracted in swarms but will not be able to get into the plastic to contaminate their feet or lay eggs.
plastic waste bag,waste collection,urine disposal,bowel movement containment,expedient sanitation
Acquire and Store Trash Bags for Waste Disposal
Standard Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Title: Acquire and Store Trash Bags for Waste Disposal

Procedure:
1. Purchase one box of 13-gallon trash bags and one box of 33-gallon trash bags.
2. Ensure the quantity is sufficient for at least 30 days of use.
3. Store them in a dry,...
Title: Acquire and Store Trash Bags for Waste Disposal

Procedure:
1. Purchase one box of 13-gallon trash bags and one box of 33-gallon trash bags.
2. Ensure the quantity is sufficient for at least 30 days of use.
3. Store them in a dry, accessible location.
4. Use the trash bags to dispose of waste safely and efficiently during emergencies.

Notes:
• Trash bags are essential for waste disposal when traditional methods may be unavailable during emergencies.
• Ensure that they are stored safely and are ready for use at any time.
trash bags, waste disposal, sanitation, preparedness
Acquire and Store a Burn Barrel for Waste Disposal
Standard Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Title: Acquire and Store a Burn Barrel for Waste Disposal

Procedure:
1. Purchase two burn barrels.
2. Ensure the quantity is sufficient for at least 90 days of use.
3. Store them in a safe location away from flammable materials.
4. Use the...
Title: Acquire and Store a Burn Barrel for Waste Disposal

Procedure:
1. Purchase two burn barrels.
2. Ensure the quantity is sufficient for at least 90 days of use.
3. Store them in a safe location away from flammable materials.
4. Use the burn barrel to dispose of household garbage safely and efficiently.
5. Install a spark arrestor on top of the barrel to prevent accidental fires.

Notes:
• Burn barrels are useful for waste disposal when traditional methods may be unavailable during emergencies.
• Ensure that the area around the burn barrel is clear and well-ventilated.
burn barrel, waste disposal, sanitation, preparedness
Acquire and Store a Spark Arrestor for Burn Barrel Safety
Standard Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Title: Acquire and Store a Spark Arrestor for Burn Barrel Safety

Procedure:
1. Purchase two spark arrestors.
2. Ensure the quantity is sufficient for at least 30 days of use.
3. Install the spark arrestor on top of the burn barrel to...
Title: Acquire and Store a Spark Arrestor for Burn Barrel Safety

Procedure:
1. Purchase two spark arrestors.
2. Ensure the quantity is sufficient for at least 30 days of use.
3. Install the spark arrestor on top of the burn barrel to prevent sparks from escaping.
4. Regularly inspect and maintain the spark arrestor to ensure it functions properly.

Notes:
• Spark arrestors are essential for preventing accidental fires when using a burn barrel.
• Ensure that the spark arrestor is securely attached and in good condition.
spark arrestor, burn barrel safety, sanitation, preparedness
Construct and Maintain Soakage Pits for Waste Disposal
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Procedure for Constructing and Maintaining Soakage Pits

1. Purpose: Soakage pits are used to dispose of wastewater from the food service area, ensuring proper drainage.
2. Construction considerations:
- Ensure proper separation of...
Procedure for Constructing and Maintaining Soakage Pits

1. Purpose: Soakage pits are used to dispose of wastewater from the food service area, ensuring proper drainage.
2. Construction considerations:
- Ensure proper separation of grease and scrap food particles from wastewater before disposal into soakage pits.
- Grease must be contained and not allowed to enter drainage ditches, evaporating beds, leaching fields, or soakage pits as it can clog soil, attract vermin, breed flies, and cause offensive odors.
3. Determine pit size:
- Consider the following factors when determining the required soakage pit volume:
- Duration of operation
- Number of personnel involved
- Amount of drainage generated per day
- Expected period of use
- Absorbent quality of the soil substrate
4. Refer to diagrams: Diagrams for soakage pits, trenches, and grease traps are found in article 9-27 of this chapter.

Important Notes:
• Soakage pits must be constructed with proper engineering and maintained regularly to ensure functionality.
• Always follow local regulations and environmental guidelines when constructing and using soakage pits.
soakage pit, waste disposal, grease separation, drainage system, sanitation
Construct and Use an Inclined Plane Incinerator for Rubbish Disposal
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Purpose: To incinerate large amounts of rubbish in temporary or extended camps.

### Materials Required:
• Sheet metal plane
• Three telescoped 55-gallon drums (with ends removed)
• Grate (made from scrap metal)
• Wood or oil fuel for fire
-...
Purpose: To incinerate large amounts of rubbish in temporary or extended camps.

### Materials Required:
• Sheet metal plane
• Three telescoped 55-gallon drums (with ends removed)
• Grate (made from scrap metal)
• Wood or oil fuel for fire
• Drained garbage

### Procedure:
1. Construct the Inclined Plane Incinerator:
- Insert a sheet metal plane through three telescoped 55-gallon drums, with both ends removed.
- Lay the drums on an incline.
- Extend the sheet metal plane about 2 feet beyond the upper end of the inclined drums to serve as a loading or stoking platform.
- Place a grate at the lower end of the inclined drum/plane mechanism.
2. Set Up and Use the Incinerator:
- Provide a wood or oil-fed fire under the grate until it becomes hot.
- Add drained garbage to the stoking platform in small amounts.
- As the garbage dries, push it down the inclined plane in small quantities and allow it to burn on the grate.

### Notes:
• The incinerator is effective for burning wet garbage where burial is not possible.
• It should be located at least 50 yards from the camp area and away from flammable materials.
• Only dry, solid waste should be burned. Wet materials may disrupt the air draft and reduce efficiency.
inclined plane incinerator,rubbish disposal,waste management,camp sanitation,garbage burning
Constructing a Soakage Pit for Field Messes
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Purpose: To construct a soakage pit for the disposal of liquid wastes in field messes.

Materials Required:
• Shovel
• Suitable contact medium (rocks, flattened tin cans, rubble, bricks, broken bottles)
• Small gravel or crushed stones...
Purpose: To construct a soakage pit for the disposal of liquid wastes in field messes.

Materials Required:
• Shovel
• Suitable contact medium (rocks, flattened tin cans, rubble, bricks, broken bottles)
• Small gravel or crushed stones (optional)
• Ventilating shafts made of scrap materials (4 to 6 inches square) (optional)
• Screen, straw, or grass for covering ventilating shafts

Steps:
1. Dig a pit that is 4 feet square and 4 feet deep.
2. Fill the pit with a suitable contact medium such as rocks, flattened tin cans, rubble, bricks, broken bottles, or any other material that allows liquid to seep into the ground.
3. Optionally, place a layer of small gravel or crushed stones on top of the contact medium.
4. If ventilating shafts are used:
- Construct them from scrap materials measuring 4 to 6 inches square.
- Ensure they extend 6 to 12 inches above the surface and reach within 6 inches of the bottom of the pit.
- Drill numerous holes in the sides of the underground sections of the shafts.
- Cover the tops with screen, straw, or grass.
5. Mark the location clearly as a soakage pit for future reference.

Notes:
• One soakage pit will service a field mess serving 200 people or less.
• If the mess is to remain operational for 2 weeks or more, construct and use two pits on alternating days.
• Each washing or drinking device should have its own soakage pit under it.
• Pits are also recommended under water trailers.
• When a soakage pit becomes clogged, close it by covering the opening with soil and mark it as: 'SOAKAGE PIT CLOSED (date)'.
• Soakage pits may vary in size depending on the volume of liquid waste they need to handle.
soakage pit, waste disposal, sanitation, field messes, liquid waste
Constructing an Evaporation Bed for Field Messes
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Purpose: To construct an evaporation bed in locations where soakage pits and grease traps are impractical.

Materials Required:
• Shovel
• Topsoil (for forming a dike)

Steps:
1. Mark off an 8 foot by 10 foot rectangle for the...
Purpose: To construct an evaporation bed in locations where soakage pits and grease traps are impractical.

Materials Required:
• Shovel
• Topsoil (for forming a dike)

Steps:
1. Mark off an 8 foot by 10 foot rectangle for the evaporation bed.
2. Remove the top soil and use it to form a dike around the perimeter of the bed.
3. Spade the area to a depth of 10 to 15 inches, then rake it into a series of ridges and depressions:
- The ridges should be about 6 inches higher than the depressions.
4. In operation, flood one bed with liquid wastes up to the top of the ridges.

Notes:
• Evaporation beds are recommended for short-term use in hot, dry climates, where soakage pits cannot be dug or where the soil is too hard (frozen or rocky) to absorb moisture.
• Each evaporation bed should provide 3 square feet of evaporation area per person per day for field mess wastes and 2 square feet per person per day for bathing wastes.
• Beds should be spaced so that waste can be distributed evenly among them.
evaporation bed, waste disposal, sanitation, field messes, hot climates, dry climates
Constructing and Using a Cat Hole for Sanitation
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Procedure:

1. Select a location for the cat hole that is at least 50 feet away from water sources, food preparation areas, and sleeping quarters.
2. Dig a hole with a diameter of 8 to 12 inches and a depth of 6 to 12 inches.
3....
Procedure:

1. Select a location for the cat hole that is at least 50 feet away from water sources, food preparation areas, and sleeping quarters.
2. Dig a hole with a diameter of 8 to 12 inches and a depth of 6 to 12 inches.
3. Remove the soil from the hole and set it aside for later use.
4. Deposit feces into the hole immediately after defecation.
5. Cover the feces with tightly compacted earth that was originally removed from the hole.
6. Compact the soil thoroughly to prevent odor and contamination.
7. Repeat steps 2 through 6 for each use, ensuring that a new cat hole is dug at least 10 feet away from previous holes to avoid cross-contamination.

Important Notes:
• The cat hole should be used only in situations where other latrines are not available (e.g., during marches or short halts).
• Ensure the area around the cat hole is kept clean and free of debris.
• After use, avoid walking near or disturbing the cat hole to prevent spreading pathogens.
cat hole,waste disposal,field sanitation,latrine,sanitation procedures
Create a Safe Needle Disposal Box
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Materials needed: A metal or hard plastic box.

Steps:
1. Find a suitable container: Choose a sturdy box made of metal or hard plastic.
2. Cut the lid: Make a long hole in the lid that is wide on one side and gets narrower on the...
Materials needed: A metal or hard plastic box.

Steps:
1. Find a suitable container: Choose a sturdy box made of metal or hard plastic.
2. Cut the lid: Make a long hole in the lid that is wide on one side and gets narrower on the other side.
3. Use the box for disposal: When you have finished using a disposable syringe, insert the needle into the box and slide it down to the narrowest point.
4. Remove the needle safely: Pull up on the syringe so that the needle falls off into the box.
5. Dispose of the syringe: The plastic syringe can be sterilized and thrown into a waste pit (see below for details on waste disposal).

Notes:
• Ensure the hole is cut correctly to allow safe disposal of needles without risk of injury.
• Always use gloves when handling used needles.
needle disposal, safe waste handling, medical waste, sanitation, syringe disposal
Dig a Waste-Disposal Pit for Long-Term Shelter Occupancy
Standard Source: Sanitation Basics
Procedure:

1. If staying in a shelter for weeks and no sufficient waste containers are available, dig a waste-disposal pit.
2. Locate the pit about 3 feet from the shelter, in the down-wind direction (usually the air exhaust end...
Procedure:

1. If staying in a shelter for weeks and no sufficient waste containers are available, dig a waste-disposal pit.
2. Locate the pit about 3 feet from the shelter, in the down-wind direction (usually the air exhaust end of an earth-covered shelter).
3. Surround the pit with a ring of mounded, packed earth that is 6 inches high to prevent surface water from heavy rains from running into it.
4. Once fallout is no longer being deposited, quickly putting or dumping wastes outside is not hazardous.
waste pit,digging waste pit,pit sanitation,shelter waste management,long-term shelter
Disposal of Trash and Rubbish in Emergency Situations
Standard Source: TACDA Emergency Food And Water
Procedure:

1. Trash and rubbish may be burned in open yard areas if local regulations allow.
2. Dispose of trash at dumps established by local authorities when possible.
3. To reduce bulk, flatten cans and break bottles before...
Procedure:

1. Trash and rubbish may be burned in open yard areas if local regulations allow.
2. Dispose of trash at dumps established by local authorities when possible.
3. To reduce bulk, flatten cans and break bottles before disposal.
trash disposal, waste reduction, emergency sanitation, recycling, bulk reduction
Dispose of Body Wastes Safely
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Identify body wastes: These include blood, stool, and the placenta.
2. Use appropriate disposal methods:
- Bury body wastes deep in the ground.
- In many communities, it is customary to bury the placenta with other...
Procedure:

1. Identify body wastes: These include blood, stool, and the placenta.
2. Use appropriate disposal methods:
- Bury body wastes deep in the ground.
- In many communities, it is customary to bury the placenta with other special objects as part of a ritual.
3. Protect yourself: Always wear gloves when handling body wastes to prevent infection.
4. Consider cultural practices: Burying the placenta may have spiritual or communal significance and should be done respectfully.
body wastes, placenta burial, waste disposal, gloves, infection prevention
Dispose of Trash Properly
Standard Source: US Antarctic Continental Field Manual
Procedure:
1. Separate all trash into appropriate categories.
2. Clean the trash before disposal.
3. Dispose of each category in the designated bins outside the BFC.
4. Bag each category of trash separately.
5. Use extra bags available in the...
Procedure:
1. Separate all trash into appropriate categories.
2. Clean the trash before disposal.
3. Dispose of each category in the designated bins outside the BFC.
4. Bag each category of trash separately.
5. Use extra bags available in the BFC bay if needed.
trash disposal,BFC,waste management
Dispose of Waste Without Water
Standard Source: Sweden In Case of Crisis or War
Procedure for managing waste when there is no water:

1. Prepare alternative waste disposal methods if there is a power cut or lack of water, as you will not be able to flush your toilet.
2. Urinate in the toilet even if it cannot be...
Procedure for managing waste when there is no water:

1. Prepare alternative waste disposal methods if there is a power cut or lack of water, as you will not be able to flush your toilet.
2. Urinate in the toilet even if it cannot be flushed.
3. Discard toilet paper in a garbage can or bucket with a lid.
4. Dispose of excrement using one of these methods:
- Fit a plastic bag or garbage bag into your toilet bowl.
- Use a portable toilet or a bucket with a lid.
5. Cover the excrement with compost litter or sawdust to reduce odor and maintain hygiene.
6. Dispose of waste properly by following local guidelines from your municipality.

Items to keep at home for this procedure:
• Toilet paper
• Plastic bags or garbage bags
• Wet wipes, hand sanitizer
• Compost litter or sawdust
• Diapers, menstrual pads
• Bucket with a lid

Additional items to keep at home:
• Camping stove, gas burner, fuel
• Torch, headlamp
• Home pharmacy and first aid kit
• Tin opener
• Matches, fire striker
• Fuel in the car tank or a charged battery
• A month’s supply of prescription medicines or disposable products (e.g., for diabetes)
toilet,waste disposal,no water,hygiene,excrement,urine,bucket,sawdust,plastic bag
Get Rid of Wastes Safely
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure:

1. Identify the type of waste:
- Infectious waste (e.g., blood, bodily fluids)
- Sharps (needles, broken glass)
- Chemical waste (drugs, solvents)
- General waste (paper, packaging)

2. Segregate wastes:
- Use...
Procedure:

1. Identify the type of waste:
- Infectious waste (e.g., blood, bodily fluids)
- Sharps (needles, broken glass)
- Chemical waste (drugs, solvents)
- General waste (paper, packaging)

2. Segregate wastes:
- Use separate containers for each type of waste.
- Label all containers clearly with the type of waste they contain.

3. Handle infectious waste:
- Place in a leak-proof, puncture-resistant container.
- Seal tightly and dispose of according to local regulations.

4. Dispose of sharps:
- Use a sharps container designed for safe disposal.
- Do not recap needles; place directly into the container.

5. Handle chemical waste:
- Store in a secure, well-ventilated area away from heat and ignition sources.
- Dispose of according to local regulations for hazardous materials.

6. Dispose of general waste:
- Place in regular trash bins.
- Ensure bins are covered and emptied regularly.

7. Follow local guidelines:
- Adhere to all local, national, or international regulations for waste disposal.
- Consult with local health authorities if unsure about proper procedures.
waste disposal, sanitation, infection control, sharps, infectious waste
Incineration of Rubbish in Extended Camps
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Purpose: To dispose of rubbish by incineration in camps expected to last more than one week.

### Materials Required:
• Incinerator (barrel or inclined plane type)
• Drained garbage
• Soakage pit
• Shovel or digging tool

### Procedure:
1....
Purpose: To dispose of rubbish by incineration in camps expected to last more than one week.

### Materials Required:
• Incinerator (barrel or inclined plane type)
• Drained garbage
• Soakage pit
• Shovel or digging tool

### Procedure:
1. Separate Liquids from Solids:
- Strain the garbage through a coarse strainer, such as an old bucket or 55-gallon drum with holes punched in the bottom.
2. Process Liquid Waste:
- Run liquid waste through a grease trap and into a soakage pit.
3. Incinerate Solid Waste:
- Use either a barrel incinerator or an inclined plane incinerator to burn the solid waste.
4. Dispose of Ash and Noncombustibles:
- After burning, bury the ash and noncombustible materials with the garbage in a designated pit.

### Notes:
• Incinerators must be located at least 50 yards from the camp area and away from flammable materials.
• Only dry, solid waste should be burned. Wet materials may disrupt the air draft and reduce efficiency.
• Ensure that soakage pits are properly maintained to prevent contamination.
rubbish incineration,waste management,camp sanitation,long-term disposal,ash burial
Proper Disposal of Garbage to Prevent Odor and Pests
Standard Source: TACDA Emergency Food And Water
Procedure:

1. Separate garbage from trash to prevent odor and insect nuisances.
2. Drain liquid waste from garbage before storage to extend its shelf life without developing an unpleasant odor.
3. **Wrap garbage in double thicknesses of...
Procedure:

1. Separate garbage from trash to prevent odor and insect nuisances.
2. Drain liquid waste from garbage before storage to extend its shelf life without developing an unpleasant odor.
3. Wrap garbage in double thicknesses of newspaper, then place it in a container with a tight-fitting lid for secure storage.
4. Never dump garbage on the ground, as it will attract rats, skunks, and other scavengers.
5. If collection by authorities is not possible, bury garbage in a hole deep enough to cover it with at least 18 to 24 inches of dirt to prevent insect breeding and discourage animals from digging it up.
garbage disposal, waste management, pest control, sanitation, emergency storage
Safe Disposal of Dental Waste
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Safe Disposal of Dental Waste

Proper disposal of dental waste is essential to prevent the spread of disease and ensure safety. Follow these steps:

1. Segregate waste into categories: infectious, sharps, chemical, and general waste.
2....
Safe Disposal of Dental Waste

Proper disposal of dental waste is essential to prevent the spread of disease and ensure safety. Follow these steps:

1. Segregate waste into categories: infectious, sharps, chemical, and general waste.
2. Infectious waste (e.g., blood-soaked gauze, used swabs) should be placed in yellow or red bags labeled for biohazardous materials.
3. Sharps (needles, broken glass, scalers) must be disposed of in puncture-resistant containers with a tight-fitting lid to prevent accidental injury.
4. Chemical waste (e.g., mercury amalgam, solvents) should be stored separately and disposed of according to local regulations for hazardous materials.
5. General waste can be disposed of normally if it does not pose any risk.
6. Ensure that all containers are clearly labeled with appropriate warnings.
7. Regularly clean and disinfect waste disposal areas to prevent contamination.
8. Follow local laws and guidelines for the disposal of medical waste.
dental waste, biohazard, sharps disposal, sanitation, infection control
Sterilize and Bury Medical Waste Safely
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Materials needed: Bleach (5% solution), a waste pit.

Steps:
1. Prepare the waste: Place used medical items such as plastic gloves, syringe barrels, or cloth soaked in blood into a container.
2. Sterilize the waste: Soak the items in...
Materials needed: Bleach (5% solution), a waste pit.

Steps:
1. Prepare the waste: Place used medical items such as plastic gloves, syringe barrels, or cloth soaked in blood into a container.
2. Sterilize the waste: Soak the items in 5% bleach solution for 20 minutes to sterilize them.
3. Dispose of the waste: When the disposal box is half full, pour 5% bleach solution into it, seal it closed, and bury it deep in the ground.
4. Choose a safe location: Find a place away from where people get their drinking water and away from where children play.
5. Dig a waste pit: Create a safe waste pit to bury all sterilized wastes.

Notes:
• Never burn plastic gloves, syringes, or any other plastics, as burning them produces poisonous smoke and ash.
• Ensure the waste is buried deep enough to prevent contamination of water sources or exposure to children.
sterilize waste, bury medical waste, safe disposal, sanitation, bleach solution
Using and Maintaining Chemical Toilets for Sanitary Facilities
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Materials:
• Chemical toilet unit (usually provided by a contractor)

Usage Steps:
1. Ensure the chemical toilet is obtained through a contracted service that provides pumping and cleaning services.
2. Use the chemical toilet as per the...
Materials:
• Chemical toilet unit (usually provided by a contractor)

Usage Steps:
1. Ensure the chemical toilet is obtained through a contracted service that provides pumping and cleaning services.
2. Use the chemical toilet as per the manufacturer’s instructions, ensuring it is properly sealed after use to prevent odor or spillage.
3. Regularly check for any signs of malfunction or damage to the unit.

Maintenance Steps:
1. Monitor the contractor's performance closely to ensure that pumping and cleaning services are carried out regularly and effectively.
2. Ensure that waste is removed in a timely manner to prevent overflow or contamination.
3. Inspect the chemical toilet periodically for leaks, cracks, or other signs of wear and replace parts as needed.
4. Keep the area around the chemical toilet clean and free from debris.

Important Notes:
• Ensure that the contractor is reliable and has a good track record with sanitation services.
• If any issues arise with the unit, contact the contractor immediately for repairs or replacement.
chemical toilet, sanitation, contractor service, waste disposal
Using and Maintaining a Urinoil for Sanitary Urine Disposal
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Materials:
• Screened 55-gallon drum
• Waste oil (to be placed inside the drum)
• 3-inch diameter pipe
• 1½-inch diameter pipe
• Soakage pit
• Covered windbreak
• Fresh sand or dirt for periodic spreading around the barrel

**Installation...
Materials:
• Screened 55-gallon drum
• Waste oil (to be placed inside the drum)
• 3-inch diameter pipe
• 1½-inch diameter pipe
• Soakage pit
• Covered windbreak
• Fresh sand or dirt for periodic spreading around the barrel

Installation Steps:
1. Place a screened 55-gallon drum in an area with access to a soakage pit.
2. Add waste oil to the bottom of the drum, ensuring it forms a layer that covers the entire base.
3. Insert a 3-inch diameter pipe into the top of the drum so that urine can flow into the oil layer.
4. Connect a 1½-inch diameter pipe from the 3-inch pipe to direct overflow into the soakage pit.

Usage and Maintenance Steps:
1. Urine enters through the screen on top of the waste oil and sinks to the bottom of the drum.
2. As urine accumulates, the liquid level rises in the 3-inch diameter pipe until it overflows into the 1½-inch diameter pipe and drains into the soakage pit.
3. The oil layer acts as a barrier against odors and flies.
4. Construct a covered windbreak around the urinoil to protect it from flooding with rainwater.
5. Periodically spread fresh sand or dirt around the barrel to maintain hygiene and absorb excess moisture.
6. One urinoil serves approximately 100 men.

Important Notes:
• Ensure that the soakage pit is deep enough and not prone to clogging.
• Regularly check for any signs of leakage or damage to the drum or pipes.
urinoil, urine disposal, soakage pit, sanitation, waste oil barrier
Burial of Rubbish in Short-Term Camps
Reference Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Purpose: To dispose of rubbish when incineration is not feasible and the camp is expected to be short-term.

### Materials Required:
• Shovel or digging tool
• Pit (for burial)

### Procedure:
1. Dig a Pit:
- Dig a pit large enough to...
Purpose: To dispose of rubbish when incineration is not feasible and the camp is expected to be short-term.

### Materials Required:
• Shovel or digging tool
• Pit (for burial)

### Procedure:
1. Dig a Pit:
- Dig a pit large enough to accommodate the day’s garbage.
2. Prepare Waste for Burial:
- Flatten cans and break down boxes to reduce volume.
3. Bury Rubbish:
- Place the flattened waste into the pit and cover it with soil.
4. Repeat as Needed:
- When the first section is full, cover it and mound dirt over it.
- Dig a new section for the next day’s garbage and repeat the process indefinitely.

### Notes:
• This method is suitable only for short-term camps (less than one week).
• Ensure that pits are not located near water sources or in areas where contamination could occur.
rubbish burial,waste management,camp sanitation,temporary disposal,burial pits
Dispose of the Placenta Safely to Prevent Infection
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Purpose: To dispose of the placenta in a safe and hygienic manner to prevent infection and protect the community from germs.

1. Burying is an effective method for disposal:
- Dig a deep pit to prevent animals from digging it up.
2....
Purpose: To dispose of the placenta in a safe and hygienic manner to prevent infection and protect the community from germs.

1. Burying is an effective method for disposal:
- Dig a deep pit to prevent animals from digging it up.
2. Alternatively, burn the placenta completely to ensure all germs are destroyed.
3. Some cultures may choose to dry or use the placenta as medicine, but these methods should be done with caution and proper hygiene.
4. If you bury the placenta, make sure it is placed in a clean location away from water sources.
5. See page 67 of *A Book for Midwives* for more information on protecting the community from germs that live in blood.
placenta disposal,burying,germs,sanitation
Proper Disposal of Urine Using Burn Barrel Latrines
Reference Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Procedure:

1. Avoid using burn barrel latrines for urine disposal if alternative methods are available, as they require additional fuel to promote complete burning.
2. Mix 4 parts diesel fuel with 1 part gasoline (mogas) until the...
Procedure:

1. Avoid using burn barrel latrines for urine disposal if alternative methods are available, as they require additional fuel to promote complete burning.
2. Mix 4 parts diesel fuel with 1 part gasoline (mogas) until the contents of the barrel are sufficiently covered.
3. Ignite the mixture using a long stick or pole used to stir the mixture. Ensure that sticks or poles less than 4 feet in length are replaced for safety.
4. Continue burning until the contents of the barrels are reduced to fine ash, which may take more than one attempt.
5. Burial of ashes is acceptable if buried to a depth of at least 12 inches. Scattering ashes over ground surfaces is not recommended.
urine disposal,burn barrel,latrine,fuel mixture,sanitation,waste management
Waste Management During Quarantine
Reference Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Purpose: Ensure proper waste management during quarantine without special precautions if individuals remain asymptomatic.

Steps:
1. Follow standard waste removal procedures for all waste generated by quarantined individuals.
2. No...
Purpose: Ensure proper waste management during quarantine without special precautions if individuals remain asymptomatic.

Steps:
1. Follow standard waste removal procedures for all waste generated by quarantined individuals.
2. No additional precautions are required as long as the individual remains asymptomatic.
3. Ensure that waste is disposed of in accordance with local regulations and public health guidelines.

Notes: Waste management should be coordinated with local authorities to ensure compliance.
quarantine waste, sanitation, pandemic influenza, waste management
Proper Disposal of Food Waste and Solid Wastes
Reference Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Procedure:

1. Proper disposal of liquid and solid wastes will greatly enhance pest control operations in any given area.
2. Methods for disposal include compacting, burial, or burning.
3. In many cases, local contractor recycling may be...
Procedure:

1. Proper disposal of liquid and solid wastes will greatly enhance pest control operations in any given area.
2. Methods for disposal include compacting, burial, or burning.
3. In many cases, local contractor recycling may be used.
4. In peacetime, consult with local civilian health authorities prior to selecting a disposal method.
5. The method employed may vary from area to area depending on environmental and tactical situations.
6. Solid wastes are not to be buried or burned in close proximity to the galley or messing areas.
waste disposal, sanitation, pest control, food waste

Water Purification (25) -

Boil Water to Prevent Diarrhea
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Collect water from a safe source (e.g., rainwater, river, lake).
2. Bring the water to a rolling boil for at least one minute.
3. Allow the boiled water to cool before use.
4. Store in clean containers and keep them sealed to...
Procedure:

1. Collect water from a safe source (e.g., rainwater, river, lake).
2. Bring the water to a rolling boil for at least one minute.
3. Allow the boiled water to cool before use.
4. Store in clean containers and keep them sealed to prevent contamination.

Note: Boiling is an effective way to kill pathogens that cause diarrheal diseases.
boil water, prevent diarrhea, water safety
Pasteurize Water Using a Solar Cooker and WAPI
High Priority Source: Solar Cookers
How to pasteurize water using a solar cooker and the Water Pasteurization Indicator (WAPI):

1. Pour water into a black pot or jar used for cooking.
2. Insert the WAPI: Place the WAPI with the washer end down and wax end up into the...
How to pasteurize water using a solar cooker and the Water Pasteurization Indicator (WAPI):

1. Pour water into a black pot or jar used for cooking.
2. Insert the WAPI: Place the WAPI with the washer end down and wax end up into the water. Ensure that the washer touches the bottom of the container, and the wax is higher. Carefully replace the lid.
3. Orient the cooker accordingly, following the specific instructions for your type of solar cooker.
4. Place the pot or jar in the cooker.
5. Allow water to heat: Leave the water in a sunny location for at least 1 hour per liter (quart) of water.
6. Determine if pasteurization has occurred: When the wax on the WAPI melts and falls to the bottom, the water is safely pasteurized. If you don’t have a WAPI, measure the temperature or observe boiling as an alternative method.
7. Repeat steps 5 and 6 until pasteurization has been confirmed.
8. Allow the water to cool before drinking: Once pasteurized, let the water sit until it reaches a safe temperature for consumption.

Important Notes:
• Keep pasteurized water covered until use to prevent recontamination.
• Avoid letting fingers or unclean objects touch clean water.
• If unsure about the safety of the water, re-pasteurize it.
• Pasteurization does not remove dangerous chemicals, such as arsenic. It is also not the same as sterilization (which kills all microorganisms, including heat-resistant spores).
pasteurize water, solar cooker, WAPI, water purification, safe drinking water, disease-causing organisms
Purify Water Using Bleach Solution
High Priority Source: All Hazard Preparedness Workbook
Procedure:

1. Wash containers with soapy water.
2. Fill the containers with a 10 per cent bleach solution.
3. Let the solution sit for five minutes, then empty and air dry the container.
4. Strain any sediment or particles from the water you...
Procedure:

1. Wash containers with soapy water.
2. Fill the containers with a 10 per cent bleach solution.
3. Let the solution sit for five minutes, then empty and air dry the container.
4. Strain any sediment or particles from the water you are purifying by pouring it through several layers of paper towels, cheesecloth, or coffee filters.
5. Use one of the following purification methods:
- Boil the water for seven to 10 minutes.
- Add liquid household bleach as follows:
- For 1 litre (1 quart) of clear water: add 2 drops of bleach.
- For 4 litres (1 gallon) of clear water: add 8 drops of bleach.
- For 20 litres (21 quarts) of clear water: add 2.5 ml (½ teaspoon) of bleach.
- For 1 litre (1 quart) of cloudy water: add 4 drops of bleach.
- For 4 litres (1 gallon) of cloudy water: add 16 drops of bleach.
- For 20 litres (21 quarts) of cloudy water: add 5 ml (1 teaspoon) of bleach.
6. Shake the container to mix the bleach and water thoroughly.
7. Let the water mixture stand for 30 minutes before using.
8. Check for a faint chlorine scent after treating. If there is no scent, add the same amount of bleach again and let it stand for another 15 minutes.

Important Notes:
• Use only household liquid bleach that contains 5.25 per cent sodium hypochlorite.
• Do not use scented bleaches, coloursafe bleaches, or bleaches with added cleaners.
• Do not use granular forms of bleach; they are poisonous.
bleach,water purification,sanitation,emergency water
Purify Water Using Tablets
High Priority Source: All Hazard Preparedness Workbook
Procedure:

1. Use water purification tablets, which are available at drug stores.
2. Follow all package instructions carefully for proper usage and dosage.
3. Ensure the water is treated as per the manufacturer’s guidelines before...
Procedure:

1. Use water purification tablets, which are available at drug stores.
2. Follow all package instructions carefully for proper usage and dosage.
3. Ensure the water is treated as per the manufacturer’s guidelines before consumption.

Note: If no drinking water is available, liquids may be obtained from canned fruits, vegetables, and soft drinks.
water purification tablets,sanitation,emergency water
Safety Precautions for Chlorine Production and Handling
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
Materials: Protective gloves, well-ventilated area, non-metallic containers

1. Do not inhale chlorine concentrate over a long period of time.
2. Work in a well-ventilated area to avoid inhalation risks.
3. **Never use metallic...
Materials: Protective gloves, well-ventilated area, non-metallic containers

1. Do not inhale chlorine concentrate over a long period of time.
2. Work in a well-ventilated area to avoid inhalation risks.
3. Never use metallic containers during the production or handling of concentrated chlorine solutions.
4. Avoid drinking the concentrated solution, as it is not toxic but has an extremely unpleasant taste.
5. Do not spill concentrated chlorine on clothing, as it acts as a bleach and can cause damage.

Important Notes:
• These precautions are essential to ensure safe handling of chlorine during production and use.
• Always store chlorine in secure, non-metallic containers away from children and direct sunlight.
chlorine safety, handling precautions, ventilation, metallic container avoidance, storage guidelines
Use Water Purification Methods in Emergencies
High Priority Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Objective: Ensure access to clean drinking water when standard filtration systems fail.

### Steps:
1. Carry Backup Water Purification Options:
- Include Potable Aqua Water Purification Tablets (50) as a backup method for purifying...
Objective: Ensure access to clean drinking water when standard filtration systems fail.

### Steps:
1. Carry Backup Water Purification Options:
- Include Potable Aqua Water Purification Tablets (50) as a backup method for purifying water.
2. Use Bleach for Disinfection:
- Add two drops of 8.25% unscented chlorine bleach per liter of water to disinfect it.
3. Utilize Potassium Permanganate:
- Add a few crystals of potassium permanganate to make the water slightly pink. If the water turns purple, you have added too much.
4. Use Sawyer Mini Water Filter Syringe:
- The Sawyer Mini Water Filter comes with a syringe that can be used for:
- Flushing out debris from the filter.
- Serving as an irrigation syringe for wounds in emergency situations.
water purification, bleach, potassium permanganate, Sawyer filter, emergency water
Boil Water for Safe Use During Births
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
Boiling water is essential to ensure it is safe for use during births.

1. If the water in your community may carry germs, boil the water before using it.
2. Boiling should be done until the water reaches a rolling boil and continues for at...
Boiling water is essential to ensure it is safe for use during births.

1. If the water in your community may carry germs, boil the water before using it.
2. Boiling should be done until the water reaches a rolling boil and continues for at least 1 minute (or longer at higher altitudes).
3. Use the boiled water for washing hands or cleaning tools before a birth.
4. Allow the water to cool before use if necessary.

Note: This is especially important in areas where the water supply may be contaminated with germs that can cause infections.
boil, water, purification, sanitation, infection prevention
Chlorination Process for Water Purification
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
Materials: Chlorine (in tablet, powder granule, or liquid form), clear water, timing device

1. Ensure the water is clear before beginning the chlorination process.
2. Add chlorine to the water according to calculated dosage...
Materials: Chlorine (in tablet, powder granule, or liquid form), clear water, timing device

1. Ensure the water is clear before beginning the chlorination process.
2. Add chlorine to the water according to calculated dosage requirements.
3. Allow 30 minutes for the chlorine to react with germs in the water.
4. Check residual chlorine levels using a WataTest or similar tool after treatment to confirm that the water is protected from recontamination during storage.

Important Notes:
• Chlorine kills 99% of pathogens, including viruses, bacteria, and parasites.
• Residual chlorine ensures continued protection against contamination during storage.
• This method is rapid (30 minutes) and effective for large volumes of water.
chlorination process, water purification, residual chlorine, WataTest, pathogen elimination
Disinfect Water Based on Test Results
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
- If the test with raw water indicates unsafe water, disinfect the water.

• If the test with raw water indicates safe water, carry out another test at a later date or with another water source.

• If the SODIS-treated water or chlorinated...
- If the test with raw water indicates unsafe water, disinfect the water.

• If the test with raw water indicates safe water, carry out another test at a later date or with another water source.

• If the SODIS-treated water or chlorinated water indicates unsafe water, repeat the test. Make sure that:
- The water has been disinfected properly
- The water quality test was conducted correctly
disinfect,water,test,unsafe,safe,SODIS,chlorinated
Disinfect Water Using Bleach
High Priority Source: Sanitation Basics
Procedure for Disinfecting Water Using Bleach:

1. For initial storage of water, add 1 scant teaspoon of ordinary household bleach per 10 gallons of water.
2. Ensure the container is sealed after adding the bleach to prevent...
Procedure for Disinfecting Water Using Bleach:

1. For initial storage of water, add 1 scant teaspoon of ordinary household bleach per 10 gallons of water.
2. Ensure the container is sealed after adding the bleach to prevent contamination.
3. When removing small quantities from a large container (e.g., a waterproof bag), follow these steps:
- First pour some water into a pot or medium-sized container.
- From this intermediate container, pour small amounts into individual cups.
4. Avoid dipping directly with a cup to reduce contamination risk.
5. The cleanest method for removing small quantities of water is by using a flexible tube to siphon the water out.
disinfect,water,bleach,sanitation,storage
Ensure Clean Drinking Water Access
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Action Steps:
1. Ensure clean drinking water is available and uncontaminated by sewage and wastewater.
2. If the purity of the water is uncertain, boil it or filter it before consumption.
3. Regularly check that **drinking water...
Action Steps:
1. Ensure clean drinking water is available and uncontaminated by sewage and wastewater.
2. If the purity of the water is uncertain, boil it or filter it before consumption.
3. Regularly check that drinking water catchments are not contaminated by improper waste disposal.
clean water,boiling,filtration,sanitation
Prepare Saturated Brine Solution for Mini-WATA Chlorine Production
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
Procedure:

1. Fill a container with clear water: Use any non-metallic container of suitable size.
2. Add salt to the water: Add approximately 400g of salt per litre of water.
3. Mix thoroughly: Shake or mix the solution for at least...
Procedure:

1. Fill a container with clear water: Use any non-metallic container of suitable size.
2. Add salt to the water: Add approximately 400g of salt per litre of water.
3. Mix thoroughly: Shake or mix the solution for at least 30 minutes to dissolve as much salt as possible.
4. Check for remaining salt: Ensure there is visible excess salt at the bottom of the container. If not, add more salt and repeat from step 2.
5. Label and store: Store the saturated brine in a clearly labelled, clean, opaque, tightly-closed, non-metallic container. Keep it away from light and children.

Important Notes:
• Never use metallic containers for this procedure.
• Ensure the solution is properly sealed to prevent contamination or evaporation.
saturated brine, mini-wata, chlorine production, water purification, salt solution
Produce Chlorine Concentrate Using Mini-WATA with Solar Power Supply
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
Procedure:

1. Prepare the brine solution: Follow the procedure for preparing saturated brine as outlined above.
2. Set up the Mini-WATA device: Ensure the device is plugged into a direct current power source (6-18V) from a solar panel...
Procedure:

1. Prepare the brine solution: Follow the procedure for preparing saturated brine as outlined above.
2. Set up the Mini-WATA device: Ensure the device is plugged into a direct current power source (6-18V) from a solar panel with a minimum of 10W output.
3. Fill the bottle: Fill a standard 0.5L plastic bottle with the saturated brine solution until it is full.
4. Insert Mini-WATA device: Insert the Mini-WATA unit into the neck of the 0.5L bottle and secure it properly.
5. Electrolysis process: Connect the clips to the solar power supply (to the “+” terminal of the panel). Bubbles should immediately form in the bottle, indicating the start of the electrolysis process.
6. Wait for completion: Allow the device to run for 5 hours under direct sunlight to produce approximately 0.5L of concentrated chlorine solution (6g/L or 6000 ppm).
7. Remove and clean the device: After the process is complete, unplug the device from the power source. Remove it from the water, rinse it thoroughly with clear water, and store it.
8. Store the concentrate: Transfer the concentrated chlorine solution to a clearly labelled, opaque, tightly-closed, non-metallic container. Store it in a dark place away from light and children.

Important Notes:
• The device must only be used by a responsible person who has read the user guide thoroughly.
• In case of accidental contact with the solution, rinse well with water and avoid inhalation.
• Never use metallic containers for storage or during the procedure.
mini-wata, solar power, chlorine production, water purification, electrolysis
Purify Water Using Solar Disinfection
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Purpose: To purify water using sunlight to kill germs.

Procedure:
1. Use a clean, clear bottle or clear plastic bag to hold the water.
2. Place the container in direct sunlight for at least 6 hours.
3. If the water is cloudy,...
Purpose: To purify water using sunlight to kill germs.

Procedure:
1. Use a clean, clear bottle or clear plastic bag to hold the water.
2. Place the container in direct sunlight for at least 6 hours.
3. If the water is cloudy, leave it exposed to sun for at least 2 days.
4. This method will kill most germs in the water, making it safe to drink.

Notes:
• This is a low-cost and effective method for purifying water when boiling is not feasible.
• Ensure that the container is clear so sunlight can pass through effectively.
• Avoid using this method if the water contains high levels of turbidity or chemical contaminants, as it may not be sufficient to remove them.
water purification, solar disinfection, UV treatment, clean water, sanitation
Boiling Water to Kill Germs
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Fill a clean container with water.
2. Bring the water to a rolling boil over high heat.
3. Boil for at least one minute to ensure all germs and bacteria are killed.
4. **Allow the boiled water to cool before using...
Procedure:

1. Fill a clean container with water.
2. Bring the water to a rolling boil over high heat.
3. Boil for at least one minute to ensure all germs and bacteria are killed.
4. Allow the boiled water to cool before using it, especially if it will be used for drinking or preparing food.

Notes:
• Boiling water is an effective method of sterilization in areas where clean water may not be available.
• This procedure should be followed during pregnancy, labor, and postpartum care.
boiling water, sanitation, infection control, midwifery
Chlorine Production and Water Treatment in School Settings
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
Materials: Chlorine production equipment, logs for chlorine usage

1. Calculate the amount of drinking water needed based on two litres per person per day.
2. Determine the required amount of chlorine to treat that volume of water.
3....
Materials: Chlorine production equipment, logs for chlorine usage

1. Calculate the amount of drinking water needed based on two litres per person per day.
2. Determine the required amount of chlorine to treat that volume of water.
3. Organise the production of chlorine:
- Assign a responsible person from the Safe Water Club to oversee maintenance, equipment management, and log-keeping for chlorine usage.

Important Notes:
• This procedure ensures consistent and safe water treatment in school environments.
• The designated individual must be trained in proper chlorine handling and storage.
chlorine production, water treatment, school setting, Safe Water Club, log-keeping
Conduct a Study on Costs and Benefits of Water Pasteurization and Disease Prevention
Standard Source: Solar Cookers
Objective: Explore the costs of pasteurizing drinking water and of treating diseases caused by unsafe water.

Steps:
1. Research the cost of solar water pasteurization systems.
2. Compare these costs to traditional methods of water treatment...
Objective: Explore the costs of pasteurizing drinking water and of treating diseases caused by unsafe water.

Steps:
1. Research the cost of solar water pasteurization systems.
2. Compare these costs to traditional methods of water treatment (e.g., boiling, chemical treatments).
3. Investigate the health impacts of untreated water on communities.
4. Analyze the economic and health benefits of using solar cookers for water pasteurization.
5. Document findings and present them to relevant stakeholders.
water pasteurization, disease prevention, sanitation, solar cookers
Dispose of Used Water Test Safely
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
- The test should be safely disposed of to avoid contamination.

• Burn the test or disinfect it with hot water (>70°C) or with chlorine.

• Do not let children play with the test.
disposal,test,hot water,chlorine,safe disposal
Local Chlorine Production for Water Safety
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
Materials: Equipment for chlorine production, storage containers

Advantages of Local Chlorine Production:
• Ensures quality control, as the production process is monitored locally.
• Independent from external supply chains, making...
Materials: Equipment for chlorine production, storage containers

Advantages of Local Chlorine Production:
• Ensures quality control, as the production process is monitored locally.
• Independent from external supply chains, making it more reliable in remote or resource-limited areas.
• Can be produced on demand, allowing for flexibility and responsiveness to water treatment needs.

Important Notes:
• Local production requires proper training, equipment, and storage facilities.
• It is particularly beneficial in schools and communities where consistent access to commercial chlorine may not be available.
local chlorine production, water safety, quality control, on-demand manufacturing
Apply Betadine Solution for Water Disinfection
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

• Use 8 drops of Betadine solution per 30 minutes in clear water.
• Use 16 drops of Betadine solution per 30 minutes in cloudy water.
• Use 8 drops of Betadine solution per 60 minutes in **cold...
Procedure:

• Use 8 drops of Betadine solution per 30 minutes in clear water.
• Use 16 drops of Betadine solution per 30 minutes in cloudy water.
• Use 8 drops of Betadine solution per 60 minutes in cold water.

Notes:
• This method is a form of chemical disinfection, not filtration.
• Always ensure the correct dosage based on water clarity and temperature to achieve effective disinfection.
Betadine solution, water disinfection, clear water, cloudy water, cold water
Disadvantages and Limitations of Chlorination for Water Purification
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
Materials: None required

Key Disadvantages and Limitations of Using Chlorine for Water Disinfection:
• Requires relatively clear water, as turbid or cloudy water may reduce effectiveness.
• Alters the taste and odour of treated...
Materials: None required

Key Disadvantages and Limitations of Using Chlorine for Water Disinfection:
• Requires relatively clear water, as turbid or cloudy water may reduce effectiveness.
• Alters the taste and odour of treated water, which can affect consumer acceptance.
• Not effective against chemical contamination, such as heavy metals or industrial pollutants.
• Lower protection from certain organisms, like *Cryptosporidium*, even with proper chlorine use.
• Concentrated solutions require careful handling, to avoid health and safety risks.
• Dosage is a main challenge, as incorrect amounts can lead to under-treatment or over-chlorination.
• Not always available locally, which may limit its use in some regions.

Important Notes:
• These limitations must be considered when deciding on the best water treatment method for a specific context.
• Chlorine is most effective when used as part of a broader water safety strategy.
chlorination drawbacks, chemical contamination, taste alteration, dosage challenges, local availability
Keep Water Pure in Iron Kettles
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Place an oyster-shell in the bottom of the iron kettle.
2. This will prevent the iron from rusting and keep the water clear.
iron kettles, water purification, oyster shells, rust prevention
Taste Test for Safe Water Consumption
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
Materials: Cups

1. Taste the water with the children.
- This confirms that you have safely produced a concentrated chlorine solution and treated your water with it.
- Ensure all participants are aware of what they are tasting and that...
Materials: Cups

1. Taste the water with the children.
- This confirms that you have safely produced a concentrated chlorine solution and treated your water with it.
- Ensure all participants are aware of what they are tasting and that this is part of verifying water safety.

2. After five hours, return to the production site:
- Ask one child to check the chlorine concentration using the WataTest.
- Label the treated water and store it properly.
- Rinse the Mini-WATA with clear water.
- Store all materials in a secure place for future use.

Important Notes:
• This step is crucial to confirm that the water has been adequately disinfected.
• The WataTest ensures accurate chlorine concentration levels.
taste test, water safety, chlorine solution, WataTest, Mini-WATA
Understand Maximum Filter Pore Size for Water Purification
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

• Understand the pore size limitations of filters to determine their effectiveness against different pathogens:

1. Parasitic eggs and larvae: Require a filter with a pore size of 20 micrometers (um).
2. **Giardia, *E....
Procedure:

• Understand the pore size limitations of filters to determine their effectiveness against different pathogens:

1. Parasitic eggs and larvae: Require a filter with a pore size of 20 micrometers (um).
2. Giardia, *E. histolytica*: Require a filter with a pore size of 5 um.
3. Cryptosporidium: Require a filter with a pore size of 1 um.
4. Enteric bacteria: Require a filter with a pore size of 0.2 um.
5. Viruses: Too small for field water filtration (require a pore size of 0.01 um).

Important Notes:
• Filters cannot remove viruses or some smaller parasites like Cryptosporidium unless they are specifically designed for that purpose.
• Always use filters in combination with chemical disinfection methods to ensure complete pathogen removal.
filter pore size, water purification, Giardia, Cryptosporidium, enteric bacteria
Advantages of Chlorination for Water Purification
Reference Source: Safe Water Manual
Materials: None required

Key Advantages of Using Chlorine for Water Disinfection:
• Most widely used disinfection method worldwide, with proven effectiveness.
• Kills 99% of pathogens, including viruses, bacteria, and parasites.
-...
Materials: None required

Key Advantages of Using Chlorine for Water Disinfection:
• Most widely used disinfection method worldwide, with proven effectiveness.
• Kills 99% of pathogens, including viruses, bacteria, and parasites.
• Reliable and rapid, requiring only 30 minutes to treat water.
• Residual chlorine provides ongoing protection against recontamination during storage.
• Low cost makes it accessible for large-scale use in communities and schools.
• Easy to use with minimal training required.

Important Notes:
• Chlorination is a highly effective method when used correctly, but requires proper handling of chlorine solutions.
• It is particularly useful in areas where access to other disinfectants may be limited.
chlorination advantages, water purification benefits, pathogen elimination, cost-effective, residual protection

Waste Management (25) -

Apply PMMs for Protection Against Diseases from Human Waste
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Steps:

1. Dig a cat-hole to use for excreting waste if your unit is on the move. Ensure it is covered sufficiently to prevent flies from spreading germs and to keep unwanted animals out of the bivouac area.
2. Use a **straddle trench...
Steps:

1. Dig a cat-hole to use for excreting waste if your unit is on the move. Ensure it is covered sufficiently to prevent flies from spreading germs and to keep unwanted animals out of the bivouac area.
2. Use a straddle trench latrine for short bivouacs or field training exercises—1 to 3 days in duration.
3. Construct deep pit latrines for temporary camps lasting more than 3 days.
4. Use individual waste collection bags on the march, on convoys, or for small groups in isolated areas. Seal and transport the waste with you until it can be burned or buried safely.
5. Use a pail latrine or burn-out latrine when chemical toilets are not available, the ground is too hard, or the soil is very wet. Burn out the latrine daily by adding sufficient fuel to incinerate excrement until it is dry and odorless. Any remaining ash should be buried.
cat-hole, straddle trench latrine, deep pit latrine, waste collection bags
Avoid Shitting in Drinking Water
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Identify and use a designated latrine or toilet area that is at least 20 meters away from any water source.
2. Ensure the latrine has proper waste containment to prevent runoff into nearby water sources.
3. If no latrine is...
Procedure:

1. Identify and use a designated latrine or toilet area that is at least 20 meters away from any water source.
2. Ensure the latrine has proper waste containment to prevent runoff into nearby water sources.
3. If no latrine is available, dig a hole in a location that is not near drinking water and cover it after use.
4. Avoid defecating anywhere close to where water is collected or stored.

Note: Contaminated water can lead to serious illness and death.
water contamination, waste disposal, sanitation
Disposal of Dead Bodies in Shelters
High Priority Source: Sanitation Basics
Procedure:

1. Immediate Action: If a body is discovered in a shelter, act quickly to prevent odor and health risks.
2. Containment Bag: Use large plastic trash bags taped together to create a secure container for the body.
3....
Procedure:

1. Immediate Action: If a body is discovered in a shelter, act quickly to prevent odor and health risks.
2. Containment Bag: Use large plastic trash bags taped together to create a secure container for the body.
3. Perforation: Perforate the bag with a few pinholes to allow gas pressure to escape safely.
4. Outdoor Disposal: As soon as the odor becomes evident, move the body outside and place it in the prepared container.
5. Avoid Sealing Completely: Avoid sealing the body in an airtight bag, as this can cause the bag to burst due to internal gas pressure.
dead body disposal, sanitation, shelter management, plastic bags, odor control
Construct and Maintain Latrines for Expeditions
High Priority Source: Base Camp Hygiene and Health
Procedure:

1. Choose the appropriate latrine system based on the expedition type:
- *For mobile or short-stay sub-camps:* Use an earth pit or trench at least 1.25m deep and 30cm wide.
- *For static base camps:* Construct long-drop...
Procedure:

1. Choose the appropriate latrine system based on the expedition type:
- *For mobile or short-stay sub-camps:* Use an earth pit or trench at least 1.25m deep and 30cm wide.
- *For static base camps:* Construct long-drop latrines that are at least 4m deep with a seat, fly-proof cover, and grab rail/foot-rest if necessary.
2. In unstable soils (e.g., sandy deserts): Use old oil drums (with perforated holes halfway up) to reinforce the sides of trenches or pits, and add sticks between the edges to prevent sand from filling them.
3. Ensure latrines are located more than 50m away from wells, kitchens, and other water sources to avoid contamination.
4. Use ash from fires in trenches to help control odours.
5. Store toilet paper in a tin to keep it dry, prevent ants, and stop it from blowing away.
6. Provide hand-washing facilities near latrines, using soap on a string and ensuring the bowl is a different colour from kitchen bowls for hygiene purposes.
7. Establish procedures for disposal of waste materials such as paper, tampons, etc., away from the latrine area.
8. Consider local and religious requirements when planning latrines (e.g., menstrual flow management in bear-prone areas or specific needs for women).
9. Ensure that local people understand how to use your facilities correctly, especially if they are expected to share them.
10. Clean and dry the latrine area daily using a long-handled brush with soapy water.
11. Check regularly that all components (seats, lids, boards) are in good working condition and replace or repair damaged parts as needed.
12. Use mesh-covered duckboards around the toilet to prevent slips and provide stability in muddy areas.
13. Mark latrine sites clearly for night-time use, and encourage expedition members to carry a torch to check under seats for potential hazards like snakes.
14. Have a fixed rope inside long-drop latrines as a safety line in case of emergencies.
15. Plan for post-expedition cleanup of the latrine area, ensuring it is safe and hygienic after the expedition ends. In areas with permanent frost, all excreta must be stored and removed for proper disposal.
latrine construction, sanitation, expedition waste management, hygiene
Proper Disposal of Garbage for Public Health
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Purpose: To prevent the spread of disease and maintain a healthy environment.

Procedure:
1. Burn all garbage that can be burned.
2. Garbage that cannot be burned should be buried in a special pit or place far away from houses and drinking...
Purpose: To prevent the spread of disease and maintain a healthy environment.

Procedure:
1. Burn all garbage that can be burned.
2. Garbage that cannot be burned should be buried in a special pit or place far away from houses and drinking water sources.
3. Ensure that waste is disposed of in a way that minimizes the risk of disease transmission.

Notes: Improper disposal of garbage can attract pests and spread disease.
garbage disposal, sanitation practices, disease prevention
Set Up and Maintain a Latrine in the Field
High Priority Source: FM 21 10 Field Hygiene and Sanitation
### Procedure: Set Up and Maintain a Latrine in the Field

Steps:
1. Select a location for the latrine at least 50 meters away from water sources, campsites, and food preparation areas.
2. Dig a hole approximately 30 cm deep and wide...
### Procedure: Set Up and Maintain a Latrine in the Field

Steps:
1. Select a location for the latrine at least 50 meters away from water sources, campsites, and food preparation areas.
2. Dig a hole approximately 30 cm deep and wide enough to accommodate a person sitting comfortably.
3. Line the hole with a tarp or plastic sheet to prevent direct contact between waste and soil.
4. Place a small stick or marker at the edge of the hole to indicate its location for future use.
5. Cover the hole after each use with dirt, sand, or leaves to minimize odor and deter animals.
6. Dig a new hole when the current one is full (approximately every 10-20 uses), ensuring that all waste is buried at least 30 cm below ground level.
7. Maintain cleanliness around the latrine area, regularly removing any debris or spilled materials.
8. Use toilet paper sparingly and dispose of it in the hole, not outside the latrine site.
9. Store extra toilet paper and cleaning supplies in a dry container to prevent contamination.
10. Monitor for signs of disease outbreaks, such as unusual illness or complaints from personnel using the latrine.

Details:
• If possible, use portable chemical toilets or waste containers instead of digging holes, especially in areas where soil is unstable or water tables are high.
• In cold climates, dig deeper holes to prevent freezing and ensure proper decomposition.
• When using a latrine, always sit on the hole, not stand over it, to avoid spreading contaminants.
• If latrines cannot be dug, use buried waste bags or containers as an alternative.

Warnings:
• Never dig a latrine in areas with high water tables or near drinking water sources, as this can contaminate the water supply and cause disease outbreaks.
• Avoid using contaminated materials to cover waste, such as animal feces or soil from polluted areas.
latrine setup, field sanitation, waste management, hygiene, portable toilets
Closing and Disposal of Latrines
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Procedure for closing a latrine when it becomes full or is to be abandoned:

1. Insecticide Application: Using an approved, residual insecticide:
- Spray the pit contents.
- Spray the side walls.
- Spray the ground surface for about...
Procedure for closing a latrine when it becomes full or is to be abandoned:

1. Insecticide Application: Using an approved, residual insecticide:
- Spray the pit contents.
- Spray the side walls.
- Spray the ground surface for about 2 feet extending out from the mouth of the pit.

2. Filling the Pit:
- Fill the pit to ground level with successive layers of earth.
- Pack each layer down before adding the next one.

3. Mounding and Final Spraying:
- Mound the pit over with at least one foot of compacted dirt.
- Spray again with insecticide.

4. Marking: When the tactical situation permits, place a rectangular sign on top of the mound stating: “LATRINE CLOSED (date).”
latrine closure, sanitation, waste management
Construction and Use of Urinoil
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Procedure for constructing and using an urinoil (urine disposal system):

1. Improvised Urinoil: An urinoil may be improvised from a 55-gallon drum, as shown in the drawing.

2. Installation Location:
- Place the urinoil on a...
Procedure for constructing and using an urinoil (urine disposal system):

1. Improvised Urinoil: An urinoil may be improvised from a 55-gallon drum, as shown in the drawing.

2. Installation Location:
- Place the urinoil on a soakage pit when possible.
- Install it with a French drain if a soakage pit is not available.

3. Purpose: The urinoil is used for the disposal of urine in field conditions, helping to manage liquid waste and prevent contamination.
urinoil, sanitation, urine disposal
Establish Camp Toilet Area
Standard Source: US Antarctic Continental Field Manual
Set up a camp toilet area:

• This may be a tent with a human waste container or a hole in the snow (where allowed, in accumulation zones).
camp sanitation,toilet setup,waste management
Maintenance of Latrines
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Procedure for the maintenance and upkeep of latrines:

1. Enclosure: After construction, enclose latrines in shelters to:
- Facilitate insect control.
- Allow for routine cleaning.
- Ensure privacy.
- Protect from flooding with...
Procedure for the maintenance and upkeep of latrines:

1. Enclosure: After construction, enclose latrines in shelters to:
- Facilitate insect control.
- Allow for routine cleaning.
- Ensure privacy.
- Protect from flooding with rainwater.

2. Drainage: Dig drainage ditches around latrines to prevent surface runoff from flooding them.

3. Toilet Paper Supply:
- Provide sufficient amounts of toilet paper daily.
- Cover rolls with inverted tin cans to protect the paper from moisture.

4. Hand Washing Facilities:
- Install a simple, easily operated hand washing device outside each latrine.
- Keep these devices filled with hand soap and potable water.

5. Cleaning and Disinfection:
- Sweep and scrub all surfaces of each latrine daily.
- Disinfect the seats using a mild chlorine solution.

6. Trash Receptacles: Provide a convenient trash receptacle inside latrine enclosures and empty it daily.

7. Butt Kits: Provide and maintain butt kits outside each latrine, and empty them daily.

8. Urine Tube Screens: Construct urine tube screens in the shape of a cone with the apex pointing up.

9. Closure and Privacy:
- Keep doors and seat lids closed when not in use.

10. Identification: Mark or number each latrine to facilitate cleaning and inspection schedules.
latrine maintenance, sanitation, waste management
Proper Rubbish Disposal and Management
Standard Source: Base Camp Hygiene and Health
Procedure for Proper Rubbish Disposal

1. Dispose of rubbish daily: Prevent smell, flies, and infectious diseases by regularly removing waste.
2. Burn rubbish before disposal: Burn all materials (including the top layer of grass or...
Procedure for Proper Rubbish Disposal

1. Dispose of rubbish daily: Prevent smell, flies, and infectious diseases by regularly removing waste.
2. Burn rubbish before disposal: Burn all materials (including the top layer of grass or leaves from grease traps) to reduce volume and eliminate odors.
3. Designate a burning area: Choose a site away from the main camp for burning.
4. Use a shallow pit for fire containment: Ensure it is safe and controlled.
5. Remove lids from containers before burning: Prevent small explosions by removing lids from tins, glass, etc.
6. Flatten items like tins: Use a mallet to flatten metal cans for easier burning.
7. Create a rubbish pit on a slight gradient: Promote drainage of rainwater and prevent waterlogging.
8. Avoid using petrol to ignite fires: Petrol is highly flammable and dangerous.
rubbish disposal,waste management,burning,sanitation,fire safety
Sanitation and Waste Management in Survival Situations
Standard Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion Recovery
Objective: Maintain sanitation to prevent disease transmission.

Steps:
1. Locate latrines at least 200 feet from water sources and away from shelter to avoid contamination.
2. **Wash hands thoroughly before preparing food or handling...
Objective: Maintain sanitation to prevent disease transmission.

Steps:
1. Locate latrines at least 200 feet from water sources and away from shelter to avoid contamination.
2. Wash hands thoroughly before preparing food or handling water using available soap substitutes if necessary.
3. Clean all eating utensils after each meal with clean water or by scrubbing with sand or soil.
4. Prevent insect bites by wearing long clothing, using insect repellent, and sleeping under a net if possible.
5. Dry wet clothing as soon as possible to prevent fungal infections and hypothermia.
6. Eat a varied diet to ensure proper nutrition.
7. Aim for 7-8 hours of sleep per day to maintain physical and mental health.

Notes: Proper sanitation is essential in preventing illness during survival scenarios.
latrine location, hand washing, sanitation, insect prevention, sleep hygiene
Determine Number of Chemical Toilets Required
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Procedure for determining the number of chemical toilets required based on personnel count:

1. Use the following ratio to determine the number of chemical toilets:
- 1 to 15 personnel: 1 toilet
- 16 to 35 personnel: **2...
Procedure for determining the number of chemical toilets required based on personnel count:

1. Use the following ratio to determine the number of chemical toilets:
- 1 to 15 personnel: 1 toilet
- 16 to 35 personnel: 2 toilets
- 36 to 55 personnel: 3 toilets
- 56 to 80 personnel: 4 toilets
- 61 to 110 personnel: 5 toilets
- 111 to 150 personnel: 6 toilets
- Over 150 personnel: Add 1 toilet for each additional 40 persons.

2. If females are not involved in the exercise, urinals may be substituted for some of the seat type latrines. However, the number of seats should not be reduced to less than 2/3 of the number specified.

3. Personnel must maintain a current telephone number for the contract service and order chemical toilets at the ratio displayed in Table 9-6.
chemical toilet, personnel count, sanitation, waste management
Garbage Disposal in Permafrost Areas
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

• In areas with high permafrost tables and cold weather conditions, garbage cannot be buried.
• Collection and Storage: Garbage must be:
- Collected in central locations
- Moved to the rear as transport becomes available
-...
Procedure:

• In areas with high permafrost tables and cold weather conditions, garbage cannot be buried.
• Collection and Storage: Garbage must be:
- Collected in central locations
- Moved to the rear as transport becomes available
• Alternative Disposal Methods: If transport is not available, garbage should be:
- Dropped into ravines or valleys
- Covered with bulldozers for concealment and environmental protection
permafrost,waste disposal,bulldozer coverage,central collection,transport availability
Maintain Salvage Area Hygiene and Separation
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Procedure:

1. Physical Separation:
- Ensure the salvage area is physically separated from all other product storage areas.
2. Daily Cleaning:
- Clean the salvage area daily.
3. Sorting Salvageable Items:
- Separate...
Procedure:

1. Physical Separation:
- Ensure the salvage area is physically separated from all other product storage areas.
2. Daily Cleaning:
- Clean the salvage area daily.
3. Sorting Salvageable Items:
- Separate salvageable items from non-salvageable items.
4. Hazardous Item Handling:
- Do not place damaged hazardous items in the salvage area at any time.
salvage area, waste management, hazardous items, separation
Manage Latrines and Garbage in Cold Weather
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Purpose: Proper management of latrines and garbage to maintain hygiene and prevent disease.

Procedure:
• Refer to Chapter 3, Section 1, Articles 307 and 308 for detailed instructions on latrine construction, waste disposal, and garbage...
Purpose: Proper management of latrines and garbage to maintain hygiene and prevent disease.

Procedure:
