• Place the E. coli plates in a dry and dark location at a temperature between 25–35°C.

• Allow them to incubate for 24 hours.

• After use, clean all equipment thoroughly and store it...
Step 3: Incubation of the E. coli plates

• Place the E. coli plates in a dry and dark location at a temperature between 25–35°C.

• Allow them to incubate for 24 hours.

• After use, clean all equipment thoroughly and store it in a dry and clean place.
E. coli incubation, colony growth, water testing
Inoculate E. coli Plate with Water Sample
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
Step 2: Inoculation of the E. coli plate

• Use a 1-ml sterile syringe and fill it with the water sample to be tested.

• Remove the lid of the E. coli plate and place it face up on a clean surface. Do not touch the inside of the lid.

-...
Step 2: Inoculation of the E. coli plate

• Use a 1-ml sterile syringe and fill it with the water sample to be tested.

• Remove the lid of the E. coli plate and place it face up on a clean surface. Do not touch the inside of the lid.

• Distribute the 1-ml water sample over the E. coli plate.

• Carefully close the lid to prevent contamination.

• Label the lid with:
- The type of water used (e.g., raw water, chlorinated water, SODIS water)
- The date of testing
E. coli inoculation, water sample preparation, sanitation, testing
Interpret E. coli Test Results for Water Safety
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
Step 5: Interpretation of the test results

• Focus on blue spots, as they indicate faecal contamination.

• Use the following table to interpret the results:

|Test result|Interpretation|Water safety|
|---|---|---|
|No...
Step 5: Interpretation of the test results

• Focus on blue spots, as they indicate faecal contamination.

• Use the following table to interpret the results:

|Test result|Interpretation|Water safety|
|---|---|---|
|No spots|Neither faecal nor environmental contamination detected.|The test indicates safe water.|
|Only red spots|No faecal contamination; only harmless environmental contamination detected.|The test indicates safe water.|
|Only blue spots|Recent faecal contamination is present. Drinking this water can cause illness.|The test indicates unsafe water.|
|Red and blue spots|Both recent faecal and environmental contamination are present. Drinking this water can cause illness.|The test indicates unsafe water.|

• Important: Safe water should have no blue spots.
E. coli interpretation, water safety, test results
Using the WataTest Reagent Kit for Chlorine Solution Quality Control
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
Overview: The WataTest reagent kit is used to check the quality of concentrated chlorine solution produced by WATA. Follow these steps carefully:

Kit Contents:
• 1 flask of WataTest reagent
• 1 syringe (1 mL): This must be used...
Overview: The WataTest reagent kit is used to check the quality of concentrated chlorine solution produced by WATA. Follow these steps carefully:

Kit Contents:
• 1 flask of WataTest reagent
• 1 syringe (1 mL): This must be used only for taking a WataTest reagent sample.
• 1 plastic pipette (3 mL): This must be used only for taking a chlorine concentrate sample.

Procedure:
1. Ensure that all components of the WataTest kit are present and in good condition before use.
2. Use the 1 mL syringe to take a precise sample of the WataTest reagent from its flask.
3. Use the 3 mL plastic pipette to take a sample of the concentrated chlorine solution you wish to test.
4. Follow the specific testing protocol provided by ANTENNA Research (wata@antenna.ch or www.antenna.ch) for performing the quality control check using these samples.
5. Dispose of used materials according to local regulations and safety guidelines.

Important Notes:
• The syringe and pipette are for specific use only as indicated on the kit packaging.
• Always follow the instructions provided by ANTENNA Research for accurate results.
WataTest reagent kit, chlorine solution quality control, water testing, sanitation, water safety

Animal Husbandry (6) -

Dip Hogs to Eliminate Lice and Disease Germs
High Priority Source: Practical Pointers On The Care of Lifestock
Dipping Hogs:

• Put the hogs and pigs through the dipping vat.
• Spray the quarters and feed receptacles occasionally with a strong solution of Pratts Dip and Disinfectant.
• Result: Comfortable, vermin-free and disease-free hogs, less...
Dipping Hogs:

• Put the hogs and pigs through the dipping vat.
• Spray the quarters and feed receptacles occasionally with a strong solution of Pratts Dip and Disinfectant.
• Result: Comfortable, vermin-free and disease-free hogs, less loss, more pork, more money.

When to Use:
• To eliminate lice and vermin that destroy comfort and reduce profits.
• To exterminate germs which cause cholera and tuberculosis.

Important Notes:
• There are other dips that look like the original Pratts, but they are not the same in efficiency. Refuse substitutes. Use Pratts Dip and Disinfectant, the dip you can depend upon.
• It costs no more but it's worth more! You be the judge: "YOUR MONEY BACK IF YOU ARE NOT SATISFIED."
hog dipping,lice removal,disease prevention,pratts dip
Disinfection of Contaminated Objects and Areas to Prevent Spread of Cattle Plague
High Priority Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Identify all contaminated objects and areas, including:
- Racks, cribs, mangers (wood, iron, or stone)
- Collars, straps, ropes, harnesses, carts, wagons, carriages
- Gutters or drains with urine from diseased cattle
...
Procedure:

1. Identify all contaminated objects and areas, including:
- Racks, cribs, mangers (wood, iron, or stone)
- Collars, straps, ropes, harnesses, carts, wagons, carriages
- Gutters or drains with urine from diseased cattle
- Implements used for removing manure
- Manure heaps and ground where diseased animals have stood
- Paths and roads walked by infected cattle

2. Apply disinfectants to all these items, using chloride of lime solution (1 pound per gallon water) applied via garden engine or watering can after thorough cleaning.

3. Bury deeply in dry ground any animal that has died from the plague, along with contaminated materials like droppings and straw.

4. Cover infected manure heaps with a layer of earth to prevent contamination spread.

5. Disinfect wooden or iron objects by exposing them to heat sufficient to char wood; galvanize any iron items afterward.
disinfection, sanitation, livestock health
Hygienic Measures for Personnel and Implements to Prevent Spread of Cattle Plague
High Priority Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Ensure all personnel working with infected cattle wash their bodies and hair thoroughly with soap after handling contaminated materials.

2. Destroy or disinfect clothing used in contaminated areas, especially if it is of...
Procedure:

1. Ensure all personnel working with infected cattle wash their bodies and hair thoroughly with soap after handling contaminated materials.

2. Destroy or disinfect clothing used in contaminated areas, especially if it is of little value, using chloride of lime water.

3. Avoid cross-contamination:
- Ensure brooms and other implements are cleaned before moving from a dirty to a clean area.
- Workers must be careful not to transfer infection between locations.

4. Disinfect all tools and equipment used in the handling of diseased animals or contaminated materials.
hygiene, sanitation, livestock health
Quarantine New Hogs to Prevent Disease Spread
High Priority Source: Practical Pointers On The Care of Lifestock
Quarantine for Newly Purchased Hogs:

• Whenever any animals are brought to the farm, or when animals are brought home from shows or from neighboring farms, they should be kept apart from the rest of the herd for at least three weeks.
• If...
Quarantine for Newly Purchased Hogs:

• Whenever any animals are brought to the farm, or when animals are brought home from shows or from neighboring farms, they should be kept apart from the rest of the herd for at least three weeks.
• If they have been exposed to hog cholera or swine plague, the diseases will be manifested within this time, and the sick animals can be treated or killed and disposed of at once.

Important Notes:
• Newly purchased hogs should be carefully examined for vermin and should not be turned out with the herd until they are known to be free from these pests.
hog quarantine,new hogs,vermin inspection,disease prevention
Sanitation Measures for Hog Quarters
High Priority Source: Practical Pointers On The Care of Lifestock
Sanitation Measures:

• The quarters in which the sickness first appeared should be thoroughly cleaned, all bedding and rubbish burned, and loose boards and old partitions torn out and burned.
• If the pen is old, knock it to pieces and burn...
Sanitation Measures:

• The quarters in which the sickness first appeared should be thoroughly cleaned, all bedding and rubbish burned, and loose boards and old partitions torn out and burned.
• If the pen is old, knock it to pieces and burn it.
• Disinfect pens and sleeping places using Pratts Dip and Disinfectant on the floors, walls, and ceilings.
• Whitewash everything.

Dealing with a Dead Hog:
• If a hog dies from any cause, the carcass should never be exposed where it may be devoured by other hogs or passing birds or beasts.
• Burn it at once or bury deeply.
• The pens should be thoroughly disinfected immediately.
• If possible, do not move the carcass from the place where it falls; but if this cannot be done, the ground over which it is dragged should be disinfected.

Quarantine Measures:
• Maintain an absolute quarantine between the sick and well hogs.
• The same attendant should not care for both lots unless he disinfects himself thoroughly after each visit to the infected hogs.
• Dogs should be confined until the disease is stamped out.
hog sanitation,disinfectant,quarantine,dead hog disposal
Sanitation and Disinfection Measures for Stalls and Sheds to Prevent Cattle Plague Spread
High Priority Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Remove all litter from stalls and sheds daily, especially those housing Group 2 or Group 3 cattle.

2. Wash and thoroughly clean the floors of these areas with water and soap.

3. Ventilate the area well to ensure...
Procedure:

1. Remove all litter from stalls and sheds daily, especially those housing Group 2 or Group 3 cattle.

2. Wash and thoroughly clean the floors of these areas with water and soap.

3. Ventilate the area well to ensure fresh air circulation.

4. Fumigate with the following:
- Burnt sulphur
- Dried juniper berries, sage, and rosemary salted with saltpetre and a little arsenic (place on coals)

5. At night, leave one of the following in the stable to diffuse vapors:
- Tar
- Creosote
- Benzine
- Petroleum
- Iodine

6. Ensure that these substances are placed safely and monitored for effectiveness.
disinfection, sanitation, fumigation, livestock health

Disease Prevention (6) -

Home-Bringing Message on Faeces and Disease Prevention
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
### Home-Bringing Message on Faeces and Disease Prevention

Key Messages:
• Faeces can contain millions of dangerous small organisms.
• Open faeces are a threat to health.
• **I will be a Safe Water Promoter to help prevent...
### Home-Bringing Message on Faeces and Disease Prevention

Key Messages:
• Faeces can contain millions of dangerous small organisms.
• Open faeces are a threat to health.
• I will be a Safe Water Promoter to help prevent diseases.

Purpose: To reinforce the importance of proper sanitation and disease prevention at home.
faeces,dangerous organisms,sanitation,health threat,safe water promoter
Identify Faecal-Oral Transmission Routes Using the F-Diagram
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
The faecal-oral transmission mechanism can occur through several routes:

1. Fingers: Contaminated hands can transfer pathogens to the mouth.
2. Flies (insects): Flies can carry pathogens from feces to food or surfaces.
3. Fields:...
The faecal-oral transmission mechanism can occur through several routes:

1. Fingers: Contaminated hands can transfer pathogens to the mouth.
2. Flies (insects): Flies can carry pathogens from feces to food or surfaces.
3. Fields: Contaminated soil or agricultural areas can harbor pathogens.
4. Fluids: Bodily fluids such as saliva, urine, and feces can transmit disease.
5. Food: Consuming contaminated food is a common route of transmission.
6. Direct to the mouth: Ingestion of contaminated water or substances directly into the mouth.

This mechanism is often referred to as the F-Diagram due to its use of multiple 'F-words' in English.
faecal-oral transmission,F-diagram,disease prevention,waterborne illness,pathogen transmission
Identify Local Practices That Break Disease Transmission Routes
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
### Identify Local Practices That Break Disease Transmission Routes

Materials: Drawing materials

Steps:
1. Ask each group to identify local practices that can break the transmission routes.
2. Distribute the images “Disease transmission...
### Identify Local Practices That Break Disease Transmission Routes

Materials: Drawing materials

Steps:
1. Ask each group to identify local practices that can break the transmission routes.
2. Distribute the images “Disease transmission barriers” to each group.
3. Let the children place the received images on the diagram.
4. The distributed images do not cover all situations. The groups can draw additional transmission barriers, such as covering food or cooking food.
5. Let the groups show and explain their completed diagrams to the other groups.
6. Ensure understanding of the disease transmission routes and repeat that there are easy and efficient ways to create disease transmission barriers.

Examples of Disease Transmission Barriers:
• Water quality improvements (e.g. water disinfection)
• Hygiene improvements (e.g. hand washing, food storage)
• Sanitation improvements (e.g. proper use of toilet)
disease transmission barriers,local practices,sanitation,washing hands,water disinfection
Preventing Spread of Respiratory Diseases in Shelters
High Priority Source: Sanitation Basics
Procedure:

1. Ventilation: Ensure adequate ventilation within the shelter to reduce the risk of disease transmission.
2. Isolation of Sick Individuals: In small shelters, have individuals who are coughing or sneezing stay near the air...
Procedure:

1. Ventilation: Ensure adequate ventilation within the shelter to reduce the risk of disease transmission.
2. Isolation of Sick Individuals: In small shelters, have individuals who are coughing or sneezing stay near the air exhaust opening to minimize contamination of other areas.
3. Crowd Management: In large shelters with many occupants, be especially vigilant about monitoring for signs of respiratory illness and take additional precautions to prevent spread.
4. Hygiene Practices: Encourage frequent handwashing and use of face coverings if available.
respiratory disease prevention, ventilation, shelter hygiene, disease control
Preventing Waterborne Diseases Through Hygiene and Latrine Use
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Preventing Waterborne Diseases Through Hygiene and Latrine Use

• Know where and how to make latrines, and use and maintain them.
• Wash your hands with clean water and soap or ash:
- before preparing food or water;
- before...
Preventing Waterborne Diseases Through Hygiene and Latrine Use

• Know where and how to make latrines, and use and maintain them.
• Wash your hands with clean water and soap or ash:
- before preparing food or water;
- before eating;
- after going to the latrine;
- after passing stools or cleaning a baby’s bottom.
• Dispose of rubbish safely, for example by burning it in a hole and then covering it properly afterwards.
waterborne diseases prevention, hygiene practices, latrine use
Protect Against Diseases from Human Waste
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Procedure:

To protect against diseases from human waste, follow these steps:

1. Ensure proper disposal of human waste in designated latrines or using portable sanitation systems.
2. Avoid contact with human waste by wearing gloves and using...
Procedure:

To protect against diseases from human waste, follow these steps:

1. Ensure proper disposal of human waste in designated latrines or using portable sanitation systems.
2. Avoid contact with human waste by wearing gloves and using appropriate protective gear when handling waste.
3. Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water after any exposure to human waste.
4. Disinfect surfaces contaminated with human waste using approved disinfectants.
5. Educate personnel on the risks of diseases transmitted through human waste, such as hepatitis A and E, and parasitic infections.

Warnings:
• Failure to properly dispose of or handle human waste can lead to serious illness.
• Always use personal protective equipment (PPE) when dealing with human waste.
human waste, disease prevention, sanitation, hygiene

Field Hygiene (6) -

Implementing Field Hygiene and Sanitation Procedures
High Priority Source: FM 21 10 Field Hygiene and Sanitation
### Field Hygiene and Sanitation Procedures

#### General Principles of Field Hygiene and Sanitation

1. Maintain personal hygiene by washing hands frequently with soap and water, especially before eating, after using the bathroom, and after...
### Field Hygiene and Sanitation Procedures

#### General Principles of Field Hygiene and Sanitation

1. Maintain personal hygiene by washing hands frequently with soap and water, especially before eating, after using the bathroom, and after handling waste.
2. Keep living areas clean and free from debris to prevent the spread of disease.
3. Ensure that food is stored in airtight containers to avoid contamination.
4. Dispose of human waste properly by digging latrines at least 50 feet away from water sources and cooking areas.
5. Wash clothing regularly using soap and clean water, and dry them in direct sunlight if possible.
6. Use insect repellent and wear protective clothing to prevent insect-borne diseases.
7. Ensure that drinking water is boiled or treated with purification tablets before consumption.
8. Regularly inspect and maintain sanitation facilities to ensure they are functional and hygienic.

#### Waste Disposal

• Human waste should be disposed of in latrines dug at least 50 feet away from water sources and cooking areas.
• Waste should be buried or covered with soil to prevent contamination.
• Animal waste should also be disposed of properly, either by burying it or using designated waste pits.

#### Water Sanitation

• All drinking water must be boiled for at least one minute or treated with purification tablets before consumption.
• Water storage containers should be cleaned and disinfected regularly to prevent bacterial growth.
• Water sources should be tested periodically for contamination, especially in areas where disease outbreaks are common.

#### Food Handling

• Food should be stored in airtight containers to prevent contamination from insects or animals.
• All food preparation surfaces should be cleaned with soap and water before and after use.
• Cook food thoroughly to kill harmful bacteria, especially meat and eggs.
• Leftover food should be stored properly and consumed within a safe time frame to avoid spoilage.

#### Personal Hygiene

• Wash hands frequently with soap and clean water, especially before eating, after using the bathroom, and after handling waste.
• Brush teeth regularly using a toothbrush and toothpaste if available, or use alternative methods such as chewing sticks.
• Keep personal items such as clothing, bedding, and towels clean and dry to prevent the spread of disease.

#### Insect Control

• Use insect repellent containing DEET or other effective ingredients to protect against mosquito bites.
• Wear long sleeves and pants when possible to reduce exposure to insects.
• Sleep under mosquito nets if available, especially in areas with high rates of malaria or other vector-borne diseases.
• Eliminate standing water around living areas to prevent the breeding of mosquitoes and other pests.

#### Sanitation Facilities

• Construct latrines at least 50 feet away from water sources and cooking areas.
• Ensure that latrines are covered with a lid or screen to prevent animals from accessing them.
• Regularly clean and maintain sanitation facilities to ensure they remain functional and hygienic.
• Use gloves when handling waste to avoid direct contact with human or animal excrement.

#### Health Monitoring

• Monitor the health of all individuals in the group for signs of illness, such as fever, diarrhea, or vomiting.
• Isolate sick individuals from the rest of the group to prevent the spread of disease.
• Provide medical care to those who are ill and seek professional help if necessary.
• Keep a record of any illnesses or injuries that occur during field operations for future reference.

#### Emergency Situations

• In the event of an outbreak of disease, take immediate steps to isolate affected individuals and implement additional sanitation measures.
• If there is a lack of clean water, prioritize boiling or purifying all available water sources before consumption.
• In extreme conditions where medical care is not available, provide basic first aid and monitor the condition of those who are ill until professional help can be obtained.
hygiene,sanitation,waste disposal,water purification,food handling,personal hygiene,insect control,latrines,health monitoring
Maintain Area Sanitation to Prevent Disease Spread
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Bury all empty ration packets and residue properly.
2. Locate, construct, and use field sanitation facilities in accordance with established guidelines.
3. Ensure latrine facilities include soap and water for handwashing.
4. Clean...
Procedure:

1. Bury all empty ration packets and residue properly.
2. Locate, construct, and use field sanitation facilities in accordance with established guidelines.
3. Ensure latrine facilities include soap and water for handwashing.
4. Clean latrines daily and avoid leaving them open or unmarked before moving.
5. Control insect and rodent populations to prevent the spread of disease.
6. Refer to FM 21-10 and FM 21-10-1 for additional information on field sanitation.
area sanitation,nbc conditions,field hygiene,latrines,insect control
Construct and Maintain Handwashing Stations
Standard Source: FM 21 10 Field Hygiene and Sanitation
Procedure:

1. Select a location for the handwashing station near latrines and food preparation areas.
2. Gather materials such as containers (e.g., buckets, cans), sticks or poles, soap if available, and water.
3. **Assemble the...
Procedure:

1. Select a location for the handwashing station near latrines and food preparation areas.
2. Gather materials such as containers (e.g., buckets, cans), sticks or poles, soap if available, and water.
3. Assemble the station by placing a container under a stick or pole to hold water for washing hands.
4. Provide soap if available, and place it near the handwashing area.
5. Ensure access to clean water by connecting the station to a nearby water source or using stored water in containers.
6. Maintain the station by regularly cleaning it with disinfectants if available and refilling water as needed.
7. Educate personnel on proper handwashing techniques, including washing hands for at least 20 seconds with soap and water.
handwashing,hygiene station,water access,soap use,sanitation maintenance
Construct and Maintain a Field Latrine
Standard Source: FM 21 10 Field Hygiene and Sanitation
Procedure:

1. Select a location for the latrine that is at least 50 meters away from food preparation areas, water sources, and sleeping quarters.
2. Dig a pit or trench that is at least 3 feet deep and 2 feet wide.
3. **Line the bottom...
Procedure:

1. Select a location for the latrine that is at least 50 meters away from food preparation areas, water sources, and sleeping quarters.
2. Dig a pit or trench that is at least 3 feet deep and 2 feet wide.
3. Line the bottom of the pit with gravel or sand to improve drainage.
4. Cover the pit with a wooden board or tarp to prevent animals from digging into it.
5. Construct a simple shelter using available materials such as branches, leaves, or tarps to provide privacy and protection from rain.
6. Maintain the latrine by regularly cleaning it with water and disinfectants if available.
7. Monitor usage to ensure that the latrine is not overflowing or becoming contaminated.
latrine construction,latrine maintenance,sanitation facilities,drainage,privacy shelter
Establish Field Sanitation Facilities
Standard Source: FM 21 10 Field Hygiene and Sanitation
Procedure:

1. Select a location for sanitation facilities that is at least 50 meters away from food preparation areas, water sources, and sleeping quarters.
2. Dig latrines using the following guidelines:
- Dig pits or trenches that...
Procedure:

1. Select a location for sanitation facilities that is at least 50 meters away from food preparation areas, water sources, and sleeping quarters.
2. Dig latrines using the following guidelines:
- Dig pits or trenches that are at least 3 feet deep and 2 feet wide.
- Line the bottom of the pit with a layer of gravel or sand for drainage.
- Cover the pit with a wooden board or tarp to prevent animals from digging into it.
3. Construct handwashing stations using available materials such as containers, sticks, and soap if available.
4. Designate waste disposal areas for non-biodegradable items like plastic, metal, and glass. These should be kept separate from latrines and food preparation areas.
5. Maintain sanitation facilities by regularly cleaning them with water and disinfectants if available.
6. Monitor the use of latrines to ensure that they are not overflowing or becoming contaminated.
7. Educate personnel on proper hygiene practices, including handwashing after using the latrine and before eating.
latrines,waste disposal,handwashing,field sanitation,facility construction
Setting Up a Field Dishwashing Unit Using GI Cans
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Procedure for Setting Up a Field Dishwashing Unit Using GI Cans

1. Use five (5) metal GI cans, each approximately 32 gallons in size, to set up a field dishwashing unit.
2. Designate the cans for specific purposes:
- One can for...
Procedure for Setting Up a Field Dishwashing Unit Using GI Cans

1. Use five (5) metal GI cans, each approximately 32 gallons in size, to set up a field dishwashing unit.
2. Designate the cans for specific purposes:
- One can for washing dishes with soapy water.
- One can for rinsing dishes with clean water.
- One can for sanitizing dishes using an approved sanitizing solution.
- One can for air drying dishes after they have been sanitized.
- One can as a holding area for dirty dishes before washing.
3. Ensure proper workflow:
- Place dirty dishes in the designated holding can.
- Wash dishes in the soapy water can, then rinse them in the clean water can.
- Submerge dishes in the sanitizing solution can to ensure they are fully sanitized.
- Transfer sanitized dishes to the air drying can for thorough drying before reuse.
4. Maintain hygiene throughout the process by ensuring that all cans and utensils used are cleaned and sanitized regularly.
field dishwashing unit,GI cans,sanitizing,dishwashing setup

Latrines (6) -

Maintain Latrine Hygiene
High Priority Source: Base Camp Hygiene and Health
Procedure for Maintaining Latrine Hygiene

1. Clean the latrine seat daily: Ensure it is free from dirt and debris.
2. Check operation and safety: Verify that the latrine functions properly and is safe to use.
3. Keep the area dry:...
Procedure for Maintaining Latrine Hygiene

1. Clean the latrine seat daily: Ensure it is free from dirt and debris.
2. Check operation and safety: Verify that the latrine functions properly and is safe to use.
3. Keep the area dry: Avoid pouring disinfectants, as this can damage surfaces or create slippery conditions.
4. Ensure fly-proofing: Make sure seats and covers are secure and prevent flies from entering.
5. Beware of snakes: Use a torch at night when using the latrine to avoid encountering snakes.
6. Provide hand-washing facilities nearby: Ensure that clean water and soap are available for proper hygiene after use.
latrine,clean,hygiene,sanitation,flies,snakes
Sanitary Facilities Planning for Collective-Protection Shelters
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Sanitary Facilities Planning for Collective-Protection Shelters

1. If the shelter is in a permanent structure:
- Use existing sanitary facilities.
2. Consider the location of existing sanitary facilities when selecting a portion of the...
Sanitary Facilities Planning for Collective-Protection Shelters

1. If the shelter is in a permanent structure:
- Use existing sanitary facilities.
2. Consider the location of existing sanitary facilities when selecting a portion of the building for a soldier relief facility.
3. Where water and sewage facilities are not available:
- Provide covered containers or chemical toilets.
sanitary facilities, chemical toilets, covered containers
Construct a Burn-Barrel Latrine
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Purpose: Create a temporary latrine in hard or frozen soil conditions where digging is difficult.

Materials Required: Plywood, framing lumber, 55-gallon drums (cut in half), burn barrels, diesel fuel, “NO SMOKING” signs.

Procedure:
1....
Purpose: Create a temporary latrine in hard or frozen soil conditions where digging is difficult.

Materials Required: Plywood, framing lumber, 55-gallon drums (cut in half), burn barrels, diesel fuel, “NO SMOKING” signs.

Procedure:
1. Select Location: Choose a site at least 100 feet from natural water sources, 100 yards from food service areas, and 50 feet from berthing areas. Ensure it is reasonably near the user.
2. Construct Enclosure: Build an enclosed structure using plywood and suitable framing lumber with a mesh screen (#18) to prevent access by animals or insects.
3. Install Seats and Barrels: Place 2–4 toilet seats over cut-in-half 55-gallon drums, each positioned under a burn barrel. Ensure the top rim of each barrel is no more than 2 inches from the underside of the seat (to avoid splashing).
4. Prime Barrels: Fill each barrel with 3 inches of diesel fuel before placing them into service to help oil soak fecal matter and enhance thermal destruction during burning.
5. Post Warning Signs: Display “NO SMOKING” signs prominently inside and outside the structure to prevent fires.
6. Usage Instructions: Users should use the latrine as needed, ensuring that each barrel is not overfilled.
7. Burn Out Process: When a barrel is 1/2 to 2/3 full, remove it from the structure and burn out the contents completely.
8. Encourage Usage: Encourage personnel to use the latrine regularly to maintain hygiene and prevent accumulation of waste.
burn barrel latrine, temporary sanitation, field latrine, waste disposal
Construct a Deep Pit Latrine
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Purpose: Create a temporary latrine for up to 50 people.

Materials Required: Latrine box (with four seats), planks, fly-proof material (wood or metal strips, sandbags, oil-soaked burlap), metal deflector, insecticide,...
Purpose: Create a temporary latrine for up to 50 people.

Materials Required: Latrine box (with four seats), planks, fly-proof material (wood or metal strips, sandbags, oil-soaked burlap), metal deflector, insecticide, dirt.

Procedure:
1. Select Location: Choose a site at least 100 feet from natural water sources, 100 yards from food service areas, and 50 feet from berthing areas. Ensure it is reasonably near the user.
2. Dig Pit: Excavate a pit that is 2 feet wide, 7½ feet long, and up to 6 feet deep (alternatively, a 4-foot square pit for a 5-foot square latrine box).
3. Construct Latrine Box: Mount the standard latrine box on two 6-inch planks over the pit. Ensure it is 8 feet long and 2½ feet wide at the base.
4. Fly-Proofing: Cover cracks in the wood with strips of wood or metal, sandbags, or oil-soaked burlap to prevent flies from entering.
5. Urine Deflector: Secure a metal deflector on the inside surface of the front panel of the box to prevent urine from soaking into the wood.
6. Seats and Lids: Provide fly-proof, self-closing lids for each seat. Sand skin contact surfaces smooth.
7. Usage Instructions: Users should close the lid after use to maintain hygiene and prevent flies.
8. Close Pit: When the pit is filled to within 1 foot from the top:
- Spray the excreta with an approved insecticide.
- Fill the pit with several layers of compacted dirt.
- Mound the dirt at least one foot above ground level.
9. Mark Closure: Post a sign indicating the type of latrine and the date it was closed, as illustrated in Figure 9–9.
10. Additional Notes: Provide a separate urinal for each male deep pit latrine to improve sanitation.
deep pit latrine, temporary sanitation, field latrine, waste disposal
Construct a Straddle Trench Latrine
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Purpose: Create a temporary latrine for up to 25 people.

Materials Required: Shovel, entrenching tool, boards, toilet paper, cans, insecticide, dirt.

Procedure:
1. Select Location: Choose a site at least 100 feet from natural water...
Purpose: Create a temporary latrine for up to 25 people.

Materials Required: Shovel, entrenching tool, boards, toilet paper, cans, insecticide, dirt.

Procedure:
1. Select Location: Choose a site at least 100 feet from natural water sources, 100 yards from food service areas, and 50 feet from berthing areas. Ensure it is reasonably near the user.
2. Dig Trench: Excavate a trench that is 1 foot wide, 2½ feet deep, and 4 feet long.
3. Provide Footing: Place boards along both sides of the trench to provide better footing for users.
4. Keep Toilet Paper Dry: Set rolls of toilet paper on posts, covered with cans to keep them dry.
5. Privacy and Wind Break: If there is no natural concealment, construct a wood or canvas screen to provide privacy and act as a wind break.
6. Manage Excavated Earth: Pile the earth removed from digging at one end of the trench for prompt covering after use.
7. Usage Instructions: Each user must cover their excreta and toilet paper with dirt using their own entrenching tool immediately after use.
8. Close Trench: When the trench is filled to within 1 foot from the top:
- Spray the excreta with an approved insecticide.
- Fill the trench with several layers of compacted dirt.
- Mound the dirt at least one foot above ground level.
9. Mark Closure: Post a sign indicating the type of latrine and the date it was closed, as illustrated in Figure 9–8.
straddle trench latrine, temporary sanitation, field latrine, waste disposal
Position Burn Barrel Under Toilet Seat
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Purpose: Ensure proper placement of burn barrels under toilet seats for safe and effective use.

Materials Required: 55-gallon drums (cut in half), burn barrels, toilet seats, framing lumber, plywood.

Procedure:
1. **Align Barrel with...
Purpose: Ensure proper placement of burn barrels under toilet seats for safe and effective use.

Materials Required: 55-gallon drums (cut in half), burn barrels, toilet seats, framing lumber, plywood.

Procedure:
1. Align Barrel with Seat Hole: Ensure the forward edge of the hole is at least 4–6 inches back from the edge of the bench to prevent splashing or spillage into the compartment.
2. Position Barrel Correctly: The top rim of the barrel should be no more than 2 inches from the underside of the seat (more than 2 inches will result in splashing and spillage into the compartment).
3. Secure Barrel Against Back Stop: Push the barrel all the way back against the back stop to help center it under the hole.
4. Use Runners for Alignment: Use runners to aid in centering the barrel under the hole, preventing spillage during use.
burn barrel placement, latrine setup, sanitation equipment

Environmental Hygiene (6) -

De-Louse the Family Regularly
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Regular de-lousing is essential to prevent disease transmission by lice and fleas.

Steps:
1. De-louse all family members regularly, especially in areas where infestations are common.
2. Use appropriate methods such as combing out lice or...
Regular de-lousing is essential to prevent disease transmission by lice and fleas.

Steps:
1. De-louse all family members regularly, especially in areas where infestations are common.
2. Use appropriate methods such as combing out lice or using insecticides if available.
3. Ensure that dogs and other animals carrying fleas do not come into the house to prevent re-infestation.
4. For more information on de-lousing, refer to page 200 of this book.
lice removal, de-lousing, flea prevention
Disinfect and Clean Bedding to Prevent Infestations
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Regularly clean and disinfect bedding to prevent infestation by bedbugs and lice.

Steps:
1. Hang or spread sheets and blankets in the sun often for cleaning.
2. If there are bedbugs, pour boiling water on cots and wash all sheets and...
Regularly clean and disinfect bedding to prevent infestation by bedbugs and lice.

Steps:
1. Hang or spread sheets and blankets in the sun often for cleaning.
2. If there are bedbugs, pour boiling water on cots and wash all sheets and blankets on the same day.
3. Ensure that all bedding is washed thoroughly with soap and clean water.
4. For more information on lice removal, refer to page 200 of this book.
bedbug removal, lice prevention, bedding hygiene
Maintain Cleanliness in the Home and Surroundings
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Keep your home clean to prevent the spread of disease and pests.

Steps:
1. Clean house often by sweeping and washing floors, walls, and beneath furniture.
2. Fill in cracks and holes in the floor or walls where roaches, bedbugs, and...
Keep your home clean to prevent the spread of disease and pests.

Steps:
1. Clean house often by sweeping and washing floors, walls, and beneath furniture.
2. Fill in cracks and holes in the floor or walls where roaches, bedbugs, and scorpions can hide.
3. Regularly inspect for signs of pests such as droppings, bites, or infestations.
4. Use safe methods to eliminate pests, such as insecticides or natural remedies.
home cleanliness, pest control, disease prevention
Maintain Sanitation Around the Home and Latrines
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Keep the home and surrounding areas clean to prevent disease spread.

Steps:
1. Clean up feces immediately if children or animals have a bowel movement near the house.
2. Teach children to use latrines or at least to go farther from the...
Keep the home and surrounding areas clean to prevent disease spread.

Steps:
1. Clean up feces immediately if children or animals have a bowel movement near the house.
2. Teach children to use latrines or at least to go farther from the house when defecating.
3. Ensure that all family members understand the importance of proper waste disposal.
waste management, sanitation, disease prevention
Prevent Disease Spread by Avoiding Spitting and Covering Coughs/Sneezes
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Avoid spitting on the floor and cover your mouth when coughing or sneezing to prevent disease spread.

Steps:
1. Do not spit on the floor, as it can spread disease.
2. When you cough or sneeze, cover your mouth with a hand, cloth, or...
Avoid spitting on the floor and cover your mouth when coughing or sneezing to prevent disease spread.

Steps:
1. Do not spit on the floor, as it can spread disease.
2. When you cough or sneeze, cover your mouth with a hand, cloth, or handkerchief.
3. Wash your hands immediately after covering your mouth to prevent the spread of germs.
cough etiquette, disease prevention, hygiene
Prevent Disease Spread by Keeping Animals Out of the Home
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Keep animals out of the home to prevent disease transmission.

Steps:
1. Do not allow pigs or other animals into the house or places where children play.
2. Prevent dogs from licking children or climbing on beds, as they can spread...
Keep animals out of the home to prevent disease transmission.

Steps:
1. Do not allow pigs or other animals into the house or places where children play.
2. Prevent dogs from licking children or climbing on beds, as they can spread diseases.
3. Ensure that pets and livestock are kept away from living areas to reduce exposure to pathogens.
animal control, disease prevention, home hygiene

Decontamination (6) -

Decontaminate Skin Using M258A1 Skin Decon Kit
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Immediately decontaminate any exposed skin if contamination occurs.
2. Use the M258A1 skin decon kit, which contains three sets of foil-packaged decontaminating wipes that neutralize most nerve and blister agents.
3....
Procedure:

1. Immediately decontaminate any exposed skin if contamination occurs.
2. Use the M258A1 skin decon kit, which contains three sets of foil-packaged decontaminating wipes that neutralize most nerve and blister agents.
3. Attach the kit to the protective mask carrier or LCE (Load Carrying Equipment) for easy access during operations.
4. Store the kit in a location protected from temperatures above 110°F (43°C) and below 32°F (0°C) to maintain its effectiveness.
5. Follow step-by-step procedures provided in *The Soldier's Manual of Common Tasks* for proper use of the kit.
6. Refer to TM 3-4240-216-10 for details on maintenance and care of the kit.
M258A1, skin decon, nerve agent, blister agent
Use M291 Skin Decontamination Kit
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Immediately decontaminate any exposed skin if contamination occurs.
2. Use the M291 skin decon kit, which consists of six individual packets containing reactive resin for decontaminating hands, face, ears, and neck.
3....
Procedure:

1. Immediately decontaminate any exposed skin if contamination occurs.
2. Use the M291 skin decon kit, which consists of six individual packets containing reactive resin for decontaminating hands, face, ears, and neck.
3. Store the kit in the large cargo pockets of the BDO (Body Armor System) trouser for easy access during operations.
4. Ensure that the M291 is used in temperatures ranging from -50°F to 120°F, as it is designed to function within this range.
5. Follow procedures similar to those of the M258A1 kit for proper use and decontamination.
M291, skin decon, reactive resin, temperature range
Conduct Environmental Remediation and Decontamination After a Toxic Gas Release
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Assess the extent of contamination: Conduct thorough surveys to determine the areas affected by toxic gas exposure, including soil, water, air, and surfaces.
2. Implement containment measures: Use barriers or other methods...
Procedure:

1. Assess the extent of contamination: Conduct thorough surveys to determine the areas affected by toxic gas exposure, including soil, water, air, and surfaces.
2. Implement containment measures: Use barriers or other methods to prevent further spread of contaminants into unaffected areas.
3. Begin decontamination operations: Apply appropriate cleaning agents and techniques to remove hazardous substances from contaminated environments, following established safety protocols.
4. Dispose of contaminated waste safely: Ensure that all materials removed during the cleanup process are disposed of in accordance with environmental regulations and health standards.
5. Monitor air and water quality post-remediation: Continuously test for residual contamination to ensure that the environment is safe for reoccupation or use.
6. Engage independent experts if necessary: Consult with environmental scientists or toxicologists to verify the effectiveness of decontamination efforts and provide guidance on long-term monitoring.
7. Document all remediation activities: Keep detailed records of all actions taken during the cleanup process, including dates, methods used, and results of testing.
environmental remediation, decontamination, toxic gas, waste disposal, air quality monitoring
Implement Contamination Control During Relocation of Non-Evacuated Populations
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Objective: Provide monitoring and decontamination services to individuals relocated from areas not previously evacuated.

Procedure:
1. Identify populations who are being relocated from areas that were not previously evacuated.
2. Use...
Objective: Provide monitoring and decontamination services to individuals relocated from areas not previously evacuated.

Procedure:
1. Identify populations who are being relocated from areas that were not previously evacuated.
2. Use existing reception centers or buffer zone monitoring/decontamination stations for these individuals if available.
3. Implement monitoring and decontamination protocols consistent with those used at reception centers and emergency worker stations.
4. Ensure resources are allocated to support continued use of these facilities during the relocation process.
5. Refer to evaluation criterion M.1.b for additional guidance on contamination control considerations.

Notes:
• Using buffer zone stations helps reduce the risk of spreading contamination outside the relocation area.
• All individuals must be monitored and decontaminated before leaving the buffer zone.
relocation, contamination control, non-evacuated populations, decontamination, buffer zone
Set Up Monitoring and Decontamination Stations at Egress Points
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Objective: Ensure proper monitoring and decontamination of individuals exiting relocation areas.

Procedure:
1. Establish monitoring and decontamination stations at points of egress in the buffer zone around relocation areas.
2. Ensure...
Objective: Ensure proper monitoring and decontamination of individuals exiting relocation areas.

Procedure:
1. Establish monitoring and decontamination stations at points of egress in the buffer zone around relocation areas.
2. Ensure these stations are equipped with necessary resources, supplies, and radiation protective equipment for workers.
3. Implement procedures to monitor and decontaminate individuals exiting the relocation area.
4. Coordinate with existing reception centers or monitoring/decontamination stations already established during the emergency phase of an incident.
5. Use buffer zone stations when available to reduce contamination spread.

Notes:
• Decontamination protocols must be consistent with those used at reception centers and emergency worker stations.
• Stations should be clearly marked and accessible for all exiting individuals.
decontamination, egress points, buffer zone, monitoring stations, relocation areas
Disposal of Contaminated Equipment and Clothing
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Store contaminated equipment and clothing out of the way of entering soldiers to avoid confusion and contamination spread.
2. Choose a location under the shelter or van if possible for storage.
3. Later, when possible, take...
Procedure:

1. Store contaminated equipment and clothing out of the way of entering soldiers to avoid confusion and contamination spread.
2. Choose a location under the shelter or van if possible for storage.
3. Later, when possible, take contaminated items to equipment decon stations for cleaning and possible reissue.
disposal procedures, contaminated equipment, decontamination, storage of contaminants

Autoclaving (6) -

Cool Down a Pressure Cooker After Autoclaving
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Cooling Off Procedure:

1. The cooling time depends on the load inside the pressure cooker.
2. Glass items may shatter during cooling and require extra caution.
3. Culture media can boil and spatter, requiring several hours of...
Cooling Off Procedure:

1. The cooling time depends on the load inside the pressure cooker.
2. Glass items may shatter during cooling and require extra caution.
3. Culture media can boil and spatter, requiring several hours of cooling time.
4. For other items, allow at least 30 minutes for the pressure cooker to cool.
5. To speed up cooling, you can run cold water over it; however, this increases the risk of shattering if glass is inside.
cooling pressure cooker, autoclave, safety, glass items
Determine Autoclaving Time, Pressure, and Altitude Adjustments
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Time, Pressure, and Altitude Considerations:

1. Do not begin timing until the pressure cooker is at full steam.
2. At sea level, run the autoclave at 121°C (250°F) for 30 minutes at 15 pounds of pressure.
3. For every **500 feet...
Time, Pressure, and Altitude Considerations:

1. Do not begin timing until the pressure cooker is at full steam.
2. At sea level, run the autoclave at 121°C (250°F) for 30 minutes at 15 pounds of pressure.
3. For every 500 feet gain in elevation, add 5 minutes to the timing.
4. If cooking textiles, increase time to 40 minutes.
5. Time and temperature requirements may vary for culture media; consult the information that came with the media for specifics, as some components can be damaged by over-autoclaving.
autoclave time, pressure, altitude adjustment, sterilization
Dry Autoclaved Packs After Cooling
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Drying Procedure:

• After the pressure cooker has cooled, place packs in a warm place on a rack to dry.
• This helps prevent contamination and maintains sterility.
drying autoclaved packs, sterilization, storage
Maintain and Clean a Pressure Cooker for Autoclaving
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Steps to Maintain and Clean a Pressure Cooker:

1. Clean after each use with distilled water or fresh rainwater.
2. Do not use detergents during cleaning.
3. Inspect the gasket, pressure vent, gauge, and other components...
Steps to Maintain and Clean a Pressure Cooker:

1. Clean after each use with distilled water or fresh rainwater.
2. Do not use detergents during cleaning.
3. Inspect the gasket, pressure vent, gauge, and other components regularly.
4. Consult the manual that comes with your pressure cooker for specific maintenance instructions.
5. The manual is crucial as different models may vary in operation, inspection requirements, and parts.
6. Ensure you have spare parts, including a spare gasket (or two) and safety plug.

Important Information from the Manual:
• Time variations for sterilization
• How much water to add
• How to determine when it is safe to open the pressure cooker
pressure cooker, autoclave, cleaning, maintenance, gasket, manual, sterilization
Operate a Pressure Cooker as an Autoclave
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Operating Steps for Autoclaving:

1. Bring the pressure cooker to full boil with the weight off or valve open.
2. When it reaches a full boil (e.g., when it whistles like a tea kettle), add weight or close the valve, and **only then...
Operating Steps for Autoclaving:

1. Bring the pressure cooker to full boil with the weight off or valve open.
2. When it reaches a full boil (e.g., when it whistles like a tea kettle), add weight or close the valve, and only then begin timing.
3. This step is crucial to evacuate all air and replace it with steam; otherwise, the required temperature will not be reached, allowing hostile organisms to survive.

Testing for Air Evacuation:
• Attach a rubber tube to the vent with the other end underwater.
• When bubbles stop coming up, all air has been evacuated.
• Record this time and use it in future operations as the required 'warm-up' time before timing begins.
operate pressure cooker, autoclave, air evacuation, sterilization
Use TST Test Tape for Autoclaving Verification
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
TST Test Tape Usage:

• TST test tape is an autoclave indicator tape that changes color when a load has been sterilized.
• It has a shelf life of 2 years from the date of manufacture for reliable results.
• Use this tape to verify...
TST Test Tape Usage:

• TST test tape is an autoclave indicator tape that changes color when a load has been sterilized.
• It has a shelf life of 2 years from the date of manufacture for reliable results.
• Use this tape to verify whether your pressure cooker has achieved proper sterilization.
TST test tape, autoclave indicator, sterilization verification

Stain Removal (5) -

Removing Fruit Spots from Fabrics
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To remove fruit spots:

1. Moisten the Spot: Let the spotted part of the cloth absorb a little water without dipping it.
2. Apply Sulphurous Acid Gas: Hold the spot over a lighted common brimstone match at a proper distance to allow...
To remove fruit spots:

1. Moisten the Spot: Let the spotted part of the cloth absorb a little water without dipping it.
2. Apply Sulphurous Acid Gas: Hold the spot over a lighted common brimstone match at a proper distance to allow sulphurous acid gas to act on the stain.
3. Alternative Method with Chlorine Water: Wet the spot with chlorine water for removal.

Note: See page 436 of the source text for further details.
fruit spots,removal,sulphurous acid gas,chlorine water
Removing Grease Spots from Cloths
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To remove grease spots from cloths:

1. Use Diluted Potash: Apply a diluted solution of potash, but use it cautiously to avoid damaging the cloth.
2. Alternative Method with Blotting Paper and Iron:
- Lay a piece of brown or blotting...
To remove grease spots from cloths:

1. Use Diluted Potash: Apply a diluted solution of potash, but use it cautiously to avoid damaging the cloth.
2. Alternative Method with Blotting Paper and Iron:
- Lay a piece of brown or blotting paper over the spot.
- Pass a hot iron over the paper to absorb the grease.
3. Remove Wax Stains: Use spirits of turpentine, sulphuric ether, or benzine for removing white wax stains from clothes.
4. White Paint Removal: The same agents (spirits of turpentine, sulphuric ether, or benzine) can also remove marks of white paint.
grease spots,cloth,removal,potash,blotting paper,hot iron,turpentine,benzine
Removing Ink Stains from Fabrics
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To remove ink stains:

1. Immediate Action: As soon as the stain occurs, wet the area with juice of sorrel or lemon, vinegar, or hard white soap.
2. Use Oxalic Acid (with Caution): A weak solution of oxalic acid is more effective but must be...
To remove ink stains:

1. Immediate Action: As soon as the stain occurs, wet the area with juice of sorrel or lemon, vinegar, or hard white soap.
2. Use Oxalic Acid (with Caution): A weak solution of oxalic acid is more effective but must be used carefully to avoid damage.
ink stains,removal,sorrel,lemon,vinegar,oxalic acid
Removing Mildew from Linen
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To remove mildew from linen:

1. Apply Soap: Rub the affected area with soap.
2. Use Fine Chalk: Scrape some fine chalk and rub it into the linen.
3. Lay on Grass: Place the treated linen on the grass.
4. Wet as It Dries: As it...
To remove mildew from linen:

1. Apply Soap: Rub the affected area with soap.
2. Use Fine Chalk: Scrape some fine chalk and rub it into the linen.
3. Lay on Grass: Place the treated linen on the grass.
4. Wet as It Dries: As it dries, wet it slightly to help remove the mildew.
5. Repeat if Necessary: Repeat the process twice for complete removal.

Note: See page 437 of the source text for further details.
mildew,linen,cleaning,chalk,soap
Removing Stains from Cloth or Silk
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To remove stains from cloth or silk:

1. Prepare Paste: Pound French chalk fine and mix it with lavender-water to create a paste of the thickness of mustard.
2. Apply to Stain: Put the paste on the stain and rub it softly with your finger or...
To remove stains from cloth or silk:

1. Prepare Paste: Pound French chalk fine and mix it with lavender-water to create a paste of the thickness of mustard.
2. Apply to Stain: Put the paste on the stain and rub it softly with your finger or palm of the hand.
3. Cover with Paper: Place blotting paper and brown paper over the top of the stain.
4. Iron Over: Smooth the papers with an iron that is milkwarm.
cloth,silk,stains,French chalk,lapdver-water,iron

Food Establishment Sanitation (5) -

Identify 20 Aspects of General Sanitary Requirements for Food Establishments
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Objective: Identify 20 aspects of general sanitary requirements for food establishments and relate them to sanitary inspections at installation facilities/activities.

Procedure:
1. Review the section in Lesson 1 that lists the 20 aspects of...
Objective: Identify 20 aspects of general sanitary requirements for food establishments and relate them to sanitary inspections at installation facilities/activities.

Procedure:
1. Review the section in Lesson 1 that lists the 20 aspects of general sanitary requirements.
2. Document each aspect, which may include:
- Handwashing procedures
- Food storage temperatures
- Cleaning and sanitizing equipment
- Pest control measures
- Employee health monitoring
- Waste disposal protocols
- Cross-contamination prevention
- Proper food labeling
- Use of approved cleaning agents
- Safe water supply management
- Equipment maintenance standards
- Food handling practices
- Personal hygiene requirements
- Sanitation training for staff
- Inspection records retention
- Corrective action procedures
- Compliance with local and federal regulations
- Food safety audits
- Emergency sanitation protocols
- Documentation of all sanitary activities.
3. Relate each aspect to how it is evaluated during sanitary inspections at installation facilities/activities.

Details:
• These aspects are used as criteria for evaluating food establishments during inspections.
• Each requirement must be met to ensure the establishment is deemed sanitarily acceptable.
sanitary requirements, food establishment compliance, military inspections, sanitation standards
Identify Improper Sanitary Practices in the Handling of Subsistence in a Food Establishment
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Objective: Identify improper sanitary practices in the handling of subsistence in a food establishment.

Procedure:
1. Review Lesson 1 to identify examples of improper sanitary practices as outlined in the text.
2. Document each practice,...
Objective: Identify improper sanitary practices in the handling of subsistence in a food establishment.

Procedure:
1. Review Lesson 1 to identify examples of improper sanitary practices as outlined in the text.
2. Document each practice, which may include:
- Not washing hands before handling food
- Using contaminated utensils or equipment
- Storing food at unsafe temperatures
- Cross-contamination between raw and cooked foods
- Failure to clean and sanitize surfaces regularly
- Improper disposal of waste materials
- Use of unapproved cleaning agents
- Lack of employee health monitoring
- Not following proper food labeling procedures
- Inadequate pest control measures.
3. Understand the consequences of these practices on food safety and compliance with military standards.

Details:
• These improper practices are typically identified during sanitary inspections.
• Corrective actions must be taken to address each issue.
improper sanitary practices, food handling, military standards, sanitation compliance
Identify Five Sources of Sanitary Requirements for Establishments Furnishing Foods to the Armed Forces
Reference Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Objective: Identify five sources of sanitary requirements for establishments furnishing foods to the Armed Forces.

Procedure:
1. Locate and review the section in Lesson 1 that discusses the five sources of sanitary requirements.
2. Identify...
Objective: Identify five sources of sanitary requirements for establishments furnishing foods to the Armed Forces.

Procedure:
1. Locate and review the section in Lesson 1 that discusses the five sources of sanitary requirements.
2. Identify each source, which may include:
- Federal regulations (e.g., FDA guidelines)
- Department of Defense directives (e.g., DOD 4145.19-R-1)
- Military health service policies
- Local and state health codes
- International standards applicable to military operations.
3. Understand the role each source plays in ensuring food safety for military personnel.

Details:
• These sources are critical for compliance during inspections and audits.
• Ensure that all identified sources are current and relevant.
sanitary requirements, food establishments, military standards, FDA guidelines, DOD directives
Identify the Four Types of Sanitary Inspections and Their Purposes
Reference Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Objective: Identify the four types of sanitary inspections and the purpose of each.

Procedure:
1. Review Lesson 1 to identify the four types of sanitary inspections discussed in the text.
2. For each inspection type, determine its purpose...
Objective: Identify the four types of sanitary inspections and the purpose of each.

Procedure:
1. Review Lesson 1 to identify the four types of sanitary inspections discussed in the text.
2. For each inspection type, determine its purpose as outlined:
- Routine inspections: To ensure ongoing compliance with sanitation standards.
- Special inspections: Conducted following complaints or incidents.
- Pre-opening inspections: Required before a food establishment begins operations.
- Follow-up inspections: Performed after corrective actions have been taken to verify compliance.
3. Understand the frequency and scope of each inspection type.

Details:
• These inspections are conducted by military veterinary personnel or other designated authorities.
• Each inspection has specific documentation requirements.
sanitary inspections, food establishment compliance, military inspections, routine inspections, pre-opening inspections
Identify Terms Common to Food Establishment Sanitation
Reference Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Objective: Identify terms common to food establishment sanitation.

Procedure:
1. Review the terminology related to food establishment sanitation as outlined in Lesson 1 of *US Army Storage And Sanitation*.
2. Understand key definitions such...
Objective: Identify terms common to food establishment sanitation.

Procedure:
1. Review the terminology related to food establishment sanitation as outlined in Lesson 1 of *US Army Storage And Sanitation*.
2. Understand key definitions such as 'sanitary inspection,' 'wholesome foods,' and 'sanitarily acceptable establishments.'
3. Ensure familiarity with terms used in military veterinary inspections and food handling protocols.

Details:
• This step is foundational for understanding the rest of the lesson.
• Terms are defined within the context of military food establishment standards.
food sanitation, terminology, military food establishments, sanitary inspection

Food Service Cleaning (5) -

Cleaning and Sanitizing Delicatessen Areas
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
### Cleaning and Sanitizing Delicatessen Areas

Procedure:
1. Follow the general requirements for cleaning and sanitizing as specified for other areas.
2. Clean and sanitize food contact surfaces after every use, after interruption of service,...
### Cleaning and Sanitizing Delicatessen Areas

Procedure:
1. Follow the general requirements for cleaning and sanitizing as specified for other areas.
2. Clean and sanitize food contact surfaces after every use, after interruption of service, and at regularly scheduled intervals throughout the day if in continuous use.
3. Display cases must be:
- Emptied
- Washed
- Rinsed
- Sanitized
- Air-dried on a daily basis.
4. If product is placed on multi-service trays, then only the trays need cleaning and sanitizing on a daily basis. The display case may be cleaned weekly or more often as necessary.
5. Ensure that all equipment used for raw and cooked products (or biologically different items) is cleaned and sanitized in between uses. This includes:
- Tools
- Utensils
- Saws
- Knives
- Tables
- Boards
- Blocks
cleaning,sanitizing,delicatessen,display cases,food contact surfaces
Personal Hygiene and Clothing Standards for Food Handlers
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
### Personal Hygiene and Clothing Standards for Food Handlers

Procedure:
1. Wear clean outer garments suitable for the work being performed.
2. Thoroughly wash hands in an adequate hand washing facility:
- Before beginning work
-...
### Personal Hygiene and Clothing Standards for Food Handlers

Procedure:
1. Wear clean outer garments suitable for the work being performed.
2. Thoroughly wash hands in an adequate hand washing facility:
- Before beginning work
- Upon returning to work after using toilet facilities, eating, smoking, or otherwise soiling their hands.
3. Wear hair restraints such as hair nets, headbands, caps, beard nets, or other effective means to prevent contamination of food or food contact surfaces.
4. Do not store clothing or personal belongings in production areas where food is prepared, stored, or handled.
5. Do not eat food, drink beverages, smoke, or use chewing tobacco in any area where food products or supplies are prepared, stored, or otherwise handled.
6. Maintain gloves used in food handling in an intact, clean, and sanitary condition.
7. Use gloves only for food handling; do not use them for other purposes.
hand washing,hair restraints,clean clothing,gloves,hygiene standards
Personnel Cleanliness and Health Standards in Food Service Areas
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
### Personnel Cleanliness and Health Standards in Food Service Areas

Procedure:
1. Daily inspection of all personnel by commissary officers or immediate supervisors at the start of the work period.
2. Refer any person showing signs of...
### Personnel Cleanliness and Health Standards in Food Service Areas

Procedure:
1. Daily inspection of all personnel by commissary officers or immediate supervisors at the start of the work period.
2. Refer any person showing signs of illness, skin disease, diarrhea (admitted or suspected), infected cuts, or boils to a medical facility for determination of fitness for duty.
3. Conduct pre-employment and periodic medical examinations as required by local medical authority.
4. Persons absent from work due to communicable illnesses (including diarrheal illness) must be referred to a medical facility before resuming work and must have a written clearance signed by a medical officer upon return.
personnel health,medical examination,fitness for duty,cleanliness standards
Separation of Delicatessen Areas and Equipment
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
### Separation of Delicatessen Areas and Equipment

Procedure:
1. Completely separate the delicatessen preparation area, cooler areas, or display areas from the remainder of operations in terms of traffic of materials and personnel.
2....
### Separation of Delicatessen Areas and Equipment

Procedure:
1. Completely separate the delicatessen preparation area, cooler areas, or display areas from the remainder of operations in terms of traffic of materials and personnel.
2. Ensure there is:
- A separate holding cooler for luncheon meats and cooked delicatessen items.
- A separate cutting/trimming/preparation/processing/packaging area for luncheon meats and cooked delicatessen items.
3. Limit raw foods in the delicatessen to vegetables and/or salads sold or served from containers.
4. Do not store or display packaged or nonpackaged raw meats, poultry, or waterfoods in the delicatessen area.
5. Cooked seafood may be displayed in the delicatessen area but must not be displayed with fresh (raw) seafood, which should be in another specified area of the commissary.
6. Do not use the same equipment for raw and cooked products or biologically different items without first cleaning and sanitizing between uses.
separation,delicatessen,equipment,raw foods,cooked foods
Temperature Monitoring in Delicatessen Areas
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
### Temperature Monitoring in Delicatessen Areas

Procedure:
1. Monitor all delicatessen cooking operations, warming tables, holding ovens, and chilling equipment with thermometers to ensure prescribed bacterial killing and...
### Temperature Monitoring in Delicatessen Areas

Procedure:
1. Monitor all delicatessen cooking operations, warming tables, holding ovens, and chilling equipment with thermometers to ensure prescribed bacterial killing and growth prevention temperatures are maintained.
2. Check the temperature of all display cases and storage/freezers:
- Three times each working day
- At least once during each nonworking day
3. Record the following information for each check:
- Date
- Hour
- Temperature
- Checker's initials
4. Do not hold any potentially hazardous or temperature-sensitive perishable product (cooked or raw) at unsafe temperatures for more than 3 hours.
5. The unsafe temperature range is defined as:
- Navy: Between 40°F and 140°F
- Army: Between 45°F and 140°F
temperature monitoring,display cases,safe temperature range,delicatessen

Food Handling (5) -

Clean and Store Food and Dishes Properly Before Leaving the Hut
Standard Source: US Antarctic Continental Field Manual
Procedure:

• Wash and put away any dishes, utensils, and cookware.
• Non-perishable food should be neatly packaged, labeled, and stored in its proper area.
• Take perishable food back to McMurdo Station.
dishwashing, food storage, McMurdo Station
Maintain Mess Facilities and Equipment in the Field
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Mess Facilities and Equipment

To ensure mess facilities are clean and functional:

1. Clean all surfaces regularly, including tables, counters, and floors, using appropriate disinfectants.
2. Inspect equipment for damage or wear, such...
Mess Facilities and Equipment

To ensure mess facilities are clean and functional:

1. Clean all surfaces regularly, including tables, counters, and floors, using appropriate disinfectants.
2. Inspect equipment for damage or wear, such as broken utensils or malfunctioning appliances, and repair or replace as needed.
3. Store food in designated areas to prevent contamination and ensure easy access during meal preparation.
4. Maintain proper ventilation in mess facilities to reduce humidity and the risk of mold growth.
5. Train personnel on the use and maintenance of equipment, such as stoves, ovens, and refrigerators.
6. Keep waste containers clean and emptied regularly to prevent pests and odors.
7. Conduct regular inspections of mess facilities to ensure compliance with hygiene standards.

Warning: Poor maintenance can lead to unsanitary conditions, equipment failure, and the spread of disease.
mess facilities, equipment maintenance, food safety, hygiene standards, sanitation practices
Maintain Sanitary Practices in Food Handling
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Importance of Sanitary Practices in the Handling of Food

Proper sanitary practices are essential to prevent foodborne illness and ensure the health of personnel. Follow these steps:

1. Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water before...
Importance of Sanitary Practices in the Handling of Food

Proper sanitary practices are essential to prevent foodborne illness and ensure the health of personnel. Follow these steps:

1. Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water before handling food, after using the restroom, and after touching contaminated surfaces.
2. Keep food storage areas clean and free from pests by regularly inspecting and cleaning storage containers and shelves.
3. Avoid cross-contamination between raw and cooked foods by using separate cutting boards, utensils, and plates for each type of food.
4. Cook food to the correct internal temperatures:
- Poultry: 165°F (74°C)
- Ground meats: 160°F (71°C)
- Beef, pork, lamb: 145°F (63°C) for medium-rare; 160°F (71°C) for well-done
- Fish: 145°F (63°C)
5. Refrigerate or freeze perishable foods promptly to prevent bacterial growth.
6. Inspect food regularly for signs of spoilage, such as unusual odors, colors, or textures.
7. Train personnel in proper food handling and sanitation procedures to ensure consistency across all operations.

Warning: Failure to follow these practices can lead to severe illness, especially in vulnerable individuals such as children, the elderly, and those with compromised immune systems.
sanitary practices, food handling, cross-contamination, food safety, temperature guidelines
Prepare and Serve Food Safely in the Field
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Preparation and Serving of Food

To ensure food is prepared and served safely:

1. Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water before handling food or utensils.
2. Use clean, sanitized equipment, including cutting boards, knives, and...
Preparation and Serving of Food

To ensure food is prepared and served safely:

1. Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water before handling food or utensils.
2. Use clean, sanitized equipment, including cutting boards, knives, and serving utensils.
3. Cook food to the correct internal temperatures:
- Poultry: 165°F (74°C)
- Ground meats: 160°F (71°C)
- Beef, pork, lamb: 145°F (63°C) for medium-rare; 160°F (71°C) for well-done
- Fish: 145°F (63°C)
4. Avoid cross-contamination by using separate cutting boards and utensils for raw and cooked foods.
5. Serve food promptly, especially perishable items, to prevent bacterial growth.
6. Keep hot foods hot (above 140°F/60°C) and cold foods cold (below 40°F/4°C) during serving.
7. Train personnel in proper cooking and serving techniques to ensure consistency and safety across all operations.

Warning: Undercooking or improper handling can lead to foodborne illness, especially for vulnerable individuals.
food preparation, food safety, cooking temperatures, cross-contamination, serving practices
Proper Food Storage in the Field
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Storage of Food

To ensure food remains safe and edible during storage in the field:

1. Store food in a cool, dry place, away from direct sunlight and sources of heat.
2. Use airtight containers to prevent moisture, pests, and...
Storage of Food

To ensure food remains safe and edible during storage in the field:

1. Store food in a cool, dry place, away from direct sunlight and sources of heat.
2. Use airtight containers to prevent moisture, pests, and contamination.
3. Separate raw and cooked foods into different storage areas or containers to avoid cross-contamination.
4. Label all food items with dates (e.g., “use by” or “best before”) to ensure timely consumption and reduce waste.
5. Keep perishable items refrigerated or frozen, if possible, using portable coolers or freezers.
6. Inspect stored food regularly for signs of spoilage, such as unusual odors, colors, or textures.
7. Control pest infestations by sealing storage areas and using traps or repellents where necessary.

Warning: Improper storage can lead to foodborne illness, waste, and the spread of disease.
food storage, food safety, cross-contamination, pest control, spoilage prevention

Food Inspection (4) -

Use the Directory of Sanitarily Approved Food Establishments for Procurement
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
The Directory of Sanitarily Approved Food Establishments for Armed Forces Procurement is published by HSC and each major overseas command.

Publication Details:
• Published annually and updated quarterly or as required.
• Lists all...
The Directory of Sanitarily Approved Food Establishments for Armed Forces Procurement is published by HSC and each major overseas command.

Publication Details:
• Published annually and updated quarterly or as required.
• Lists all establishments, alphabetically by state or country, that have been inspected for sanitation by the military veterinary service and found acceptable.

Procurement Policy:
• Purchasing activities of the DOD will purchase foods only from establishments listed in the Directory.
• Establishments found acceptable by other government agencies may be exempt from listing in the Directory. These exemptions are detailed in AR 40-657.
Directory of Sanitarily Approved Food Establishments, DOD procurement, food establishment listing
Access and Use Army Regulation 40-657 for Food Inspection Policies
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Army Regulation 40-657, Veterinary/Medical Food Inspections and Laboratory Service, is a multiservice regulation used by the Army, Air Force, Navy, and Marines. This document defines the food inspection mission of the military veterinary...
Army Regulation 40-657, Veterinary/Medical Food Inspections and Laboratory Service, is a multiservice regulation used by the Army, Air Force, Navy, and Marines. This document defines the food inspection mission of the military veterinary services and prescribes general policies and procedures for inspecting food and food establishments that supply all military departments.

Scope of Application:
• Applies to the inspection of food procured for the military services by local or central procurement activities with appropriated or nonappropriated funds.
• Covers all food intended for human consumption.
• Applies to the Army National Guard, US Army Reserve, and all military and civilian establishments, facilities, equipment, and methods used in the manufacturing, processing, storing, issuing, and shipment of food.

Purpose:
• Ensures compliance with sanitary standards for food inspection and establishment approval.
Army Regulation 40-657, food inspection, military veterinary services, sanitation standards
Access the Location List for Military Veterinary Personnel
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
The Location List is published by The Surgeon General, Department of the Army.

Purpose:
• Lists the locations of military veterinary personnel available for inspections and services required for central procurement actions.

**Content...
The Location List is published by The Surgeon General, Department of the Army.

Purpose:
• Lists the locations of military veterinary personnel available for inspections and services required for central procurement actions.

Content Details:
• Includes geographical areas of responsibility for each element of the military veterinary service inside the Continental US (CONUS).
• Shows assignment of inspection responsibilities in foreign countries specifically within the area of responsibility of any overseas command.
Location List, military veterinary personnel, inspection locations
Recognize and Apply Standards from Other Government Agencies
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
The Department of Defense (DOD) recognizes the sanitary standards, inspections, and results of other government agencies such as:
• US Department of Agriculture (USDA)
• US Department of Commerce (USDC)
• **US Public Health Service...
