The Department of Defense (DOD) recognizes the sanitary standards, inspections, and results of other government agencies such as:
• US Department of Agriculture (USDA)
• US Department of Commerce (USDC)
• US Public Health Service (USPHS)

Policy:
If an establishment is found acceptable by one of these agencies, the DOD will accept food items originating in that establishment.

Purpose:
Avoids duplication of inspection systems and ensures consistency with established standards.
DOD, USDA, USDC, USPHS, food establishment approval

Disinfection (4) -

Disinfecting with Bleach Solution
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Prepare a disinfecting solution by mixing 1 part bleach with 9 parts water.
2. Use this solution for:
- Sterilizing tools: Soak instruments in the solution for at least 10 minutes, then rinse thoroughly with clean...
Procedure:

1. Prepare a disinfecting solution by mixing 1 part bleach with 9 parts water.
2. Use this solution for:
- Sterilizing tools: Soak instruments in the solution for at least 10 minutes, then rinse thoroughly with clean water and air dry.
- Washing floors: Mop the floor with the solution, ensuring full coverage. Allow it to sit for 5-10 minutes before rinsing with clean water.
3. Never mix bleach with ammonia as this creates toxic fumes that can be harmful or fatal.
4. Always wear gloves and ensure proper ventilation when using bleach.
bleach,disinfecting solution,sanitation,sterilizing tools,floor cleaning
Produce Bleach for Disinfection
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Materials Required:
• Low voltage power source (e.g., 12 V car battery)
• Carbon conductor (charcoal)
• Water
• Table salt
• Wires with plastic covering removed

Procedure:
1. Set Up Electrodes: Connect the positive electrode to a piece...
Materials Required:
• Low voltage power source (e.g., 12 V car battery)
• Carbon conductor (charcoal)
• Water
• Table salt
• Wires with plastic covering removed

Procedure:
1. Set Up Electrodes: Connect the positive electrode to a piece of carbon (or charcoal) and place the negative wire (with the plastic covering removed) into a container filled with water.
2. Add Salt: Add a small amount of table salt to the water solution.
3. Apply Current: Apply electrical current from the power source to the setup. This will cause chlorine gas to be produced, which can be collected and used as bleach.
4. Collect Bleach: Collect the resulting chlorine solution in a sealed container for use as a disinfectant.

Uses:
• Disinfect water
• Clean surfaces
• Sterilize medical instruments

Warnings/Notes:
• Handle with care, as chlorine gas is toxic if inhaled in large quantities.
• Ensure proper ventilation during the process.
bleach production, chlorine solution, disinfectant, survival sanitation, water purification
Using Pratts Poultry Disinfectant for Sanitation
Standard Source: Practical Pointers On The Care of Lifestock
Procedure:
1. Use Pratts Poultry Disinfectant to maintain a clean and disease-free environment.
2. Dilute one gallon of the disinfectant with water to make fifty gallons of solution.
3. Spray this solution on poultry houses, furnishings, feed...
Procedure:
1. Use Pratts Poultry Disinfectant to maintain a clean and disease-free environment.
2. Dilute one gallon of the disinfectant with water to make fifty gallons of solution.
3. Spray this solution on poultry houses, furnishings, feed containers, and water containers at frequent intervals.
4. This helps eliminate germs and prevent the spread of diseases among birds.
5. Regular use ensures a healthier and more productive flock.
disinfectant, poultry sanitation, disease control
Disinfecting Contaminated Surfaces And Objects With Bleach
Standard Source: Water Treatment
Overview: Chlorine bleach is an effective disinfectant for cleaning contaminated surfaces and objects.

Procedure:
1. Preparation: Wipe the surface with a towel to remove any visible dirt, fluids, or debris.
2. Cleaning: Clean the...
Overview: Chlorine bleach is an effective disinfectant for cleaning contaminated surfaces and objects.

Procedure:
1. Preparation: Wipe the surface with a towel to remove any visible dirt, fluids, or debris.
2. Cleaning: Clean the area with soap and water to remove gross contamination.
3. Application Of Bleach Solution: Spray or wipe the appropriate bleach solution onto the entire contaminated surface using a sponge, towel, or spray bottle.
4. Contact Time: Allow the bleach solution to sit undisturbed on the surface for 30 minutes to ensure complete disinfection.
5. Rinsing And Wiping: After the contact time has elapsed, wipe off the remaining solution with a moist towel or rinse it off with clean water.
6. Final Inspection: Ensure that the area is thoroughly cleaned and free of contamination.
bleach disinfection, surface cleaning, sanitation, decontamination

Decontamination Procedures (4) -

Implement Patient Decontamination and Triage for BW Exposure
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Patient decontamination and triage is critical in managing biological warfare (BW) casualties to prevent further contamination of medical personnel and other patients.

• Key Considerations:
- Decentralization is necessary; casualties...
Patient decontamination and triage is critical in managing biological warfare (BW) casualties to prevent further contamination of medical personnel and other patients.

• Key Considerations:
- Decentralization is necessary; casualties must not be forced to wait at a central point for decontamination.
- All medical units should have comparable sets of medical items and decontamination equipment for treatment of contaminated patients originating in their area.

Action Steps:
1. Decontaminate patients immediately upon arrival at the medical unit to prevent further contamination.
2. Use standard nursing management techniques, including universal infectious disease precautions (e.g., impermeable surgical gowns, masks, face shields, gloves).
3. Ensure that all materials soiled by fluids from contaminated patients are clearly identified as hazardous and handled appropriately.
4. Implement barrier nursing techniques to prevent secondary contamination.
patient decontamination,bw exposure,triage,universal precautions
Perform Initial Decontamination for BW Exposure
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Initial decontamination is critical to reduce the risk of further contamination and disease transmission after exposure to a biological warfare (BW) agent.

• Decontamination Steps:
- Remove outer clothing and shoes as soon as possible....
Initial decontamination is critical to reduce the risk of further contamination and disease transmission after exposure to a biological warfare (BW) agent.

• Decontamination Steps:
- Remove outer clothing and shoes as soon as possible. This can usually be done before admission without interfering with medical treatment.
- If available, use 0.5% hypochlorite for decontamination of dermal exposure. Otherwise, use soap and water.
- Avoid contact with mucous membranes (eyes, mouth, nose, genitals) or open cuts during the process.
- Shower and change to clean clothing as soon as possible.

Action Steps:
1. Remove contaminated outerwear immediately in a designated area away from non-contaminated patients.
2. Wash exposed skin with soap and water or 0.5% hypochlorite solution if available.
3. Use an NBC protective mask to avoid further exposure.
4. Ensure that potentially contaminated clothing is handled by protected personnel.
decontamination,bw exposure,soap and water,hypochlorite
Cleanup and Decontamination Following a Fire or Explosion at a COMAH Site
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Assess the Area:
- Conduct a thorough assessment of the affected area to identify hazardous materials, debris, and potential contamination.
- Use specialized equipment and trained personnel for safety and accuracy.

2....
Procedure:

1. Assess the Area:
- Conduct a thorough assessment of the affected area to identify hazardous materials, debris, and potential contamination.
- Use specialized equipment and trained personnel for safety and accuracy.

2. Secure the Site:
- Establish secure boundaries around the site to prevent unauthorized access.
- Post warning signs and use barriers or fencing as necessary.

3. Cleanup Operations:
- Remove rubble, debris, and any hazardous materials from the site.
- Use appropriate tools and equipment designed for safe cleanup of industrial sites.
- Ensure that all waste is disposed of in accordance with local regulations.

4. Decontamination Process:
- Decontaminate affected areas using approved methods to remove chemical, biological, or radiological contaminants.
- Monitor air and water quality during the decontamination process to ensure safety for workers and nearby communities.

5. Post-Cleanup Inspection:
- Conduct a final inspection of the site to confirm that all hazards have been removed.
- Ensure that the area is safe for future use or reoccupation.

Important Notes:
• All cleanup personnel must be trained in handling hazardous materials and wear appropriate protective gear.
• Decontamination should follow guidelines set by health and safety authorities.
cleanup, decontamination, COMAH site, fire explosion, hazardous materials
Perform Secondary Decontamination for Medical Personnel
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Secondary decontamination is necessary to prevent contamination of medical personnel from biological warfare (BW) agents on the clothing or equipment of exposed soldiers.

• Decontamination Steps:
- Use an appropriately diluted sodium...
Secondary decontamination is necessary to prevent contamination of medical personnel from biological warfare (BW) agents on the clothing or equipment of exposed soldiers.

• Decontamination Steps:
- Use an appropriately diluted sodium hypochlorite solution (0.5%) or copious quantities of plain soap and water for cleansing exposed areas.
- Avoid using stronger solutions (e.g., 5.0% chlorine) on skin, masks, splints, or to irrigate wounds; it is only suitable for gloves and equipment.

Action Steps:
1. Ensure that all health care providers wear appropriate protective gear during handling of exposed casualties until decontamination is complete.
2. Use 0.5% sodium hypochlorite solution or soap and water to cleanse contaminated areas promptly after exposure.
3. Follow any agent-specific guidance if available.
secondary decontamination,medical personnel,bw agents,hypochlorite

Water Testing (4) -

Monitoring Water for Chemical Contamination
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Use water testing kits to detect the following agents: mustard, nerve agents (at 0.05 ppm), arsenic, antimony, cyanogen agents, and heavy metals (lead, copper, mercury).
2. Test for acidity: Water with a pH less than 3 may...
Procedure:

1. Use water testing kits to detect the following agents: mustard, nerve agents (at 0.05 ppm), arsenic, antimony, cyanogen agents, and heavy metals (lead, copper, mercury).
2. Test for acidity: Water with a pH less than 3 may indicate contamination with mustard. Free chlorine can destroy mustard within 30 minutes but may interfere with testing if present in excess of 5 ppm. Reduce chlorine levels using thiosulphate.
3. Note that water testing kits will not detect mental incapacitants, biological agents, or nuclear fallout.
water testing, chemical agents, pH levels, contamination detection
Measure Residual Chlorine in Drinking Water Using WataBlue Reagent Kit
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
Procedure:

1. Prepare the water sample: Take 5 mL of the drinking water sample from the container.
2. Transfer to test-tube: Transfer the 5 mL sample into the provided test-tube.
3. Add reagent: Add 1 drop of WataBlue liquid reagent...
Procedure:

1. Prepare the water sample: Take 5 mL of the drinking water sample from the container.
2. Transfer to test-tube: Transfer the 5 mL sample into the provided test-tube.
3. Add reagent: Add 1 drop of WataBlue liquid reagent using the plastic pipette (3 ml) specifically designed for this purpose.
4. Seal and shake: Seal the test-tube and shake it well to ensure thorough mixing of the sample and reagent.
5. Wait 30 minutes: Allow the mixture to sit for 30 minutes after chlorination.
6. Interpret results:
- If the sample remains colourless, double the dose of chlorine in the water, wait another 30 minutes, and repeat from step 1 (residual chlorine <0.5 ppm).
- If the sample turns light blue, you have safe drinking water (residual chlorine between 0.5-1 ppm).
- If the sample turns dark blue, reduce the chlorine dose by half, wait another 30 minutes, and repeat from step 1 (residual chlorine >1 ppm).
7. Rinse test-tube: Rinse the test-tube thoroughly between tests to avoid cross-contamination.

Storage instructions for WataBlue reagent:
• Keep the reagent away from light and at room temperature (25°C).
• Close the flask tightly after each use to prevent oxidation of the liquid.
• Check the expiry date on the bottle. Note that over time, the reagent may turn red or brown; this does not affect the quality of the test.

Safety note: The WataBlue reagent is non-hazardous.
residual chlorine, water testing, WataBlue kit, sanitation, safe drinking water
Recognize the Importance of Water Quality Testing
Reference Source: Safe Water Manual
Procedure for Recognizing the Value of Water Quality Testing:

1. Understand the purpose: A water quality test is a useful tool to detect invisible contaminants in drinking water, such as bacteria and other harmful organisms.

2. **Consider...
Procedure for Recognizing the Value of Water Quality Testing:

1. Understand the purpose: A water quality test is a useful tool to detect invisible contaminants in drinking water, such as bacteria and other harmful organisms.

2. Consider the impact: The presence of *E. coli* bacteria indicates recent faecal contamination, which can pose serious health risks if consumed.

3. Use results for decision-making: Test results help determine whether water is safe to drink or requires further treatment before consumption.
water quality test, E. coli, sanitation, health risks, water safety
Testing Chlorine Solution Concentration with WataTest
Reference Source: Safe Water Manual
### Procedure: Testing Chlorine Solution Concentration with WataTest

1. Prepare the Sample:
• Mix well the concentrated chlorine solution you want to measure.
• Using a clean and dry syringe, take exactly 2 mL of the chlorine...
### Procedure: Testing Chlorine Solution Concentration with WataTest

1. Prepare the Sample:
• Mix well the concentrated chlorine solution you want to measure.
• Using a clean and dry syringe, take exactly 2 mL of the chlorine solution and place it in a cup or small recipient.

2. Prepare WataTest Reagent:
• Shake well the WataTest flask.
• Fill the syringe with WataTest reagent and be prepared to count the drops you use. Close the flask immediately.

3. Conduct the Test:
• Add one drop of WataTest reagent to the recipient containing the chlorine solution, and mix gently.
• If after a few seconds, the contents remain transparent, add another drop of WataTest reagent and continue until the sample turns dark in color after mixing.

4. Calculate Chlorine Content:
• Chlorine content (in g/L) = number of WataTest drops divided by two.
- Example: If you used 12 drops, the chlorine concentration is 6 g/L.
• Any strength above 6g/L is fine. If the test shows a strength below 6g/L, then change the instructions for diluting the chlorine. Check the details in your user guide for active chlorine.

5. Storage and Handling:
• Keep WataTest away from light and at room temperature (25°C).
• Close tightly the flask after each use to avoid liquid oxidation.
• Beware of the expiry date.

6. Safety Note:
• This reagent is non-hazardous.
WataTest, chlorine concentration test, water testing, sanitation, active chlorine

Latrines Toilets (4) -

Construct a Latrine in the Field
Standard Source: FM 21 10 Field Hygiene and Sanitation
Steps to Construct a Latrine in the Field:

1. Choose a location that is at least 50 feet away from water sources, campsites, and food preparation areas.
2. Dig a hole approximately 6-8 inches deep and 12-18 inches wide for each user...
Steps to Construct a Latrine in the Field:

1. Choose a location that is at least 50 feet away from water sources, campsites, and food preparation areas.
2. Dig a hole approximately 6-8 inches deep and 12-18 inches wide for each user (one hole per person).
3. Line the hole with a tarp or plastic sheet to prevent contamination of soil and groundwater.
4. Use a separate pit for feces, urine, and waste materials if possible.
5. Cover the waste with at least 6 inches of dirt after each use to reduce odor and deter animals.
6. Construct a simple seat or platform using wood or stone to elevate the user above the ground.
7. Use a separate container for handwashing near the latrine, filled with clean water and soap.
8. Mark the location clearly with signs or flags to prevent accidental use by others.
9. Maintain the latrine regularly, ensuring that it is not overflowing and remains functional.
10. Dispose of waste materials properly after use, following local regulations if applicable.

Additional Tips:
• Use a shovel or other digging tool for excavation.
• Ensure that the latrine is accessible to all users but located in a discreet area.
• If possible, construct multiple pits and rotate their use to allow time for waste decomposition.

Warnings:
• Do not dig near water sources or campsites.
• Avoid using contaminated soil or materials for covering waste.
• Ensure that the latrine is used only by designated individuals.
latrine, field sanitation, toilet construction, waste management, hygiene practices
Building the Fly-Killing VIP Latrine
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Select a suitable location for the latrine that is at least 30 meters away from water sources and living areas.
2. Dig a pit approximately 1.5 to 2 meters deep, depending on the number of users.
3. **Construct a platform...
Procedure:

1. Select a suitable location for the latrine that is at least 30 meters away from water sources and living areas.
2. Dig a pit approximately 1.5 to 2 meters deep, depending on the number of users.
3. Construct a platform or slab over the pit using durable materials such as concrete or wood for hygiene and safety.
4. Install a fly-killing mechanism, which may include:
- A trap with a baited container (e.g., oil or food) to attract and kill flies.
- Hanging strips of fabric soaked in kerosene or other insecticides.
5. Ensure the latrine has a roof to protect users from rain and keep the area dry.
6. Provide a handwashing station with soap and clean water near the latrine to promote hygiene.
7. Encourage community members to use the latrine regularly and maintain cleanliness to prevent disease spread.
VIP latrine,flies,sanitation,hygiene
Constructing and Maintaining a Trough Urinal with Splashboard and Soakage Pit
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Materials:
• Wood and tar paper
• Tin, galvanized iron, or any other suitable material

Construction Steps:
1. Build the trough urinal using wood and tar paper, or improvise it with tin, galvanized iron, or similar materials.
2. Install a...
Materials:
• Wood and tar paper
• Tin, galvanized iron, or any other suitable material

Construction Steps:
1. Build the trough urinal using wood and tar paper, or improvise it with tin, galvanized iron, or similar materials.
2. Install a splashboard to prevent splashing of urine.
3. Construct a soakage pit beneath the urinal for disposal of urine.

Additional Features:
• Ensure the structure is sturdy and watertight where necessary.
• The trough should be sloped slightly to allow proper drainage into the soakage pit.

Maintenance Steps:
1. Regularly inspect the structure for damage or wear.
2. Clean the urinal periodically to prevent buildup of waste and odors.
3. Ensure the soakage pit is not clogged and is functioning properly.
4. If using a covered windbreak, ensure it is in place to protect from rainwater flooding.
trough urinal, splashboard, soakage pit, sanitation, latrine construction
Build a Single Pit Latrine
Reference Source: Safe Water Manual
Materials Required:
• Shovel
• Locally available materials (bricks, rot-resistant timber, concrete, oil drums, bamboo or stones)

Steps:
1. Dig a hole: The pit is ideally deeper than 3 m and of 1 – 1.5 m in diameter. The risk of...
Materials Required:
• Shovel
• Locally available materials (bricks, rot-resistant timber, concrete, oil drums, bamboo or stones)

Steps:
1. Dig a hole: The pit is ideally deeper than 3 m and of 1 – 1.5 m in diameter. The risk of collapsing increases if the diameter exceeds 1.5 m.
2. Line the pit: If the pit is to be reused or built in soft, loose soil, the upper half of the pit should be lined. Use adequate materials like bricks, rot-resistant timber, concrete, oil drums, bamboo or stones for lining.

Additional Considerations:
• Construct single pit latrines at a distance of 30 m from the next water source and also at an appropriate distance from buildings.
• A full single pit latrine can be covered and the superstructure moved to a new pit or it can be pumped out and reused.
• If the pit is to be reused or built in soft, loose soil, it should be lined with adequate, locally available materials.
single pit latrine, sanitation infrastructure, construction, lining materials, groundwater contamination

Hygiene (4) -

Proper Handwashing Technique for Safe Water Handling
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
To properly wash hands for safe water handling, follow these steps:

1. Wet your hands with clean, running water (warm or cold).
2. Apply soap: Use enough soap to cover all surfaces of the hands.
3. Rub palms together: Rub the palms of...
To properly wash hands for safe water handling, follow these steps:

1. Wet your hands with clean, running water (warm or cold).
2. Apply soap: Use enough soap to cover all surfaces of the hands.
3. Rub palms together: Rub the palms of your hands together to create a lather.
4. Interlace fingers: Interlace your fingers and rub them together to clean between the fingers.
5. Scrub the backs of your hands: Use the opposite hand to scrub the back of each hand with soap.
6. Clean thumbs and fingertips: Rub your thumbs in a circular motion against the opposite palm, then interlace your fingers and rub them together to clean the fingertips.
7. Rinse thoroughly: Rinse your hands under running water to remove all soap and contaminants.
8. Dry your hands using a clean towel or air dryer.
9. Avoid touching contaminated surfaces: After washing, avoid touching any objects that may be contaminated until you have completed the task at hand.
handwashing, hygiene, water handling, sanitation
Distinguish Between Dirty and Clean Households
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
Lesson 1: Dirty and clean household

• Dirty household: Flies in the house, chicken on storage container, open defecation from child and animals, open waste disposal, faecal contamination of the water source, untied animals near the house,...
Lesson 1: Dirty and clean household

• Dirty household: Flies in the house, chicken on storage container, open defecation from child and animals, open waste disposal, faecal contamination of the water source, untied animals near the house, unhygienic food storage.
• Clean household: Protected water source, latrine, tied animals, distance between house and animals, water storage container with lid, clean house, container for waste disposal.

Picture references: [96 x 132], [96 x 132], [152 x 8] intentionally omitted.
household hygiene, sanitation practices, clean vs dirty home
Acquire and Store Sponges for Cleaning Purposes
Standard Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Title: Acquire and Store Sponges for Cleaning Purposes

Procedure:
1. Purchase ten sponges.
2. Ensure the quantity is sufficient for at least 30 days of use.
3. Store them in a dry, accessible location.
4. Use the sponges for cleaning...
Title: Acquire and Store Sponges for Cleaning Purposes

Procedure:
1. Purchase ten sponges.
2. Ensure the quantity is sufficient for at least 30 days of use.
3. Store them in a dry, accessible location.
4. Use the sponges for cleaning surfaces, dishes, or personal hygiene during emergencies.

Notes:
• Sponges are essential for maintaining cleanliness and hygiene in survival situations.
• Ensure they are stored safely to prevent contamination.
sponges, cleaning, sanitation, preparedness
Acquire and Store a Toilet Seat for Emergency Use
Standard Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Title: Acquire and Store a Toilet Seat for Emergency Use

Procedure:
1. Purchase one toilet seat.
2. Ensure the quantity is sufficient for at least 90 days of use.
3. Store it in a dry, accessible location.
4. Use the toilet seat to provide...
Title: Acquire and Store a Toilet Seat for Emergency Use

Procedure:
1. Purchase one toilet seat.
2. Ensure the quantity is sufficient for at least 90 days of use.
3. Store it in a dry, accessible location.
4. Use the toilet seat to provide a more comfortable and hygienic option during emergencies when regular toilets may be unavailable.

Notes:
• A toilet seat can improve comfort and hygiene in survival situations.
• Ensure that it is stored safely and is ready for use at any time.
toilet seat, sanitation, emergency use, preparedness

Hand Hygiene (4) -

Build a Hand Washing Station with Bottles
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
Materials Required:

• Piles of wood, metal, or bamboo
• Nails
• Bottles (0.5–3 litres)
• Strong rope

### Step 1: Set Up the Framework
Use wood, metal, or bamboo to build a stable horizontal framework on solid pillars. Ensure that wastewater is...
Materials Required:

• Piles of wood, metal, or bamboo
• Nails
• Bottles (0.5–3 litres)
• Strong rope

### Step 1: Set Up the Framework
Use wood, metal, or bamboo to build a stable horizontal framework on solid pillars. Ensure that wastewater is collected in containers or that soakaways are used to prevent muddy pools.

### Step 2: Prepare the Bottles
Cut empty PET bottles with screw caps into two pieces about 5 cm above the bottom. Pierce each side of the bottle parts with a hot nail or knife. Pull the rope through the holes.

• The smaller part hangs inside the larger one and functions as a tap or soap dish.
• While filling the larger part of the bottle with water, the smaller part hangs on the rope alongside.

### Step 3: Hang the Bottles on the Framework
Hang the prepared bottles on the framework. Ensure that the bottles are at an appropriate height for children to use comfortably.

Optional Modification:
To avoid direct contact of the screw cap with dirty hands, pierce the cap on one side with a hot nail and prepare a clean stick or match to seal the hole. This creates an additional structure for water exit.

### Usage:
Children can turn the screw cap until water emerges to wash their hands according to the three key steps of hand washing. It is important that children touch the screw caps with caution to prevent recontamination of the water.
hand washing, sanitation, hygiene station, bottle, water container, soap dish
Build a Hand Washing Station with a Water Container
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
Materials Required:

• Specially fabricated containers with a tap and spigot
• Table or platform for placing the container
• Soap
• Wastewater collection container or soakaway

### Step 1: Set Up the Structure
Place specially fabricated...
Materials Required:

• Specially fabricated containers with a tap and spigot
• Table or platform for placing the container
• Soap
• Wastewater collection container or soakaway

### Step 1: Set Up the Structure
Place specially fabricated containers with a tap and spigot on a table or platform. Ensure that soap is available near the station.

### Step 2: Prepare Wastewater Collection
Place another container for wastewater collection or install a soakaway on the ground to prevent muddy pools from forming.

Optional Design:
Hand washing stations can also be produced with a five-litre container hanging on a horizontal stick. This provides an alternative setup that is easy to construct and use in various environments.
hand washing, sanitation, hygiene station, water container, wastewater collection
Protect Against Diseases from Soil and Common Objects
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Steps:

1. Wash your hands regularly to defend against germs from the soil and objects handled by others:
- After using the latrine.
- Before touching eating utensils or food.
- After eating.
- After handling any item that can...
Steps:

1. Wash your hands regularly to defend against germs from the soil and objects handled by others:
- After using the latrine.
- Before touching eating utensils or food.
- After eating.
- After handling any item that can potentially transfer germs.
- Frequently during the workday to keep your hands free of germs.
2. Use hand-sanitizing gel as an effective way to disinfect hands from nearly all germs that cause illnesses.
3. Wash your face and body with soap and water.
hand washing, germ prevention, hand sanitizer
Wash Hands with Soap After Defecating
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
Procedure:

1. Immediately after defecating, wash both hands thoroughly.
2. Use clean water and soap (or alternatives like ash or detergent if soap is unavailable).
3. Rub the front and back of your hands together at least three times.
4. Ensure...
Procedure:

1. Immediately after defecating, wash both hands thoroughly.
2. Use clean water and soap (or alternatives like ash or detergent if soap is unavailable).
3. Rub the front and back of your hands together at least three times.
4. Ensure you rub between all fingers and around the thumbs at least three times.
5. Dry your hands using a clean cloth or paper towel.

Notes:
• Hand washing after defecating is critical to prevent the spread of disease-causing germs.
• If soap is not available, ash or detergent can be used as effective alternatives.
hand washing, hygiene, sanitation, soap, defecation

Surface Cleaning (3) -

Cleaning Gold Lace and Embroidery
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To clean gold lace and embroidery:

1. Avoid Alkaline Liquors: Do not use alkaline liquors as they may corrode silk or alter colors.
2. Use Spirit of Wine: Use spirit of wine to clean without damaging the gold or silk.
3. **Caution with...
To clean gold lace and embroidery:

1. Avoid Alkaline Liquors: Do not use alkaline liquors as they may corrode silk or alter colors.
2. Use Spirit of Wine: Use spirit of wine to clean without damaging the gold or silk.
3. Caution with Spirit of Wine: It is not suitable in all cases, especially if the gold is worn off or alloyed with base metals that may be corroded by air.
4. Tarnish Consideration: Removing tarnish may make the lace or embroidery less like gold than before.
gold lace,embroidery,cleaning,sanitation,spirit of wine
Cleaning Marble Stains
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To clean marble stains:

1. Mixing Ingredients: Take verdigris, pumice-stone (well powdered), and lime newly slaked.
2. Add Soap Lees: Mix with soap lees to achieve a putty-like consistency.
3. Application: Put the mixture in a woollen...
To clean marble stains:

1. Mixing Ingredients: Take verdigris, pumice-stone (well powdered), and lime newly slaked.
2. Add Soap Lees: Mix with soap lees to achieve a putty-like consistency.
3. Application: Put the mixture in a woollen rag and rub the stains well in one direction.
4. Washing: Wash off the residue with soap and water.
5. Repeat if Necessary: If the stain remains, repeat the process.

Alternative Method:
1. Cover Stains: Cover the stains with fuller's earth or plaster of Paris.
2. Dry and Brush Off: Allow it to dry, then brush it off.
marble,cleaning,stains,verdigris,pumice-stone,lime,soap lees,fuller's earth,plaster of Paris
Removing Stains from Silver Plate
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To remove stains from silver plate:

1. Soak in Soap Lyes: Steep the plate in soap lyes for 4 hours.
2. Apply Whiting: Cover the plate with whiting, wet it with vinegar to make it stick thickly onto the surface.
3. Dry by Fire: Allow it...
To remove stains from silver plate:

1. Soak in Soap Lyes: Steep the plate in soap lyes for 4 hours.
2. Apply Whiting: Cover the plate with whiting, wet it with vinegar to make it stick thickly onto the surface.
3. Dry by Fire: Allow it to dry over a fire.
4. Remove Whiting: Rub off the whiting.
5. Buff with Bran: Pass the plate over with dry bran to restore its brightness.
silver,plate,stains,soap lyes,whiting,vinegar,dry bran

Disinfection Deodorization (3) -

Prepare Condy's Solution for Disinfection and Deodorization
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Condy's Solution is a saturated solution of permanganate of potassa (potassium permanganate) used as an efficient disinfectant and deodorizer. To prepare:

1. Dissolve one teaspoonful of potassium permanganate in a **soup-plate full of...
Condy's Solution is a saturated solution of permanganate of potassa (potassium permanganate) used as an efficient disinfectant and deodorizer. To prepare:

1. Dissolve one teaspoonful of potassium permanganate in a soup-plate full of water.
2. Expose the solution in a room to quickly remove offensive smells.
3. When the pink color disappears, more must be added until the water retains a slight pinkish tint.
4. To use for foul water purification:
- Add one teaspoonful to a hogshead (approximately 59 gallons) of water.
- If necessary, add more potassium permanganate until the water has a slight pinkish tint.
5. The pink color will disappear if a stick is placed in the water for a few minutes.
condy's solution,potassium permanganate,disinfectant,deodorizer,foul water purification
Prepare Eau de Javelle (Chlorinated Lime Solution)
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Eau de Javelle is a disinfectant and deodorizing solution made from chloride of lime. To prepare:

### Method 1:
1. Dissolve 2 oz. (approximately 57 grams) of carbonate of potassa (potassium carbonate) in **1/4 pint (approximately 118 ml) of...
Eau de Javelle is a disinfectant and deodorizing solution made from chloride of lime. To prepare:

### Method 1:
1. Dissolve 2 oz. (approximately 57 grams) of carbonate of potassa (potassium carbonate) in 1/4 pint (approximately 118 ml) of water.
2. Prepare a paste by mixing chloride of lime (calcium hypochlorite) with water.
3. Mix the two solutions together and allow them to settle.
4. Pour off the clear portion for use.

### Method 2:
1. Pass a stream of free chlorine gas through a solution of carbonate of potassa (potassium carbonate) until it is saturated.
2. The resulting solution contains hypochlorite of potassa and chloride of potassium.
eau de javelle,chlorinated lime,disinfectant,deodorizer
Use Carbolic Acid for Disinfection and Deodorization
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Carbolic Acid (phenol) is a powerful antiseptic and deodorizer used in conjunction with lime. To use:

1. Mix carbolic acid with lime to create a disinfecting agent.
2. Ridgewood's Disinfecting Powder contains:
- 5-8% lime
...
Carbolic Acid (phenol) is a powerful antiseptic and deodorizer used in conjunction with lime. To use:

1. Mix carbolic acid with lime to create a disinfecting agent.
2. Ridgewood's Disinfecting Powder contains:
- 5-8% lime
- 5-8% carbolic acid
- 70-80% fuller's earth.

### Uses:
• Used to coagulate organic matter and act as an antiseptic.
• Effective in deodorizing and purifying water.
carbolic acid,phenol,lime,disinfectant

Food Safety Inspection (3) -

Conduct Special Sanitary Inspection of Commercial Food Establishment
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Purpose: Ensure continued approval of an establishment listed in the Directory by conducting a complete sanitary inspection.

Procedure:
1. Conduct a full inspection similar to an initial sanitary inspection.
2. Inspect all areas of the...
Purpose: Ensure continued approval of an establishment listed in the Directory by conducting a complete sanitary inspection.

Procedure:
1. Conduct a full inspection similar to an initial sanitary inspection.
2. Inspect all areas of the establishment, including food handling, storage, and preparation zones.
3. Evaluate whether deficiencies identified during previous inspections have been corrected.
4. Determine if the establishment remains approved as a source of food for the military services.
5. If necessary, initiate this inspection based on requests from commanders or due to health threats posed by the establishment's products.

Notes:
• Special inspections may be initiated when an establishment fails to correct deficiencies within a reasonable time frame.
• This inspection is comprehensive and must be conducted by military veterinary service personnel.
special inspection, food establishment, sanitation, health threat
Conduct Sanitary Inspections of Food Establishments for DOD Sales
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Objective: Protect the health of military personnel from foodborne illnesses.

Procedure:
1. Conduct sanitary inspections of food establishments desiring to sell to the Department of Defense (DOD) by personnel of the Military Veterinary...
Objective: Protect the health of military personnel from foodborne illnesses.

Procedure:
1. Conduct sanitary inspections of food establishments desiring to sell to the Department of Defense (DOD) by personnel of the Military Veterinary Service.
2. Ensure that the procedures used and recommendations made during inspections are in accordance with Army Regulation 40-657, *Veterinary Food Inspections*.
3. Focus on inspecting food for wholesomeness and food establishments for sanitation to ensure compliance with accepted public health practices.
sanitary inspection, DOD food sales, military veterinary service, food safety
Conduct a Sanitary Inspection of a Commissary Store
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Procedure:

1. Inspect using MIL-STD-903C:
- Use the *Sanitary Standards for Commissaries* to evaluate compliance with minimum sanitary requirements.
- Refer to DD Form 2460, *Commissary Sanitary Compliance Checklist*, included in...
Procedure:

1. Inspect using MIL-STD-903C:
- Use the *Sanitary Standards for Commissaries* to evaluate compliance with minimum sanitary requirements.
- Refer to DD Form 2460, *Commissary Sanitary Compliance Checklist*, included in Appendix C of the subcourse.

2. Check construction and repair of equipment and utensils:
- Ensure all equipment and utensils are smooth, easily cleaned, and sanitized.
- Confirm that they are made from durable, nontoxic materials approved by the National Sanitation Foundation (NSF).
- Verify that no wooden materials are used in construction.

3. Verify thermometer usage:
- Ensure bimetallic thermometers are issued to all supervisors for temperature monitoring.
- Prohibit the use of unprotected glass/mercury or alcohol-type thermometers.

4. Record violations:
- Document any unsanitary conditions found during the inspection.
- Ensure that the commissary officer is responsible for correcting these issues.

5. Frequency of inspections:
- Conduct periodic inspections by a commissioned Medical or Veterinary Corps officer.
- At the discretion of the Deputy for the Veterinary Activities, more frequent inspections may be conducted by a veterinary officer or qualified enlisted personnel (e.g., veterinary food inspection specialist).
sanitary inspection, commissary store, MIL-STD-903C, NSF approved materials, thermometer usage, food safety compliance

Warehouse Management (3) -

Maintain Sanitary Conditions in Warehouse Refrigeration Areas
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Procedure:

1. Odor and Mold Control:
- Ensure refrigeration areas are free from objectionable odors and mold.
2. Sanitation Maintenance:
- Maintain clean and sanitary conditions at all times.
3. Thermometer Installation:
-...
Procedure:

1. Odor and Mold Control:
- Ensure refrigeration areas are free from objectionable odors and mold.
2. Sanitation Maintenance:
- Maintain clean and sanitary conditions at all times.
3. Thermometer Installation:
- Equip refrigeration areas with an accurate thermometer.
4. Temperature Checks:
- Conduct temperature checks on storage refrigerators and freezers at least three times each working day and once on nonworking days.
5. Storage Temperatures:
- Maintain chill storage requirements (below 40°F) and freezer storage requirements (0°F or below).
refrigeration areas, warehouse, temperature checks, sanitation
Proper Storage and Inspection in Warehouse Facilities
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Procedure:

1. Storage Facility Maintenance:
- Ensure storage facilities are clean, sanitary, and in good repair.
2. Use of Shelves and Pallets:
- Use shelves, cabinets, dunnage, or pallets where necessary to protect materials from...
Procedure:

1. Storage Facility Maintenance:
- Ensure storage facilities are clean, sanitary, and in good repair.
2. Use of Shelves and Pallets:
- Use shelves, cabinets, dunnage, or pallets where necessary to protect materials from contamination.
3. Compliance with Directives:
- Follow directives and technical manuals of the military services concerned for storage practices.
4. Daily Inspection:
- Inspect storage facilities daily for cleanliness and proper stock rotation.
5. Damaged Item Handling:
- Remove damaged items (e.g., cans with missing labels, dents, leakers, swellers) to the salvage area for inspection.
6. Product Disposal Recommendations:
- Make recommendations to the commissary officer regarding serviceability of the product.
- Dispose of the product through regular issue, special sale at reduced prices, forced issue, or condemnation.
storage facilities, warehouse management, damaged items, product disposal
Maintain Cleanliness in Warehouse Areas
Reference Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Procedure:

1. Daily Spillage Removal:
- Remove food product spillage from the warehouse on a daily basis.
2. Floor Cleaning:
- Sweep and clean floors weekly or more often if necessary.
3. Storage of Cleaning Equipment:
-...
Procedure:

1. Daily Spillage Removal:
- Remove food product spillage from the warehouse on a daily basis.
2. Floor Cleaning:
- Sweep and clean floors weekly or more often if necessary.
3. Storage of Cleaning Equipment:
- Store cleaning utensils and equipment separately from storage areas.
warehouse cleanliness, spillage removal, floor cleaning

Bedding Cleaning (3) -

Cleaning or Sterilizing Bedding at Home
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Wash cloth bedding in soap and water.
2. Dry it thoroughly by hanging it in the sun or ironing it.
3. Do not dry bedding on the ground, as this can cause it to pick up germs.
bedding hygiene, home cleaning, infection prevention, midwifery practices
Sterilizing Bedding in a Hospital or Clinic
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

After each birth, sterilize bedding using one of the following methods:

1. Wash the bedding with soap and water, then boil it for 30 minutes. Dry thoroughly in a clean place.
2. Or, wash the bedding with soap and water,...
Procedure:

After each birth, sterilize bedding using one of the following methods:

1. Wash the bedding with soap and water, then boil it for 30 minutes. Dry thoroughly in a clean place.
2. Or, wash the bedding with soap and water, then use a hot iron to dry it.
3. If neither boiling nor hot ironing is possible, wash the bedding with soap and water, then hang it in the sun until fully dried. Ensure both sides are exposed to sunlight and keep it clean during drying.
hospital bedding sterilization, infection control, midwifery hygiene, sanitation
Storing Bedding to Prevent Germs
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. If bedding is not going to be used immediately, keep it clean and dry until ready for use.
2. Place the bedding in a clean bag or wrap it in clean paper, then store it in a clean, dry place.
3. Do not store **damp or...
Procedure:

1. If bedding is not going to be used immediately, keep it clean and dry until ready for use.
2. Place the bedding in a clean bag or wrap it in clean paper, then store it in a clean, dry place.
3. Do not store damp or wet bedding, as germs can return and contaminate it.
bedding storage, infection prevention, midwifery hygiene, sanitation

Equipment Cleaning (3) -

Sterilizing Equipment by Pressure Steaming
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Prepare the equipment: Ensure all tools and items to be sterilized are clean and free of debris.

2. Place in a pressure steamer: Arrange the items in a single layer within the pressure steamer, ensuring they are not...
Procedure:

1. Prepare the equipment: Ensure all tools and items to be sterilized are clean and free of debris.

2. Place in a pressure steamer: Arrange the items in a single layer within the pressure steamer, ensuring they are not overcrowded.

3. Fill the steamer with water: Add enough water to the bottom compartment of the pressure steamer as instructed by the manufacturer.

4. Close and lock the steamer: Ensure that the lid is securely closed and locked in place before starting the process.

5. Apply heat and pressure: Place the steamer on a heat source and allow it to build up pressure according to the manufacturer's instructions, typically reaching around 120°C (248°F) with sufficient steam pressure.

6. Sterilize for 15–20 minutes: Maintain the pressure and temperature for at least 15–20 minutes to ensure complete sterilization of all items.

7. Cool before use: Allow the steamer to cool completely before opening it and handling the tools.

Notes:
• This method is highly effective for sterilizing a wide range of equipment, including metal, glass, and some plastic items.
• Always follow the manufacturer's guidelines for operating the pressure steamer safely.
• Ensure that all items are fully submerged in steam during the process.
sterilizing equipment,pressure steaming,sanitation
Sterilizing Equipment with Chemicals
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Prepare the equipment: Ensure all tools and items to be sterilized are clean and free of debris.

2. Select an appropriate chemical solution: Use a high-level disinfectant or sterilizing agent such as sodium hypochlorite...
Procedure:

1. Prepare the equipment: Ensure all tools and items to be sterilized are clean and free of debris.

2. Select an appropriate chemical solution: Use a high-level disinfectant or sterilizing agent such as sodium hypochlorite (bleach) or other approved chemical solutions.

3. Submerge items in the solution: Place the tools into the chemical solution, ensuring they are fully submerged.

4. Soak for the recommended time: Follow the manufacturer's instructions for the specific chemical being used; typically, this may range from 10 to 30 minutes depending on the tool and solution.

5. Rinse thoroughly with clean water: After soaking, rinse all items under running clean water to remove any residual chemicals.

6. Dry before use: Allow the items to air dry completely before handling or using them.

Notes:
• Always wear protective gloves and goggles when handling chemical solutions.
• Ensure proper ventilation in the area where the sterilization is taking place.
• Follow all safety guidelines provided by the manufacturer of the chemical solution.
sterilizing equipment,chemicals,sanitation
Storing Tools and Supplies After Sterilization
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Ensure items are completely dry: After sterilizing tools and supplies, make sure they are fully dried to prevent the growth of bacteria or mold.

2. Use clean containers: Place the sterilized items in clean, dry containers...
Procedure:

1. Ensure items are completely dry: After sterilizing tools and supplies, make sure they are fully dried to prevent the growth of bacteria or mold.

2. Use clean containers: Place the sterilized items in clean, dry containers that are specifically designated for storing sterile equipment.

3. Keep away from contaminants: Store the containers in a location that is free from dust, moisture, and potential sources of contamination such as animals or insects.

4. Label containers clearly: Clearly label each container with the contents and the date of sterilization to ensure proper tracking and usage.

5. Maintain sterility until use: Ensure that the tools remain sterile until they are ready for use, avoiding any unnecessary exposure to the environment.

Notes:
• Regularly inspect stored items for signs of damage or contamination.
• Replace containers if they become damaged or difficult to clean.
• Follow local health guidelines for the storage and handling of medical equipment.
storing tools,sanitation,equipment_cleaning

Infection Control (3) -

Prevent Disease Transmission from Sick Individuals During Eating
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Purpose: To prevent the spread of contagious diseases during meal preparation and consumption.

Procedure:
1. People with tuberculosis, flu, colds, or other easily transmitted diseases should eat separately from others.
2. Ensure that...
Purpose: To prevent the spread of contagious diseases during meal preparation and consumption.

Procedure:
1. People with tuberculosis, flu, colds, or other easily transmitted diseases should eat separately from others.
2. Ensure that plates and utensils used by sick individuals are cleaned very well before being used by others.
3. Use separate eating areas or utensils for sick individuals to avoid cross-contamination.

Notes:
• This is especially important in communal settings where disease can spread quickly.
• If resources are limited, ensure that all shared utensils and surfaces are thoroughly disinfected after use by a sick person.
disease prevention, infection control, food safety, sanitation
Sterilizing Tools and Bedding
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Clean all tools and bedding thoroughly with soap and water before sterilization.
2. Boil the items in clean water for at least 15 minutes to kill germs and bacteria.
3. Allow the items to cool completely before...
Procedure:

1. Clean all tools and bedding thoroughly with soap and water before sterilization.
2. Boil the items in clean water for at least 15 minutes to kill germs and bacteria.
3. Allow the items to cool completely before handling or using them again.
4. Store sterilized tools and bedding in a dry, clean container to prevent recontamination.

Notes:
• This procedure is essential for preventing infections during childbirth and postpartum care.
• It should be performed regularly, especially after each use.
sterilization, infection control, midwifery, tools
Sterilization of Instruments
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Sterilization of Dental Instruments

Proper sterilization is crucial to prevent infections during dental procedures. Follow these steps:

1. Clean instruments: Wash all instruments with soap and water, then rinse thoroughly.
2. **Soak in...
Sterilization of Dental Instruments

Proper sterilization is crucial to prevent infections during dental procedures. Follow these steps:

1. Clean instruments: Wash all instruments with soap and water, then rinse thoroughly.
2. Soak in disinfectant: Soak the cleaned instruments in a suitable disinfectant solution (e.g., bleach or alcohol) for at least 30 minutes to kill bacteria and viruses.
3. Use steam sterilization if available: Place instruments in an autoclave or pressure cooker with water, close it tightly, and heat until steam is released. Maintain high pressure for at least 15-20 minutes.
4. Dry the instruments: Remove the instruments from the sterilizer and dry them thoroughly to prevent rusting.
5. Store in a clean container: Place the sterilized instruments in a clean, covered container to keep them protected until use.
6. Label containers clearly: Mark containers with dates or expiration times for sterilization to ensure they are used within their effective period.
7. Avoid reusing single-use items: Never reuse disposable items like gloves or gauze.
8. Monitor the process regularly: Ensure that all instruments are properly cleaned and sterilized before each use.
instrument sterilization, infection control, dental hygiene

Biological Warfare Decontamination (3) -

Chemical Decontamination of Biological Warfare Agents
High Priority Source: Emergency War Surgery
Chemical decontamination renders BW agents harmless through the use of disinfectants.

• Soap and water followed with copious rinsing with water: Often sufficient for decontamination.
• For patients requiring urgent decontamination,...
Chemical decontamination renders BW agents harmless through the use of disinfectants.

• Soap and water followed with copious rinsing with water: Often sufficient for decontamination.
• For patients requiring urgent decontamination, biologic agents are neutralized within 5 minutes when contaminated areas are washed with a 0.5% hypochlorite solution (1 part household bleach mixed with 9 parts water).

Important warning: Do not use hypochlorite in the eyes, abdominal cavity, or on nerve tissue.

• A 5% hypochlorite solution (household bleach) may be used to decontaminate clothing or equipment.
chemical decontamination,hypochlorite,BW agents,decontamination solution
Mechanical Decontamination of Biological Warfare Agents
High Priority Source: Emergency War Surgery
Mechanical decontamination removes, but not necessarily neutralizes, the BW agent.

• Brushing: Ensures loosening of the BW agent from surfaces.
• Filtration and chlorination of drinking water: Removes organisms from water sources.
mechanical decontamination,BW agents,brushing,chlorination
Physical Decontamination of Biological Warfare Agents
High Priority Source: Emergency War Surgery
Physical decontamination such as heat and solar ultraviolet (UV) radiation:

• Dry heat: For 2 hours at 160 °C.
• Autoclaving: At 120 °C under 1 atm of overpressure for 20 minutes.
• UV radiation: Difficult to standardize, but can be...
Physical decontamination such as heat and solar ultraviolet (UV) radiation:

• Dry heat: For 2 hours at 160 °C.
• Autoclaving: At 120 °C under 1 atm of overpressure for 20 minutes.
• UV radiation: Difficult to standardize, but can be used in some scenarios.

Note: Dry biological agents can be a hazard through secondary aerosolization; however, adequate liquid decontamination will prevent this hazard. There is no vapor hazard, and special protective masks are generally not required for surgical personnel.
physical decontamination,dry heat,UV radiation,BW agents

Cold Weather Operations (3) -

Control of Rats and Mice in Cold Weather Conditions
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Rats and mice are a definite menace to health and property in cold regions:

1. Use rat poisons or traps when available.
2. Maintain strict control over rodent populations to prevent damage to supplies and health risks.
rodent control,cold weather,pest management,field hygiene
Field Sanitation in Colder Regions - Waste Disposal
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
In colder regions, field sanitation follows the same principles as temperate climates but with added challenges due to extreme conditions:

Human Excreta Disposal:
1. Use pit or 'cross-tree' type latrines for disposal of human waste.
2. One...
In colder regions, field sanitation follows the same principles as temperate climates but with added challenges due to extreme conditions:

Human Excreta Disposal:
1. Use pit or 'cross-tree' type latrines for disposal of human waste.
2. One latrine should serve 3-4 shelters or a platoon-sized unit.
3. Place the latrine downwind from the bivouac, but not so far as to encourage unsanitary behavior.
4. Use ration boxes or similar materials to collect waste.
5. Designate a urinal within 4-5 meters (4-5 yards) of each shelter.
6. Construct a windbreak using boughs, tarpaulins, ponchos, or snow walls to protect the latrine from wind.

Disposal After Leaving Bivouac:
1. Burn or bury accumulated human waste in the latrine.
2. Clearly mark closed latrine sites if tactical situation permits.
waste disposal,cold weather sanitation,latrines,field hygiene
Trash and Garbage Disposal in Winter Conditions
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
In winter conditions, trash and garbage should be disposed of as follows:

1. Edible Food Waste:
- Collect edible portions in receptacles.
- Bury in snow at a safe distance from the bivouac.
2. Non-Edible Trash and Garbage:
- Burn...
In winter conditions, trash and garbage should be disposed of as follows:

1. Edible Food Waste:
- Collect edible portions in receptacles.
- Bury in snow at a safe distance from the bivouac.
2. Non-Edible Trash and Garbage:
- Burn the bulk of trash and garbage, especially in areas where bears are present to avoid attracting them to campsites.
3. Marking Disposal Sites:
- Mark all trash and garbage dumps with appropriate signs for future users.
4. Camouflage:
- Camouflage trash and garbage to prevent visibility from the air or enemy:
- Place under available cover.
- Use snow, branches, or other materials to camouflage dark trash on white snow.
- Avoid leaving glittering items like tin cans or bottles visible.
trash disposal,winter sanitation,camouflage,field hygiene

Mold Prevention (3) -

Disposing of Moldy Food Safely
Standard Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Procedure:

1. Do not sniff moldy food, as it can cause respiratory difficulties for those with mold allergies.
2. Dispose of moldy food in a manner that prevents access by children and animals.
3. Mycotoxins are harmful to both...
Procedure:

1. Do not sniff moldy food, as it can cause respiratory difficulties for those with mold allergies.
2. Dispose of moldy food in a manner that prevents access by children and animals.
3. Mycotoxins are harmful to both humans and animals, so proper disposal is essential.

Important: Never allow children or pets to come into contact with moldy food.
moldy food,disposal,sanitation,mold prevention
Cleaning Refrigerator to Minimize Mold Growth
Reference Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Procedure:

1. Prepare a cleaning solution by dissolving one tablespoon of baking soda in a quart of warm water.
2. Use this solution to wash the inside of the refrigerator every couple of months.
3. Rinse the interior thoroughly with...
Procedure:

1. Prepare a cleaning solution by dissolving one tablespoon of baking soda in a quart of warm water.
2. Use this solution to wash the inside of the refrigerator every couple of months.
3. Rinse the interior thoroughly with clean water.
4. Allow the refrigerator to dry completely before replacing food items.

Additional Steps for Mold on Rubber Gaskets:
• Prepare a disinfecting solution by mixing three tablespoons of household bleach in a quart of water.
• Use a soft bristle brush to wipe down affected areas, such as rubber door gaskets.
• Note: In severe cases, the mold may not return to its original color and could turn pink or red after cleaning.

Important: Ensure proper ventilation during and after cleaning.
refrigerator,cleaning,mold,baking soda,bleach,sanitation
Maintaining Mold-Free Kitchen Environment
Reference Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Procedure:

1. Keep the kitchen area clean and dry at all times.
2. Spray surfaces occasionally with a disinfectant product such as Lysol to prevent mold growth.
3. Use the same bleach solution (three tablespoons of household bleach in a...
Procedure:

1. Keep the kitchen area clean and dry at all times.
2. Spray surfaces occasionally with a disinfectant product such as Lysol to prevent mold growth.
3. Use the same bleach solution (three tablespoons of household bleach in a quart of water) to eliminate patches of mold that may appear elsewhere in the kitchen.

Important: Avoid purchasing more fresh food than you can consume within a short period to reduce the risk of moldy leftovers.
kitchen,clean,mold prevention,Lysol,sanitation

Education (3) -

Conduct Safe Water School Lessons for Children
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
Objective: Train children aged 5-12 on water quality, hygiene, and health through nine structured lessons.

Steps:
1. Conduct one lesson per week, ideally repeating key activities to reinforce learning.
2. Ensure the lessons are...
Objective: Train children aged 5-12 on water quality, hygiene, and health through nine structured lessons.

Steps:
1. Conduct one lesson per week, ideally repeating key activities to reinforce learning.
2. Ensure the lessons are participatory, playful, and practice-oriented.
3. Maintain the thematic structure of the lessons for consistency in teaching.
4. Repeat good behavior practices such as hand washing, water disinfection, and safe storage at regular intervals.

Lesson Structure:
• Lesson 1: Water and health
• Lesson 2: Water contamination
• Lesson 3: Water disinfection
• Lesson 4: Solar water disinfection
• Lesson 5: Chlorination
• Lesson 6: Water quality test
• Lesson 7: Water recontamination
• Lesson 8: Hygiene
• Lesson 9: Sanitation

Details:
• Lessons are designed to empower children to improve water and hygiene practices.
• Children learn to transfer knowledge to families and the wider community.
• The lessons complement each other thematically, ensuring a comprehensive understanding of safe water and health.
safe water school lessons, child education, hygiene training, water quality
Use Life Skills Approach in Safe Water School Lessons
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
Objective: Develop children’s knowledge, attitudes, and skills through a life skills approach.

Steps:
1. Incorporate participatory teaching methods such as PHAST (Participatory Hygiene and Sanitation Transformation) and CHAST...
Objective: Develop children’s knowledge, attitudes, and skills through a life skills approach.

Steps:
1. Incorporate participatory teaching methods such as PHAST (Participatory Hygiene and Sanitation Transformation) and CHAST (Community-based Hygiene and Sanitation Transformation).
2. Focus on developing practical skills related to water treatment, hygiene, and health.
3. Encourage active participation from students in learning activities.
4. Reinforce positive attitudes towards maintaining clean water and good hygiene practices.

Details:
• The life skills approach ensures that children can apply what they learn in real-life situations.
• It supports long-term behavior change by integrating knowledge into daily routines.
• Methods like PHAST and CHAST are used to engage students effectively.
life skills approach, phast, chast, education methods
Calculating Faecal Waste in a School Setting
Reference Source: Safe Water Manual
Understanding the volume of faeces produced in schools helps emphasize the importance of safe sanitation practices.

### Calculation:
1. Estimate daily faeces production: Assume an average of 100 grams per defecation.
2. **Multiply by...
Understanding the volume of faeces produced in schools helps emphasize the importance of safe sanitation practices.

### Calculation:
1. Estimate daily faeces production: Assume an average of 100 grams per defecation.
2. Multiply by number of students and days in a month (30):
- Example: 100 g x number of people x 30 days = total monthly faecal waste.

### Visualizing the Volume:
• Compare the calculated amount to the capacity of wheelbarrows or trucks to illustrate the scale.
• Ask students how many wheelbarrow loads would be needed to transport all this waste.

> Note: This exercise helps children understand the importance of safe faeces disposal and the potential health risks of open defecation.
faecal waste calculation, sanitation education, school hygiene

Water Contamination (3) -

Demonstrate Contaminated Water Risk
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
### Materials:
• 1 transparent glass
• 0.2 litre of safe water
• 1 twig

### Procedure:
1. Fill the glass with safe water and ask if anyone is willing to drink it. Let him/her take some sips.
2. Walk through the school or community and find...
### Materials:
• 1 transparent glass
• 0.2 litre of safe water
• 1 twig

### Procedure:
1. Fill the glass with safe water and ask if anyone is willing to drink it. Let him/her take some sips.
2. Walk through the school or community and find some open faeces. Take a piece of grass or twig, touch the faeces, and dip it into the water.
3. Ask if anyone is willing to drink the water now. Normally nobody wants to drink it. Ask why they refuse to drink it. Emphasise the fact that water can also be contaminated if it is clear. If some children want to drink the water, do not let them. Repeat the message about the dangerous faeces.
contaminated water,demonstration,school activity,faeces,health risk
Discuss Water Contamination and Safety
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
### Discussion Points:
• Why is some water not safe for drinking?
• What are the potential water contamination stages?
• Do you think the water you drink at home is safe?
• **What are the local water sources and what is their...
### Discussion Points:
• Why is some water not safe for drinking?
• What are the potential water contamination stages?
• Do you think the water you drink at home is safe?
• What are the local water sources and what is their quality?
• Would you drink water contaminated with faeces?
discussion,contaminated water,school lesson,water safety
Home-Bringing Message on Safe Water
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
### Key Messages:
• Contaminated water is a threat to our health.
• Water can be contaminated at the source, during transport or through inaccurate storage.
home message,safe water,contamination sources

Water Treatment Infrastructure (3) -

Constructing a SODIS Table
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
Constructing a SODIS Table

• The table can be built with wood and corrugated iron, ensuring stability and additional water heating from the iron.
• Use bamboo or sticks as an alternative construction material if needed.
• The...
Constructing a SODIS Table

• The table can be built with wood and corrugated iron, ensuring stability and additional water heating from the iron.
• Use bamboo or sticks as an alternative construction material if needed.
• The size of the table should be adapted according to the requirements.
• An inclined surface can direct the bottles perfectly towards the sun, increasing the impact of UV-A radiation.
• The table should be high enough to prevent animals from urinating over it, but still reachable for children.
sodis table,water treatment,sanitation
Selecting Bottles for the SODIS Method
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
Selecting Bottles for the SODIS Method

• Use PET bottles, as they are light and do not break.
• PET bottles with only a light bluish tinge are also suitable.
• Avoid using heavily scratched bottles, as these must be replaced.
-...
Selecting Bottles for the SODIS Method

• Use PET bottles, as they are light and do not break.
• PET bottles with only a light bluish tinge are also suitable.
• Avoid using heavily scratched bottles, as these must be replaced.
• The bottles should be transparent and colourless.
• Ensure that the bottles do not hold more than three litres, as UV radiation is reduced with increasing water depth.
sodis bottles,water treatment,sanitation
Setting Up a SODIS Station
High Priority Source: Safe Water Manual
Setting Up a SODIS Station

• The SODIS station is made of a solid place to treat water with the SODIS method.
• It should ideally include a sheet of corrugated iron for additional water heating.
• The selected place should...
Setting Up a SODIS Station

• The SODIS station is made of a solid place to treat water with the SODIS method.
• It should ideally include a sheet of corrugated iron for additional water heating.
• The selected place should provide sufficient solar radiation and be easily accessed by children.
• If a table cannot be built, the SODIS station can also be positioned on a low roof or wall.
sodis station,water treatment,sanitation

Food Hygiene (2) -

Hygienic Food Preparation and Serving in Shelters
High Priority Source: Sanitation Basics
Procedure for Hygienic Food Preparation and Serving in Shelters:

1. Mix sugar with cereals during cooking to avoid spilling.
2. When feeding infants or small children, use a piece of plastic as a surface to prevent spillage from...
Procedure for Hygienic Food Preparation and Serving in Shelters:

1. Mix sugar with cereals during cooking to avoid spilling.
2. When feeding infants or small children, use a piece of plastic as a surface to prevent spillage from complicating shelter life.
3. Serve most foods on squares of plastic, which can be used as disposable plates.
4. Use spoons and plastic dishes for eating, but clean them thoroughly after each use:
- Lick the utensils or dishes clean after eating.
- Disinfect by boiling or by dipping into a chlorine bleach solution:
- Mix 1 tablespoon of Clorox-type bleach to 1 quart of water.
5. If no spoon is available, eat thick grain mush in a sanitary manner by placing it on a piece of plastic held in the hand, forming it into a ball, and taking bites.
6. Avoid using dishes when possible; use plastic squares or other disposable materials for serving food.
hygiene,shelter,food,serving,clean
Cleanliness of Eating Utensils
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Procedure:
• Wash eating utensils immediately after a meal.
• Leave them exposed to sunlight if possible after washing for better hygiene.

Note: Cleanliness is essential in bushcraft to prevent illness.
clean utensils,bushcraft food hygiene,hygiene practices

Cleaning Items (2) -

Clean Straw Hats with Lemon and Gum-Water
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Clean Straw Hats:

• Rub the soiled straw with a cut lemon.
• Wash off the juice with water.
• Stiffen the hat using gum-water.
straw hats,lemon,gum-water,cleaning
Cleanse Gloves with Benzine
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Cleanse Gloves:

• Use benzine as the best material for cleaning gloves.
• Apply benzine using a soft sponge or a piece of cloth.
gloves,cleaning,benzine,sponge

Disinfection Methods (2) -

Use Disinfectants to Prevent Spread of Contagious Diseases
High Priority Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Maintain thorough cleanliness in all areas where contagious or epidemic diseases may be present.
2. Ensure abundant air circulation to dilute and disperse any infectious agents.
3. Remove organic matter promptly using water or soap...
Procedure:
1. Maintain thorough cleanliness in all areas where contagious or epidemic diseases may be present.
2. Ensure abundant air circulation to dilute and disperse any infectious agents.
3. Remove organic matter promptly using water or soap and water.
4. Use lime for whitewashing, as it destroys organic matter and absorbs offensive gases.
5. Apply sulphurous acid to check organic change or fermentation.
6. Expose items to high temperatures (e.g., 240° Fahr.) to disinfect clothes, letters, etc.
7. Use Condy's Solution to destroy organic matter.
8. Utilize solutions of chloride of zinc, corrosive sublimate, persulphate or perchloride of iron to coagulate certain organic matters and prevent further decay; these also absorb sulphuretted hydrogen.
9. For deodorizing purposes, use chloride of lime (bleaching salt), chlorine, nitrate of lead, and copperas.
10. Use pastils, burned sugar vinegar, or burning tar to disguise offensive odors.

Notes:
• True prevention of disease spread relies on cleanliness, air circulation, and removal of organic matter.
disinfectants, sanitation, disease prevention, lime, sulphurous acid
Using Bleach for Disinfecting Without Heat
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure:
1. Dilute bleach (e.g., 1 part bleach to 9 parts water) for general disinfection.
2. Apply the diluted solution to the surface that needs disinfection.
3. Allow the solution to sit on the surface for at least 10 minutes to ensure...
Procedure:
1. Dilute bleach (e.g., 1 part bleach to 9 parts water) for general disinfection.
2. Apply the diluted solution to the surface that needs disinfection.
3. Allow the solution to sit on the surface for at least 10 minutes to ensure proper disinfection.
4. Rinse the surface thoroughly with clean water after the solution has been applied.
5. Wipe the surface clean with a sterile cloth or gauze after rinsing.
6. Repeat as needed, especially in areas where infection risk is high (e.g., dental instruments).
7. Ensure that the area is well-ventilated when using bleach to avoid inhalation risks.
bleach,disinfecting,dental tools,sanitation

Emergency Sanitation (2) -

Establish Proper Sanitation Procedures During Emergencies
High Priority Source: TACDA Emergency Food And Water
Procedure:

1. Maintain Hygiene Standards: Ensure that all individuals follow proper handwashing and personal hygiene practices to prevent the spread of disease.
2. Manage Waste Disposal: Establish a system for handling garbage, sewage,...
Procedure:

1. Maintain Hygiene Standards: Ensure that all individuals follow proper handwashing and personal hygiene practices to prevent the spread of disease.
2. Manage Waste Disposal: Establish a system for handling garbage, sewage, and other waste materials in a safe and hygienic manner, especially when normal sanitation methods are disrupted.
3. Secure Water Sources: Ensure that all water used for drinking, cooking, and washing is clean and uncontaminated. If necessary, use purification methods such as boiling or filtration.
4. Store Food Safely: Keep food in sealed containers to prevent contamination from pests or moisture. Avoid leaving food exposed where it can attract animals or insects.
5. Monitor Health Conditions: Watch for signs of illness or infection and take appropriate measures to treat or isolate affected individuals if necessary.

Why This Matters: Proper sanitation is critical during emergencies to prevent the spread of disease, maintain health, and avoid disastrous outcomes from poor hygiene practices.
emergency sanitation, waste management, hygiene during crisis, water safety, disease prevention
Maintain Sanitation in Emergency Situations
Standard Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Maintain Sanitation in Emergency Situations

During times of emergency, it is critical that sanitation be strictly observed in the cleaning of clothing, bedding materials, and all kitchen and food preparation utensils. Follow these steps:

1....
Maintain Sanitation in Emergency Situations

During times of emergency, it is critical that sanitation be strictly observed in the cleaning of clothing, bedding materials, and all kitchen and food preparation utensils. Follow these steps:

1. Keep all food in covered containers to prevent contamination.
2. Keep cooking and eating utensils clean. Diarrhea may result from dish soap that is not thoroughly rinsed from dishes.
3. Keep all garbage in a closed container or dispose of it outside the home when it is safe to go out. If possible, bury it. Avoid letting garbage or trash accumulate inside the shelter, both for fire and sanitation reasons.
4. Wash hands and utensils frequently to prevent illness.
5. Prepare only as much food as will be eaten at each meal to avoid waste and contamination.
6. Use paper cups and plates, paper towels, and napkins if the water supply is cut off to reduce the need for cleaning.
7. Keep refrigerators and home freezer units closed as much as possible once the services they depend on are cut off. The food they contain will keep longer if you plan your meals well in advance so that you can use the contents efficiently.
sanitation,emergency,cleaning,garbage disposal,toilet

Toilet Facilities (2) -

Construct and Maintain Toilet Rooms
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Procedure:

1. Ensure toilet rooms are conveniently located.
2. Construct toilet rooms using materials that can be easily cleaned and sanitized.
3. Provide adequate lighting in all toilet rooms.
4. Ensure toilet rooms are separately vented to...
Procedure:

1. Ensure toilet rooms are conveniently located.
2. Construct toilet rooms using materials that can be easily cleaned and sanitized.
3. Provide adequate lighting in all toilet rooms.
4. Ensure toilet rooms are separately vented to the outside.
5. Construct toilet rooms so they do not open directly into processing or storage areas.
6. Install tight-fitting, self-closing doors on all toilet rooms.
7. Post a sign directing employees to wash their hands before returning to work in all toilet rooms.
toilet room construction, sanitation standards, hand washing signs
Determine Minimum Number of Toilets Required
Reference Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Procedure:

1. Calculate the number of employees of the same sex.
2. Refer to Table 1-1 to determine the minimum number of toilets required based on the number of employees:
- 1-15 employees: 1 toilet
- 16-35 employees: 2 toilets
...
Procedure:

1. Calculate the number of employees of the same sex.
2. Refer to Table 1-1 to determine the minimum number of toilets required based on the number of employees:
- 1-15 employees: 1 toilet
- 16-35 employees: 2 toilets
- 36-55 employees: 3 toilets
- 56-80 employees: 4 toilets
- 81-110 employees: 5 toilets
- 111-150 employees: 6 toilets
- Over 150 employees: Add 1 additional fixture for each additional 40 employees.
3. If the facility is not used by women, urinals may be provided instead of toilets, but the number must not be reduced to less than two-thirds of the minimum specified.
toilets, privies, sanitation, employee count, minimum requirements

Equipment Design (2) -

Construct and Repair Equipment and Utensils for Sanitation
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Procedure:

1. Ensure all equipment and utensils are constructed of smooth, easily cleanable, and durable materials.
2. Food contact surfaces must be:
- Easily accessible for cleaning
- Nontoxic
- Corrosion-resistant
- Made from...
Procedure:

1. Ensure all equipment and utensils are constructed of smooth, easily cleanable, and durable materials.
2. Food contact surfaces must be:
- Easily accessible for cleaning
- Nontoxic
- Corrosion-resistant
- Made from nonabsorbent material
3. Solder used on food contact surfaces must be hard solder that is nontoxic under use conditions and free from toxic materials such as antimony, bismuth, cadmium, lead, zinc, or others.
4. Ensure equipment is located to provide adequate space for cleaning, maintenance, and inspection.
equipment construction, food contact surfaces, corrosion resistance
Design and Use of Equipment and Utensils to Prevent Contamination
Standard Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Procedure:

1. Ensure all equipment and utensils are designed and constructed to prevent contamination.
2. Avoid the use of toxic lubricants, fuel, metal fragments, or contaminated water on contact surfaces.
3. Use only edible and nontoxic...
Procedure:

1. Ensure all equipment and utensils are designed and constructed to prevent contamination.
2. Avoid the use of toxic lubricants, fuel, metal fragments, or contaminated water on contact surfaces.
3. Use only edible and nontoxic lubricants on moving parts of pumps, product handling, and processing equipment.
4. Apply lubricants sparingly to minimize risk of contamination.
equipment design, contamination prevention, lubricants

Cleaning Procedures (2) -

Clean and Organize Hut Floors, Surfaces, and Furniture
