Environmental Cleaning (1) -

Cleaning the Space for Birth or Medical Procedures at Home
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Sweep the area where the baby will be born or where procedures such as a pelvic exam or IUD insertion will take place, to remove dust and dirt.
2. Wash surfaces with soap and water to ensure they are clean.
3. Place tools...
Procedure:

1. Sweep the area where the baby will be born or where procedures such as a pelvic exam or IUD insertion will take place, to remove dust and dirt.
2. Wash surfaces with soap and water to ensure they are clean.
3. Place tools or birth kits on a clean surface.
4. Move animals out of the house, and avoid performing any medical procedures in areas where animals sleep or pass stool, or where people urinate or defecate.
5. If the floor is made of animal waste (dung), do not let the woman’s body or tools touch it; dung contains many germs that can spread to pregnant women.
6. Cover the floor with clean straw, cloth, or plastic if necessary.
home birth hygiene, infection prevention, environmental cleaning, sanitation, midwifery practices

Institutional Cleaning (1) -

Cleaning the Space for Birth or Medical Procedures in a Hospital or Clinic
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Be extra careful with hygiene in hospitals and clinics due to higher germ transmission risk.
2. After each birth, wash floors and surfaces thoroughly.
3. If possible, use a 5% bleach solution (sodium hypochlorite) to...
Procedure:

1. Be extra careful with hygiene in hospitals and clinics due to higher germ transmission risk.
2. After each birth, wash floors and surfaces thoroughly.
3. If possible, use a 5% bleach solution (sodium hypochlorite) to clean the floor.
4. Ensure that the cleaning solution is made fresh daily, as it loses effectiveness over time.
hospital hygiene, infection control, institutional cleaning, bleach solution, medical sanitation

Water Protection (1) -

Wash-Up Practices to Protect Water Sources
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Title: Wash-Up Practices to Protect Water Sources

Procedure:
1. Wash dishes, toothpaste, and soap at least 50 metres away from lakes and streams.
2. Scatter wash water so that it filters through the soil before returning to the stream.
3....
Title: Wash-Up Practices to Protect Water Sources

Procedure:
1. Wash dishes, toothpaste, and soap at least 50 metres away from lakes and streams.
2. Scatter wash water so that it filters through the soil before returning to the stream.
3. Avoid washing up directly under the tap of a rainwater tank or in stock troughs on pastoral properties.
4. Do not dispose of food scraps into lakes, streams, or other natural water sources.
5. Use biodegradable detergents if available, but be aware that even these can harm aquatic life.
wash-up practices, water protection, environmental impact

Toilet Practices (1) -

Proper Toileting Practices in the Wilderness
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Title: Proper Toileting Practices in the Wilderness

Procedure:
1. Use existing toilets if available.
2. In areas without toilets, bury faecal waste at least 100 metres away from campsites and watercourses.
3. Dig a hole 15 cm deep within...
Title: Proper Toileting Practices in the Wilderness

Procedure:
1. Use existing toilets if available.
2. In areas without toilets, bury faecal waste at least 100 metres away from campsites and watercourses.
3. Dig a hole 15 cm deep within the soil’s organic layer using a hand trowel or similar tool.
4. Bury all faecal waste in the hole to minimize environmental impact.
5. Ensure that you do not leave any traces of your presence, such as toilet paper or other materials.
wilderness toileting, faecal waste disposal, sanitation practices

Radiation Decontamination (1) -

Decontamination of Patients in Radiation Exposure Scenarios
High Priority Source: Emergency War Surgery
Patients entering a medical treatment facility should be routinely decontaminated if monitoring for radiation is not available.

This step is critical to prevent the spread of radioactive contamination within the facility and among healthcare...
Patients entering a medical treatment facility should be routinely decontaminated if monitoring for radiation is not available.

This step is critical to prevent the spread of radioactive contamination within the facility and among healthcare workers.
decontamination,radiation,patient safety

Hygiene In Internment Facilities (1) -

Enforce Field Hygiene and Sanitation Principles in Internment Facilities
High Priority Source: Emergency War Surgery
- The internment facility must enforce field hygiene and sanitation principles.

• Plan for personal hygiene requirements and protective measures (insect netting, insect repellent, sunscreen).
field hygiene,sanitation,internment facilities,hygiene planning

Preventive Medicine Support (1) -

Coordinate with Supporting Medical HQ for Preventive Medicine and Veterinary Services Support
High Priority Source: Emergency War Surgery
- Coordinate with the supporting medical HQ for additional Preventive Medicine support (pest management, potable water, dining facility sanitation, and waste disposal) and Veterinary Services support for food safety as required.
medical coordination,preventive medicine,veterinary services,food safety

Hygiene In Conflict Zones (1) -

Maintain Hygiene and Prevent Disease in Conflict Zones
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Hygiene and Disease Prevention Steps:

1. Handwashing:
- Wash hands with soap and clean water before preparing food, after using the toilet, and after handling casualties.

2. Water Safety:
- Use boiled or treated water for...
Hygiene and Disease Prevention Steps:

1. Handwashing:
- Wash hands with soap and clean water before preparing food, after using the toilet, and after handling casualties.

2. Water Safety:
- Use boiled or treated water for drinking and cooking to prevent waterborne diseases.
- If no safe water is available, use purification tablets or filters.

3. Waste Disposal:
- Dispose of human waste in designated latrines or pits away from water sources.
- Bury solid waste properly to avoid attracting pests and spreading disease.

4. Insect Control:
- Use insect repellent and wear long sleeves and pants to prevent mosquito bites.
- Eliminate standing water around the area to reduce breeding sites for mosquitoes.

5. Food Safety:
- Cook food thoroughly, especially meat and eggs.
- Avoid consuming raw or undercooked food in areas with poor sanitation.
handwashing, water purification, waste management, disease prevention, food safety

Post Closure Disposal (1) -

Closing and Disposal Procedures for First Aid Posts
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Post Closure Procedures

• When a First Aid post closes, the premises must be cleaned thoroughly.
• Rubbish should be disposed of correctly:
- Single-use items such as gloves and needles should be placed in disposable containers and...
### Post Closure Procedures

• When a First Aid post closes, the premises must be cleaned thoroughly.
• Rubbish should be disposed of correctly:
- Single-use items such as gloves and needles should be placed in disposable containers and burnt.
• Ensure that all equipment and supplies are inventoried, stored properly, and their use is monitored throughout the operation.
disposal procedures, post closure, sanitation

Emergency Defecation (1) -

Temporary Defecation Methods in Emergencies
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Temporary Defecation Methods in Emergencies

• For individual needs and during a short period of time (i.e., one or two days), you can dig small holes to defecate into; cover stools with soil.
temporary sanitation, emergency defecation, hygiene

Water Contamination Survival (1) -

Understand Giardia Survival in the Environment
Standard Source: giardia factsheet
Giardia cysts can survive for varying lengths of time depending on environmental conditions:

• Cold water (below 10°C / 50°F): Cysts can remain viable for up to 2 to 3 months.
• Moderate temperature (21°C / 70°F): Cysts may survive for...
Giardia cysts can survive for varying lengths of time depending on environmental conditions:

• Cold water (below 10°C / 50°F): Cysts can remain viable for up to 2 to 3 months.
• Moderate temperature (21°C / 70°F): Cysts may survive for almost one month.
• High temperatures: At 54°C (129°F), Giardia cysts are killed in 10 minutes. Boiling water immediately kills all Giardia cysts.
• Freezing and thawing: A small fraction of Giardia cysts can survive a single freeze-thaw cycle.

These survival times highlight the importance of proper water treatment, especially in environments with fluctuating temperatures or where contamination is likely.
Giardia survival, environmental conditions, temperature effects, water treatment

Zoonotic Transmission (1) -

Understand Giardia Transmission Between Animal Species
Standard Source: giardia factsheet
Transmission of Giardia between animal species:

• Some Giardia isolates exhibit high host specificity, meaning they infect only certain animals.
• Other isolates may be able to infect multiple hosts, including humans.
• **Beavers and...
Transmission of Giardia between animal species:

• Some Giardia isolates exhibit high host specificity, meaning they infect only certain animals.
• Other isolates may be able to infect multiple hosts, including humans.
• Beavers and muskrats are known sources of human infection, suggesting a potential zoonotic transmission pathway.

Precautions:

• Avoid contact with feces from potentially infected animals (especially beavers and muskrats) in natural water sources.
• Practice good hygiene when handling or cleaning up after animals.
• In areas where Giardia is prevalent, consider using protective gear such as gloves and masks during activities that may expose you to animal waste.
Giardia transmission, zoonotic, beaver, muskrat, host specificity

Transport Decontamination (1) -

Transportation Decontamination for BW Casualties
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Transportation decontamination is essential to prevent the spread of biological warfare (BW) agents during evacuation.

• Key Considerations:
- All personnel suspected of being contaminated must be cleaned before entering an...
Transportation decontamination is essential to prevent the spread of biological warfare (BW) agents during evacuation.

• Key Considerations:
- All personnel suspected of being contaminated must be cleaned before entering an uncontaminated transport vehicle.
- Only personnel in full protective posture should operate a contaminated vehicle.
- Casualties must be transported to a decontamination station for cleaning prior to entering uncontaminated conveyances or treatment units.

Action Steps:
1. Decontaminate all suspected BW casualties before transport.
2. Ensure that only personnel in full protective gear handle contaminated vehicles or equipment.
3. Forward evacuation within the combat zone is typically managed by component commands using organic service-assigned assets.
4. Use specialized aeromedical evacuation crews for forward evacuation if terrain and distance make it necessary.
transport decontamination,bw casualties,protective gear,evacuation

Water Decontamination (1) -

Decontamination of Water Supplies
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Assess water contamination based on exposure to chemical agents:
- Open water sources subjected to chemical attack should be considered contaminated until tested.
- Deep well water is safe if the well mouth is covered.
...
Procedure:

1. Assess water contamination based on exposure to chemical agents:
- Open water sources subjected to chemical attack should be considered contaminated until tested.
- Deep well water is safe if the well mouth is covered.
- Closed metal tank water is safe if the tap and air inlets are decontaminated.
2. Use appropriate methods for decontamination:
- Filtration: In small-scale emergencies, run water through a spare unused respirator canister at a slow flow rate (drop by drop). Discard any initial water that flows faster than this. Limit to 5 liters per canister.
- Superchlorination: For small quantities (e.g., 1 liter), use superchlorination. Simple chlorination is not sufficient for chemical agent decontamination.
- Flocculation: Treat larger quantities with metal salts, followed by chlorine treatment.
- Reverse Osmosis: An effective method for removing contamination, including heavy metals.
water decontamination, chemical agents, filtration, superchlorination

Food Transportation (1) -

Transport Food Safely in the Field
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Objective: Ensure food is transported safely to prevent contamination and spoilage.

Steps:
1. Use clean vehicles for transporting food, and ensure they are completely enclosed, if possible.
2. Protect food from contamination by...
Objective: Ensure food is transported safely to prevent contamination and spoilage.

Steps:
1. Use clean vehicles for transporting food, and ensure they are completely enclosed, if possible.
2. Protect food from contamination by using clean tarpaulins, boxes, or bags to shield it from dust, dirt, and the elements.
3. Do not use vehicles that have previously transported garbage, trash, chemicals, petroleum products, or similar materials for transporting food unless they are properly cleaned and sanitized.
4. For bulk quantities of perishable foods being transported over long distances, use refrigerated containers to maintain proper temperatures.
5. Stock perishable food products at a level that is commensurate with the capacity of the unit’s food service storage facilities.

Warnings/Contraindications:
• Using unclean or improperly sanitized vehicles for transporting food can lead to contamination and spoilage, increasing the risk of illness.
transport food, vehicle sanitation, refrigerated containers, food contamination, perishable foods

Vermin Control (1) -

Conduct Regular Sanitation Inspections of Storage Areas
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Procedure:

1. All storage spaces must be inspected regularly for evidence of vermin infestation.
2. Ensure that food is stored in a manner that minimizes the risk of contamination or spoilage.
3. Address any signs of pests immediately to...
Procedure:

1. All storage spaces must be inspected regularly for evidence of vermin infestation.
2. Ensure that food is stored in a manner that minimizes the risk of contamination or spoilage.
3. Address any signs of pests immediately to prevent further infestation and damage to supplies.
storage inspection, vermin control, pest prevention

Food Service Hygiene (1) -

Cleaning and Sanitizing Food Preparation Equipment in Mess Facilities
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Procedure for Cleaning and Sanitizing Food Preparation Equipment in Mess Facilities

1. Thoroughly clean and sanitize vat cans, ovens, stoves, grills, and other food preparation and serving equipment after each meal period.
2. **Make all...
Procedure for Cleaning and Sanitizing Food Preparation Equipment in Mess Facilities

1. Thoroughly clean and sanitize vat cans, ovens, stoves, grills, and other food preparation and serving equipment after each meal period.
2. Make all needed repairs as soon as practicable to maintain functionality and hygiene.
3. Clean and sanitize all food contact surfaces according to the guidelines in Chapter 1 of this manual.
4. Install all food service equipment off the ground to protect from contamination by dust and vermin.
5. Cover wooden surfaces with clean, heavy wrapping paper or waxed paper after each meal period. If paper is not available, wipe down and scrub surfaces with an approved sanitizing solution and air dry them thoroughly.
6. Follow pesticide label directions strictly when using pesticides in food service areas. Ensure that all food and food contact surfaces are properly protected during spraying and dusting operations. Pesticides should never be stored in food service areas, and only certified personnel may apply them. Refer to Chapter 8 of this manual for detailed information on pesticide application and pest control programs.
7. Encourage the use of disposable eating utensils to reduce disease risk and conserve water/fuel, despite the increased solid waste disposal challenge.
cleaning,sanitizing,mess facilities,food preparation equipment,pesticides

Food Storage Hygiene (1) -

Proper Use and Maintenance of Insulated Food Containers (Vat Cans)
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Procedure for Proper Use and Maintenance of Insulated Food Containers (Vat Cans)

1. Use three aluminum inserts in each insulated food container, ensuring they are filled to capacity (5 2/3 quarts) with either hot or cold food.
2. **Separate...
Procedure for Proper Use and Maintenance of Insulated Food Containers (Vat Cans)

1. Use three aluminum inserts in each insulated food container, ensuring they are filled to capacity (5 2/3 quarts) with either hot or cold food.
2. Separate containers must be used for hot and cold foods, placing inserts of hot food in one container and inserts of cold food in another.
3. Preparation, filling, pre-heating, and prechilling should be done before inserting food into the container:
- Fill the container with 2 quarts of boiling water, ice water, or crushed ice.
- Cover the container and let it stand for 30 minutes before use.
4. Label each container across the top of the lid with the item name, date and time of preparation, and number of servings.
5. Discard any food held for over four hours, as it must be treated as garbage to prevent spoilage and disease risk.
6. Clean vat cans before and after every use:
- Do not immerse vat cans in water.
- Remove inserts and rubber gaskets, then wash them with soap and water.
- Rinse the gaskets in boiling water.
- Place the gaskets flat side down on the container to dry to prevent warping.
insulated food containers,vat cans,preparation,cleaning,sanitizing

Oral Hygiene (1) -

Rinsing the Mouth for Oral Health and During Pregnancy
Reference Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure:

1. Prepare a salt water solution: mix 1 teaspoon of salt in 250 ml (about 1 cup) of warm water.
2. Use this solution to rinse the mouth several times a day, especially after meals or when experiencing oral pain (page 7,...
Procedure:

1. Prepare a salt water solution: mix 1 teaspoon of salt in 250 ml (about 1 cup) of warm water.
2. Use this solution to rinse the mouth several times a day, especially after meals or when experiencing oral pain (page 7, 178).
3. During pregnancy, rinse with salt water to maintain oral hygiene and reduce the risk of infection (page 16, 178).
4. Alternatively, use hydrogen peroxide diluted in water for rinsing (page 8, 179).
5. Avoid using harsh or acidic solutions that may irritate the mouth.
6. Encourage regular rinsing, especially if the patient has a history of oral infections or is pregnant.
mouth rinse,salt water,pregnancy,hydrogen peroxide

Community Assessment (1) -

Assess Housing and Sanitation Conditions in the Community
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Objective: Evaluate housing structures and sanitation practices that may impact health.

Steps:
1. Housing materials: Ask what materials are used for walls, floors, and roofs of homes.
2. Cleanliness: Determine if houses are kept...
Objective: Evaluate housing structures and sanitation practices that may impact health.

Steps:
1. Housing materials: Ask what materials are used for walls, floors, and roofs of homes.
2. Cleanliness: Determine if houses are kept clean and how cleanliness is maintained.
3. Cooking areas: Identify where cooking takes place and whether it is done on the floor or in designated spaces.
4. Ventilation: Assess how smoke escapes from homes (e.g., through windows, chimneys, etc.).
5. Sleeping arrangements: Find out what people sleep on (mats, beds, etc.) and where they are located within the home.
6. Pest control: Inquire about the presence of pests such as flies, fleas, bedbugs, or rats and how these are managed.
7. Food storage: Determine how food is protected from contamination and what improvements could be made.
8. Animals in homes: Identify if animals like dogs, chickens, or pigs are kept inside houses and the impact they have on health.
9. Animal diseases: Ask about common animal illnesses and their effects on human health.
10. Water sources: Find out where families obtain water and whether it is safe for drinking.
11. Sanitation facilities: Determine how many families have latrines, how many use them properly, and the overall cleanliness of the village.
12. Waste disposal: Identify where garbage is disposed of and the reasons behind these practices.
housing conditions, sanitation assessment, pest control, food storage, water safety, latrine use, waste disposal

Water Cleanliness (1) -

Maintain Cleanliness of Public Water Sources
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Purpose: To prevent contamination and ensure safe drinking water for the community.

Procedure:
1. Keep wells and public water holes clean.
2. Do not let animals go near where people get drinking water.
3. If necessary, put a fence around...
Purpose: To prevent contamination and ensure safe drinking water for the community.

Procedure:
1. Keep wells and public water holes clean.
2. Do not let animals go near where people get drinking water.
3. If necessary, put a fence around the place to keep animals out.
4. Do not defecate or throw garbage near the water hole.
5. Take special care to keep rivers and streams clean upstream from any place where drinking water is taken.

Notes: Contaminated water can lead to serious illness. Maintaining cleanliness of water sources is critical.
public water cleanliness, sanitation, contamination prevention

Latrine Maintenance (1) -

Maintain a Closed Latrine for Hygiene and Functionality
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Daily maintenance:
• Keep the latrine clean: Wash the slab regularly with water.
• Cover the hole: Ensure that the hole in the slab has a cover, which can be made of wood or other suitable material.
• Secure the cover: Keep the lid...
Daily maintenance:
• Keep the latrine clean: Wash the slab regularly with water.
• Cover the hole: Ensure that the hole in the slab has a cover, which can be made of wood or other suitable material.
• Secure the cover: Keep the lid in place to prevent flies and odors from escaping.

Additional tips:
• If using a fly-trapping VIP latrine, ensure the ventilation pipe is covered with fly screen (wire screen preferred) and that the out-house structure remains dark inside.
latrine maintenance, hygiene, fly-proofing, sanitation practices

Cold Weather Decontamination (1) -

Decontamination in Low Temperatures
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Recognize that low temperatures make decontamination of personnel more difficult.
2. Modify standard procedures to use field-expedient methods when bathing facilities and a change of clothing are not available.
3. Ensure...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that low temperatures make decontamination of personnel more difficult.
2. Modify standard procedures to use field-expedient methods when bathing facilities and a change of clothing are not available.
3. Ensure contaminated personnel receive adequate decontamination even in cold conditions.

Details:
• Field expedient methods may be necessary due to environmental constraints.
• Decontamination is critical for safety, even in extreme cold.
decontamination,cold weather,field expedient methods,bathing facilities

Burial Procedures (1) -

Burial Procedures in Northern Areas
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

• In northern areas with harsh weather and terrain conditions, burial of the dead is difficult.
• Documentation and Storage: The deceased must be:
- Documented properly
- Placed in "body bags" as per current regulations
-...
Procedure:

• In northern areas with harsh weather and terrain conditions, burial of the dead is difficult.
• Documentation and Storage: The deceased must be:
- Documented properly
- Placed in "body bags" as per current regulations
• Evacuation: Bodies should be evacuated rearward on logistic transport for proper handling.
northern burial,body bags,evacuation,logistic transport,documentation

Tent Safety (1) -

Prevent Tent-Eye Injuries in Shelters
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Tent-eye injuries are caused by improperly maintained stoves and lamps, as well as poor ventilation.

Key considerations:
• Ensure that all stoves, lamps, and other heat sources within shelters are properly maintained and cleaned...
Tent-eye injuries are caused by improperly maintained stoves and lamps, as well as poor ventilation.

Key considerations:
• Ensure that all stoves, lamps, and other heat sources within shelters are properly maintained and cleaned regularly.
• Provide adequate ventilation in tents and shelters to prevent the buildup of harmful fumes.
• Encourage personnel to leave the shelter for fresh air at regular intervals.
• Maintain discipline within tent groups to ensure cleanliness and proper use of equipment.
tent-eye,ventilation,sanitation,cold,weather

Surface Disinfection (1) -

Disinfect Surfaces and Equipment in the Field
Standard Source: FM 21 10 Field Hygiene and Sanitation
### Procedure: Disinfect Surfaces and Equipment in the Field

Steps:
1. Identify surfaces or equipment that require disinfection, such as cooking utensils, first aid kits, latrines, and personal items.
2. Gather necessary materials,...
### Procedure: Disinfect Surfaces and Equipment in the Field

Steps:
1. Identify surfaces or equipment that require disinfection, such as cooking utensils, first aid kits, latrines, and personal items.
2. Gather necessary materials, including soap, water, alcohol-based wipes, bleach solution (1 part bleach to 9 parts water), or other approved disinfectants.
3. Wipe down surfaces with a clean cloth soaked in the disinfectant solution, ensuring full coverage and allowing it to sit for at least 2 minutes.
4. Rinse surfaces thoroughly with clean water after disinfection, especially if they will be used for food preparation or contact with skin.
5. Dry surfaces completely using a clean cloth or allow them to air dry in a well-ventilated area.
6. Store disinfected items in a sealed container or covered area to prevent recontamination.
7. Repeat the process regularly, especially after handling waste, bodily fluids, or contaminated materials.
8. Dispose of used cloths and wipes properly, following local sanitation guidelines.
9. Monitor for signs of contamination, such as unusual odors, visible dirt, or complaints from personnel using the equipment.
10. Train all personnel on proper disinfection procedures to ensure consistency and effectiveness.

Details:
• If disinfectants are unavailable, use boiled water or sunlight exposure for sterilization where possible.
• For metal tools or weapons, use a mild detergent solution followed by thorough rinsing and drying to prevent rust and corrosion.
• In cold environments, ensure that surfaces are fully dry before storing to prevent freezing damage.

Warnings:
• Never mix bleach with other chemicals, such as ammonia or vinegar, as this can produce toxic fumes.
• Avoid using disinfectants on sensitive materials, such as certain fabrics or electronics, unless they are specifically approved for use.
disinfection, surface cleaning, field hygiene, equipment sanitation, bleach solution

Shelter Maintenance (1) -

Proper Shelter Maintenance in the Field
Standard Source: FM 21 10 Field Hygiene and Sanitation
Procedure:
1. Ensure that all shelters are constructed with clean materials to prevent contamination.
2. Regularly inspect shelters for signs of damage or wear and repair as needed.
3. Keep shelters dry and well-ventilated to prevent the growth...
Procedure:
1. Ensure that all shelters are constructed with clean materials to prevent contamination.
2. Regularly inspect shelters for signs of damage or wear and repair as needed.
3. Keep shelters dry and well-ventilated to prevent the growth of mold and bacteria.
4. Store personal items in airtight containers to prevent contamination by insects, rodents, and other pests.
5. Wash hands with soap and water before entering or exiting shelters.

Details:
• Shelters should be constructed at least 1 meter above ground level to prevent contamination by moisture and pests.
• If no airtight containers are available, use natural materials like leaves or bark to cover personal items.
• In cold climates, ensure that shelters are insulated to maintain warmth and prevent the growth of bacteria and fungi.
shelter maintenance, sanitation, field conditions, hygiene

Campsite Hygiene (1) -

Maintain a Campsite Sanitation Area
Standard Source: FM 21 10 Field Hygiene and Sanitation
Steps to Maintain a Campsite Sanitation Area:

1. Designate a specific area for sanitation purposes, ensuring it is at least 50 feet away from water sources and food preparation areas.
2. Construct or use existing latrines in the...
Steps to Maintain a Campsite Sanitation Area:

1. Designate a specific area for sanitation purposes, ensuring it is at least 50 feet away from water sources and food preparation areas.
2. Construct or use existing latrines in the designated area, following proper construction guidelines.
3. Provide handwashing facilities, such as a container with clean water and soap, near the sanitation area.
4. Ensure that waste is disposed of properly, using the designated pits or latrines and covering them with at least 6 inches of dirt after each use.
5. Store unused waste materials in sealed containers if possible, especially during transport or when moving locations.
6. Clean and disinfect tools used for excavation or waste removal with soap and water after each use.
7. Rotate pits regularly, allowing time for waste to decompose before reusing them.
8. Monitor the sanitation area regularly, ensuring that it remains functional and is not overflowing.
9. Educate all users on proper sanitation practices to maintain hygiene standards.
10. Dispose of contaminated soil or materials properly, following local regulations if applicable.

Additional Tips:
• Use a shovel or other digging tool for excavation.
• If no latrine is available, use a tarp or plastic sheet as a temporary containment area.
• Ensure that the pit is deep enough to prevent contamination of groundwater.

Warnings:
• Do not dispose of waste near water sources or campsites.
• Avoid using contaminated soil for covering waste.
• Seek medical attention immediately if signs of infection (e.g., redness, swelling, fever) appear.
campsite sanitation, hygiene practices, waste management, field conditions, infection prevention

Camp Setup (1) -

Establish a Sanitary Camp Site
Standard Source: FM 21 10 Field Hygiene and Sanitation
Steps to Establish a Sanitary Camp Site:

1. Choose a campsite that is elevated and well-drained, at least 50 meters away from water sources.
2. Clear the area of debris, animal droppings, and other potential contaminants.
3. Dig latrines at...
Steps to Establish a Sanitary Camp Site:

1. Choose a campsite that is elevated and well-drained, at least 50 meters away from water sources.
2. Clear the area of debris, animal droppings, and other potential contaminants.
3. Dig latrines at least 50 meters away from food preparation areas and water sources.
4. Construct latrines with a pit covered by a board or tarp to prevent contamination.
5. Use designated areas for waste disposal and ensure that all human waste is buried properly.
6. Set up a washing area near the campsite, using clean water and soap if available.
7. Store food in sealed containers and keep it separate from waste and cooking areas.
8. Ensure that all personnel are trained on proper sanitation practices.
9. Regularly inspect the campsite for signs of contamination or pests.
10. Maintain a logbook to track sanitation activities and any issues encountered.

Warnings:
• Never use the same area for waste disposal and food preparation.
• Avoid using water sources that are visibly contaminated.
• Ensure that all personnel follow hygiene protocols consistently.
camp setup,latrines,waste disposal,hygiene,site selection

Water Inspection (1) -

Inspect and Maintain Water Sources in the Field
Standard Source: FM 21 10 Field Hygiene and Sanitation
Steps to Inspect and Maintain Water Sources:

1. Regularly inspect water sources for signs of contamination or spoilage.
2. Use clean, sealed containers for storing water and label them clearly.
3. Test water quality regularly using appropriate...
Steps to Inspect and Maintain Water Sources:

1. Regularly inspect water sources for signs of contamination or spoilage.
2. Use clean, sealed containers for storing water and label them clearly.
3. Test water quality regularly using appropriate testing kits or methods.
4. Disinfect water sources as needed using boiling, chemical treatment, or filtration.
5. Store disinfected water in cool, dry places to prevent spoilage.
6. Ensure that all personnel are trained on proper water source maintenance protocols.
7. Maintain a logbook to track water quality tests and any issues encountered.
8. Use first-in, first-out (FIFO) principles when using stored water to ensure freshness.
9. Discard expired or spoiled water immediately to prevent the spread of disease.
10. Regularly inspect and clean water storage containers to prevent contamination.

Warnings:
• Never use contaminated water for drinking or cooking.
• Follow manufacturer instructions carefully when using chemical treatments for water disinfection.
• Ensure that all personnel follow hygiene protocols consistently.
water inspection,disinfection,storage containers,sanitation,hygiene

Medical Waste (1) -

Handle Medical Waste in the Field
Standard Source: FM 21 10 Field Hygiene and Sanitation
Steps to Handle Medical Waste:

1. Use designated containers for storing medical waste and ensure they are clearly labeled.
2. Separate infectious waste from non-infectious waste for proper disposal.
3. Store medical waste at a safe distance...
Steps to Handle Medical Waste:

1. Use designated containers for storing medical waste and ensure they are clearly labeled.
2. Separate infectious waste from non-infectious waste for proper disposal.
3. Store medical waste at a safe distance from food preparation areas, water sources, and living quarters.
4. Disinfect medical waste containers regularly to prevent the spread of disease.
5. Use gloves and protective clothing when handling medical waste to protect against contamination.
6. Ensure that all personnel are trained on proper medical waste disposal protocols.
7. Maintain a logbook to track medical waste management activities and any issues encountered.
8. Transport medical waste to designated disposal sites following local regulations.
9. Discard expired or spoiled medical waste immediately to prevent the spread of disease.
10. Regularly inspect medical waste storage areas for signs of contamination or pests.

Warnings:
• Never dispose of infectious waste near water sources or food preparation areas.
• Follow manufacturer instructions carefully when using chemical treatments for disinfection.
• Ensure that all personnel follow hygiene protocols consistently.
medical waste,disposal,hygiene,sanitation,infectious waste

Field Operations (1) -

Establish a Sanitation Plan for Field Operations
Standard Source: FM 21 10 Field Hygiene and Sanitation
1. Identify the location and size of the area to be occupied.
2. Determine the number of personnel and their daily needs (waste, water, etc.).
3. Select appropriate sanitation facilities based on terrain, climate, and availability of resources.
4....
1. Identify the location and size of the area to be occupied.
2. Determine the number of personnel and their daily needs (waste, water, etc.).
3. Select appropriate sanitation facilities based on terrain, climate, and availability of resources.
4. Ensure that all sanitation facilities are located at least 100 feet from food preparation areas and water sources.
5. Establish a schedule for cleaning and maintaining sanitation facilities.
6. Assign personnel to oversee sanitation operations and ensure compliance with hygiene standards.
7. Provide training on proper waste disposal, handwashing, and personal hygiene practices.
8. Monitor the effectiveness of the sanitation plan and make adjustments as needed.
sanitation, field operations, hygiene, waste disposal, training

Insect Avoidance (1) -

Avoid Dangerous Insects and Animals
Standard Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion and Recovery Multiservice Procedures
To avoid dangerous insects and animals:

1. Avoid hairy insects; the hairs could cause irritation or infection.
2. Avoid poisonous insects, such as:
- Centipedes.
- Scorpions.
- Poisonous spiders.
3. **Avoid disease-carrying...
To avoid dangerous insects and animals:

1. Avoid hairy insects; the hairs could cause irritation or infection.
2. Avoid poisonous insects, such as:
- Centipedes.
- Scorpions.
- Poisonous spiders.
3. Avoid disease-carrying insects, such as:
- Flies.
- Mosquitoes.
- Ticks.
avoid insects, disease prevention, poisonous animals

Radiation Hygiene (1) -

Personal Hygiene and Radiation Protection
Critical Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion Recovery
Objective: Maintain personal hygiene to reduce the risk of radiation exposure and infection.

Steps for Personal Hygiene:
1. Wash entire body with soap and any water: Pay close attention to fingernails and hairy parts.
2. If no water is...
Objective: Maintain personal hygiene to reduce the risk of radiation exposure and infection.

Steps for Personal Hygiene:
1. Wash entire body with soap and any water: Pay close attention to fingernails and hairy parts.
2. If no water is available, wipe all exposed skin surfaces with a clean cloth or uncontaminated soil.
3. In fallout/dusty conditions: Keep the entire body covered. Use a handkerchief or cloth over the mouth and nose. Improvise goggles if possible.
4. Do not smoke in contaminated areas.

Daily Radiation Time Table (for no rate meter):
• 4-6 hours: Complete isolation
• 3-7 hours: Brief exposure (maximum 30 minutes)
• 8 hours: Brief exposure (maximum 1 hour)
• 9-12 hours: 2-4 hours of exposure per day
• 13+ hours: Normal movement allowed

Important Notes:
• Keep contaminated materials out of shelter.
• In good weather, bury contaminated clothing outside the shelter and recover later.
• In bad weather, shake or beat wet clothing with branches. Rinse and/or shake; do not wring out.
• Pick a shelter as soon as possible—5 minutes unsheltered is maximum!
nuclear hygiene, radiation protection, personal sanitation

Radiation Cleaning (1) -

Handling Contaminated Clothing and Equipment in Nuclear Conditions
Standard Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion Recovery
Objective: Safely manage contaminated clothing and equipment to reduce radiation exposure.

Steps for Handling Contaminated Items:
1. Leave contaminated equipment and clothing near shelter for retrieval after radioactive decay.
2. In...
Objective: Safely manage contaminated clothing and equipment to reduce radiation exposure.

Steps for Handling Contaminated Items:
1. Leave contaminated equipment and clothing near shelter for retrieval after radioactive decay.
2. In good weather:
- Bury contaminated clothing outside of the shelter (recover later).
3. In bad weather:
- Shake strongly or beat with branches to remove contaminants.
- Rinse and/or shake wet clothing.
- Do not wring out contaminated clothing.
4. Keep contaminated materials out of shelter at all times.

Important Notes:
• Bury clothing in good weather to allow decay before recovery.
• Use shaking or rinsing in bad weather to remove contaminants without spreading them further.
• Avoid bringing contaminated items into the shelter to prevent recontamination.
nuclear clothing, radiation decon, emergency cleaning

Urban Sanitation (1) -

Maintaining Sanitation Systems in Urban Areas
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Maintaining Sanitation Systems:

1. Identify urban areas with sophisticated sanitation systems and assess their condition.
2. If these systems are destroyed, implement emergency measures to prevent the deterioration of sanitary...
Procedure for Maintaining Sanitation Systems:

1. Identify urban areas with sophisticated sanitation systems and assess their condition.
2. If these systems are destroyed, implement emergency measures to prevent the deterioration of sanitary conditions.
3. Ensure that sanitation systems are maintained to avoid epidemics and reduce the burden on biological-decon capabilities.
4. Control contaminated water and residue to prevent hazards to civilians or interference with sanitation systems.
5. Coordinate with engineer support if necessary to construct controlled runoff areas for managing waste and contamination.

Important Considerations:
• The destruction of sanitation systems can lead to severe public health risks.
• Engineer support may be required for large-scale decontamination efforts.
sanitation, urban areas, epidemics, controlled runoff, engineer support

Foot Hygiene (1) -

Maintain Foot Hygiene in Cold Mountain Environments
Standard Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
Foot hygiene is essential to prevent cold injuries and blisters in high mountain environments.

### Steps:
1. Lace boots tightly when climbing for support, but not so tight that circulation is constricted.
2. Wear socks without wrinkles...
Foot hygiene is essential to prevent cold injuries and blisters in high mountain environments.

### Steps:
1. Lace boots tightly when climbing for support, but not so tight that circulation is constricted.
2. Wear socks without wrinkles to avoid blisters on the feet.
3. Wash feet daily, and keep them as dry and clean as possible.
4. If regular foot washing is impossible:
- Change socks often (at halts, rest periods, or at least once a day).
- Massage, dry, and sprinkle foot powder on the feet.
- Use talc or antifungal powder during massage; brush off excess to avoid clumping that may cause blisters.
5. If washing with snow is necessary, dry feet quickly afterward.
6. When changing socks, examine feet for wrinkles, cracks, blisters, and discoloration.
7. Trim toenails but not too short; long nails wear out socks, while short nails do not provide proper support for the toes.
8. Seek medical attention immediately if any foot problems are suspected.

### Additional Notes:
• Use aluminum chlorohydrate antiperspirant on feet two to three times a day for a week, then once daily for the rest of winter.
• If fissures or cracks occur in the feet, discontinue spraying until they heal or reduce frequency to control sweating.
foot hygiene, blisters prevention, cold injuries, antiperspirant use, mountain environment

Insect Repellent (1) -

Carry Insect Repellent for Mountain Climbing
Reference Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
Insect repellent is essential to prevent insect bites and related health issues during mountain climbing.

1. Choose a repellent that is effective against the types of insects encountered in the area.
2. Apply it regularly, especially during...
Insect repellent is essential to prevent insect bites and related health issues during mountain climbing.

1. Choose a repellent that is effective against the types of insects encountered in the area.
2. Apply it regularly, especially during times when insects are most active.
insect repellent, mountaineering, bug protection

Parasite Control (1) -

Control of Body Parasites in Cold Weather Conditions
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Procedure:

1. Inspect clothing and body each night for parasites when living in crowded or previously occupied shelters.

2. If clothing is infested with lice:
- Hang garments in the cold to paralyze lice, then beat and brush them...
Procedure:

1. Inspect clothing and body each night for parasites when living in crowded or previously occupied shelters.

2. If clothing is infested with lice:
- Hang garments in the cold to paralyze lice, then beat and brush them off.
- This method does not remove lice eggs (nits), so repeat as needed.

3. Use an appropriate insecticide powder on both body and clothing to eliminate parasites.

4. Maintain good hygiene practices and ensure access to cleaning facilities when possible.
lice control, body parasites, cold weather sanitation, insecticide use

Food Water Safety (1) -

Protect Against Diseases from Contaminated Food and Water
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Steps:

1. Drink water only from approved sources.
2. Eat food only from approved sources.
Note: Street vendors are never approved as they are not inspected by U.S. military preventive medicine or veterinary personnel. Consuming food from...
Steps:

1. Drink water only from approved sources.
2. Eat food only from approved sources.
Note: Street vendors are never approved as they are not inspected by U.S. military preventive medicine or veterinary personnel. Consuming food from street vendors can cause severe illness in U.S. troops.
contaminated food, approved water sources, street vendors

Field Sanitation (1) -

Establish Organizational Field Sanitation
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Objective: Ensure Soldiers are aware of the major components of the medical threat to field forces and that personnel follow preventive medicine measures (PMMs) to prevent disease and nonbattle injury (DNBI).

Steps:
1. **Educate Soldiers on...
Objective: Ensure Soldiers are aware of the major components of the medical threat to field forces and that personnel follow preventive medicine measures (PMMs) to prevent disease and nonbattle injury (DNBI).

Steps:
1. Educate Soldiers on Medical Threats:
- Inform personnel about the following major components of the medical threat:
- Heat
- Cold
- Arthropods (insects)
- Foodborne and waterborne diseases
- Toxic industrial materials (TIM)
- Noise hazards
- Other medical threats:
- Skin disease—common in extremely dry or humid climates.
- Altitude sickness—locations above 8,000 feet.
- Poisonous plants and animals.
- Tobacco use.
- Poor medical threat intelligence.

2. Train Personnel on Preventive Medicine Measures (PMMs):
- Ensure personnel are trained in the following PMMs:
- Protect against cold injuries.
- Protect against heat injuries.
- Protect against arthropods and arthropod-borne diseases.
- Protect against poisonous plants and animals.
- Protect against diseases from contaminated food and water.

3. Monitor Training and Performance:
- Ensure field sanitation teams (FST) are trained and performing their duties effectively.

4. Enforce Army Tobacco Use Policies:
- Implement and enforce policies related to tobacco use within the unit.

5. Coordinate with Preventive Medicine Units:
- Coordinate, as necessary, with division-level and corps-level preventive medicine units.
field sanitation, preventive medicine measures, PMMs, disease prevention, nonbattle injury, medical threats, heat, cold, arthropods, foodborne diseases, waterborne diseases, toxic industrial materials, noise hazards, skin disease, altitude sickness, poisonous plants, tobacco use

Access Field Manual for Unit Field Sanitation Team

2. Verify the publication date is 25 January 2002.
3. Use this manual to understand...
Title: Access Field Manual for Unit Field Sanitation Team

3. Use this manual to understand procedures and techniques for unit field sanitation team operations.

Health Protection (1) -

Enforce Individual Health Protection Measures
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Procedure: Enforce Individual Health Protection Measures

Steps:
1. Ensure that all personnel follow health and safety protocols.
2. Provide education on disease prevention, personal protection, and hygiene practices.
3. Monitor compliance...
Procedure: Enforce Individual Health Protection Measures

Steps:
1. Ensure that all personnel follow health and safety protocols.
2. Provide education on disease prevention, personal protection, and hygiene practices.
3. Monitor compliance with health measures to prevent illness and injury.
4. Address any violations of health protocols promptly and effectively.

Details:
• Individual health protection is critical for maintaining unit readiness and preventing the spread of disease.
• Leaders must ensure that all personnel understand and follow established health guidelines.
• Regular inspections and training are necessary to reinforce these measures.
